General  information  for  members  and  non-members  as

THE    CATALOGUE. 

DIVISION     I. 

Works  of  History,  Biography,  Travel,  &c.,  arranged  under 
authors'  names  and  under  subjects  of  general  interest — 
pages   1-83. 

DIVISION     II. 

Works  of  Fiction,  collected  under  authors'  names  and 
arranged  alphabetically  under  titles,  many  of  the  best  known 
novels  being  briefly  described — pages  84-4(S6. 

DIVISION    III. 

Juvenile  Books  under  titles — 487-505. 

List  of  branches  of  the  Library — pages  506-509. 


PREFACE. 


Since  the  issue  of  the  last  general  catalogue  in  October, 
1902,  this  business  has  undergone  a  great  development.  The 
membership  has  increased  to  an  enormous  extent,  a  result 
brought  about  in  great  measure  by  the  recommendation  of 
members  to  their  friends. 

For  this  emphatic  approval  of  our  endeavours  we  tender 
our  best  thanks  to  the  public,  and  intend  in  the  future,  as  in 
the  past,  still  to  merit  its  confidence. 

When  the  scheme,  formulated  by  the  management,  has 
been  realised  in  its  entirety  the  Library  will  be  the  largest 
and  most  popular  in  the  kingdom. 

The  frontispiece  shows  one  of  the  new  London  depots, 
and  is  typical  of  the  way  in  which  a  number  of  the  new 
branches  are  fitted.  All  true  book-lovers  find  great  pleasure 
in  handling  clean  books,  and  this  pleasure  is  intensified  if 
the  volumes  are  housed  in  quiet  and  comfortably  furnished 
rooms,  such  as  the  one  depicted. 

The  system  of  exchanging  books,  too,  that  is  in  use  at 
this  Library,  works  extremely  well,  saving  the  members  much 
annoyance,  and  the  assistants  a  great  deal  of  time  and 
unnecessary  labour. 


titrl^     6ooo/^=/overs    Lihncirij. 


It  will  be  noticed  that  this  edition  of  the  general 
catalogue  has  been  much  enlarged  and  revised,  a  fact  which  has 
necessitated  the  raising  of  the  price  to  1/-  nett.  In  perusing 
the  catalogue  readers  will  observe  tliat  fiction  predominates — 
a  result  brought  about  solely  by  the  superior  demand,  which 
we,  in  common  with  other  libraries,  experience.  Recognising 
this,  we  have  embodied  an  entirely  new  and  unique  feature 
in  this  section,  whereby  a  considerable  number  of  the  better 
known  titles  have  been  annotated  and  the  stories  described. 
For  many  of  these  characterisations  our  thanks  are  due  to 
Mr.  Swan  Sonnenschein,  for  permission  to  make  use  of  one 
of  his  publications  entitled,  "A  Guide  to  the  Best  Fiction," 
by  Ernest  A.  Baker,  M.A. 

Whilst  we  have  thus  given  prominence  to  fiction,  we 
are  very  desirous  that  subscribers  should  understand  that 
this  does  not  imply  that  we  deem  the  better  class  of  book 
unwelcome  or  unprofitable  to  us. 

We  trust  that  the  catalogue  will  prove  a  valuable  aid 
to  readers  and  students  for  the  purpose  of  study,  as  well  as 
recreation,  and  at  the  same  time  serve  to  demonstrate  the 
resources  of  the  library  in  the  different  branches  of  literature. 


JESSE    BOOT. 


Nottingham, 

October,   1903. 


THE  LATEST  PUBLICATIONS. 

PRICE   ONE    PENNY. 

This  booUlet,  which  is  issued  ;it  frequent  intervals  from 
the  ofSces  of  Boots  Book-lovers  Library,  contains  an 
alphabetical  list  of  the  very  newest  books. 

Many  of  the  titles  are  annotated,  and  it  is  hoped  tliat 
the  characterisations  of  the  principal  novels  will  be 
found  of  value  to  the   reader. 


The  "Book-loveks"  Reading  Cases 

ARE    ON    SALK    AT    ALL    BRANCHES    OF    THE    LIBRARY. 

ONE    PENNY    EACH. 

These    detachable    book    covers    are    made    in    five   sizes, 
and  in  several  shades  of  cloth. 

A  sample  case  will  be    posted  to  any  address  on  receipt 
of  two  penny  stamps. 


jAWJ^    SSookBlovens    l^ibrary. 


UdDantages  of  n^embersbip. 

Books  taken  out  at  one  branch  may  be  exchanged 
at  any  other  branch  in  the  kingdom,  and  exchanges 
will  be  made  as  often  as  desired. 

We  have  already  branches  in  the  most  important 
towns  in  England,  and  London,  Manchester,  Liverpool 
and  Birmingham,  will  be  covered  by  numerous  sub- 
branches.  A  list  of  the  branches  already  open  will 
be  found  on  pages  506'509. 

Any  book  that  is  in  print  and  suitable  for 
circulation  will  be  quickly  obtained  for  Members. 
Technical  and  medical  works,  expensive  illustrated 
volumes,  and  books  for  which  there  is  little  demand, 
will  not  be  added  to  the  Library. 

Much  worn  or  soiled  books  are  at  once  withdrawn 
from  circulation  and  replaced  by  new  copies. 

A  list  of  the  latest  publications  is  published  at 
frequent  intervals,  price  one  penny. 

The  extremely  simple  system  of  exchange,  the 
absence  of  irritating  rules,  and  the  perfect  service  given 
to  Members,  make  this  the  best  Circulating  Library 
in  the  kingdom.

The  Managing  Director  reserves  the  right  to  refuse  or 
cancel  any  membership  that  will  in  his  opinion  be  detrimental 
to  the  Library. 

For  the  convenience  of  those  who  occasionally 
require  to  use  the  Library,  the  following  terms  have 
been  devised : 

The  newest  books  that  are  available  for  non-members  may  be 
borrowed  at  Id.  per  day.  The  older  books  at  Id.  and  2d. 
per  week.  These  fees  should  be  paid  when  the  books  are 
issued. 

A  deposit  (2/6)  is  required  on  each  volume— this  is  returnable 
at  any  branch  of  the  Library. 

The  Library  supply,  gratis,  suitable  boxes  for  the 
exchange  of  books  to  subscribers  living  at  a  distance, 
and  who  change  their  books  by  rail  or  carrier. 

Books  may  also  be  exchanged  by  parcel  post ; 
in  such  cases  money  to  defray  the  postage  should  be 
left  with  the  Librarian. 

Special  terms  will  be  quoted  to  Clubs,  Book 
Societies,  and  Institutions,  if  particulars  of  require 
ments  are  sent  to  the  Head  Office.

INFORMATION  FOR  MEMBERS 
AND  NON^MEMBERS. 


TO  INTENDING 
MEMBERS. 


THE  TERMS. 


THE  MEMBERSHIP 
TOKEN. 


The  membership  entitles  you  to  exchange  books 
at  any  branch  of  the  Library.  For  addresses  of  the 
branches  see  pages  506'509. 

Subscriptions  may  commence  at  any  date,  and 
are  payable  in  advance,  A  receipt  is  given  on 
payment,  and  the  membership  token  is  posted  to  the 
member  the  day  following. 

The  terms  of  membership  and  charges  to  non- 
members  -will  be  found  on  page  xi. 

The  token  (a  small  ivorine  paper  knife)  can  be 
securely  attached  to  any  volume  belonging  to  the 
Library.  This  is  done  by  opening  the  book  and 
threading  the  little  silver-plated  tube  through  the 
wire  loop  that  appears  at  the  top  of  each  volume. 

To  w^ithdraw  the  token,  thread  the  little  tube 
back  again  after  having  opened  the  book  in  the  middle. 

The  membership  tokens  are  the  property  of  the 
Library,  and  must  be  returned  when  the  subscription 
is  renewed  or  discontinued. 

Each  membership  token  is  stamped  with  the  date 
on  -which  the  membership  expires.  On  payment  of 
the  renew^al  of  the  subscription  a  nev/  token  is  sent 
to  the  branch,  and  is  exchanged  for  the  old  one  when 
next  the  member  calls  at  the  Library. 


DISCONTINUANCE  When  discontinuing  the  membership  it  is  essential 

OF  SUBSCRIPTION,     that  a  token  be  handed  in  with  each  volume  returned 
to  the  Library. 


RENEWAL  OF 
SUBSCRIPTION. 


EXCHANGES  AND 
TIME  ALLOWED 
FOR  READING. 

METHOD  OF 
EXCHANGING  T'.iE 
BOOKS. 


Exchanges  may  be  made  as  often  as  desired,  and 
there  is  no  limit  to  the  time  allowed  for  reading. 

Books  may  be  exchanged  at  any  branch  on  pro- 
duction of  a  membership  token  for  each  volume. 
Unless  a  token  is  attached  books  will  only  be 
exchanged  on  payment  of  the  fee  charged  to  non- 
subscribers. 


J^fOtTi^     'Jjoo/i= lovers    Uihrany. 


INFORMATION   FOR   MEMBERS 
AND    NON^MEMBERS   icouunucd). 


WHEN  CHANGING  You  will  doubtless   appreciate    the    fact  that  you 

BOOKS.  arc  not  kept  waiting  while  an  entry  is  made  of   the 

book  you  return,  and  of  the  one  taken  in  its  place  ; 
also  that  you  are  not  questioned  as  to  your  name  and 
address.  All  that  you  have  to  do  is  to  show  the 
librarian  that  you  have  a  token  for  each  book  you 
wish  to  exchange,  and  you  are  then  at  liberty  to 
choose  other  volumes. 

BOOKS  OBTAINED  Any    book    that    is    in     print    and    suitable    for 

TO  ORDER.  circulation    will    be    quickly   obtained    for    members 

(see  page  s..).     Order  post  cards,  wth  postage  stamp 

affixed,  may   be   obtained   at   any   branch,  price   one 

half'penny. 

When  particulars  of  requirements  have  been  filled 
in,  the  card  must  be  handed  to  the  assistant,  who  will 
order  the  books  from  the  Head  Offices,  and  on  their 
receipt  will  post  the  card  to  the  member's  address. 

Order  post  cards  may  also  be  had  without  the 
stamp  affixed  (gratis),  but  in  this  case  the  member 
must  not  expect  to  receive  a  notification  that  the  work 
required  has  been  obtained. 

Members  may  have  order  post  cards  to  be  filled 
in  at  home,  and  can  order  books  to  be  sent  to  the 
branch  of  the  library  that  is  most  convenient  for  their 
next  exchange. 

No  book  will  be  reserved  for  more  than  three 
days,  and  after  the  expiration  of  that  time  will  be 
placed  on  the  shelves  for  general  use. 

Non-members  can  only  obtain  the  books  in 
demand  after  members  have  been  supplied.  In  no 
case  are   books  specially  purchased  for  non-members. 

LIST  KEPT  OF  Many  members    keep   a   list   of    the   works  they 

BOOKS  REQUIRED,      wish   to    read.      These    lists   the   assistants   will    be 

pleased  to   file    for   reference,  but   it   must  be  on  the 

understanding   that    members   do   not    expect  to  find 

books  constantly  placed  aside  in  their  names.     To  do

nary. 


INFORMATION    FOR    MEMBERS 
AND    NON^MEMBERS   (Contuuced). 


this  would  mean  that  if  six  volumes  were  subscribed 
for,  six  would  be  waiting  aside,  and  probably  another 
half-dozen  in  process  of  being  despatched  to  the 
assistants'  instructions.  This  converts  a  six  volume 
subscriber  into  one  for  eighteen  volumes. 

If,  however,  due  notice  is  given  that  certain 
books  will  be  called  for,  the  volumes  w^ill  be  obtained 
and  placed  aside. 

We  only  accept  subscriptions,  with  the  guarantee 
that  books  shall  alw^ays  be  awaiting  your  next 
exchange,  at  double  the  terms  advertised.  Of  course 
it  is  essential  that  a  sufficient  list  of  the  books  desired 
is  kept  with  the  librarian. 

LEAVING  BOOKS  Should  you    desire    to    return    your    books   to  the 

TO  BE  CALLED  FOR  Library  with  the  intention  of  applying  for  them  later 
on,  or  at  another  branch,  you  must  be  careful  to 
obtain  from  the  librarian  a  receipt  for  each  book. 
This  receipt  takes  the  form  of  a  small  metal  shield 
that  is  easily  attached  to  the  membership  token.  On 
producing  the  membership  token  and  shield  at  any 
branch  of  the  Library  you  wll  be  entitled  to  one 
volume.  A  separate  token  as  ■well  as  a  shield  w^ill  be 
required  for  each  additional  volume. 

SPECIAL  PRIVILEGES  The   Management    respectfully  request   that  you 

AND  CONCESSIONS,  will  not  ask  any  concessions  of  the  assistants.  It  is 
desired  that  everyone  shall  be  treated  with  uniform 
attention  and  courtesy,  and  when  assistants  are 
tempted  to  break  the  instructions  for  working  the 
Library  it  is  in  direct  disobedience  to  the  Management. 

SUGGESTIONS  AND  The    Management    is    always  glad     to    hear   of 

COMPLAINTS.  any  suggestions  for   the   improvement  of  the  Library 


Complaints  of  want  of  courtesy  on  the  part  of  the 
assistants,  or  difficulty  in  obtaining  the  books  desired, 
will  have  immediate  attention. 

Communications  regarding  the  Library  manage- 
ment  should  be  addressed  to  Mr.  Jesse  Boot.

Adcock,  A.  St.  John 
In  the  Image  of  God 

Two  series  of  stories  that  form  a  careful  presentment  of  the  life 
of  the  very  poor  in  London. 

In  the  Wake  of  the  War 

The    home    results    of    a   war  abroad,  the  sufferings  of  families 
deprived  of  their  supporters,  and  of  mourners  for  the  fallen. 
Luck  of  Private  Foster 

A  romance  of  the  great  Boer  War:  the  hero  enlists,  leaving  his 
girl  behind  him,  and  meets  with  a  considerable  amount  of 
adventure. 

Adderley,  Hon.  and  Rev.  James.     Paul  Mercer 

Paul,  the  son  of  a  millionaire,  goes  exploring  among  the  poor 
and  the  ameliorating  agencies  of  the  East  End,  is  converted  to 
Christian  socialism,  and  on  inheriting  his  money  founds  a  large 
manufacturing  community  on  these  principles.  A  book  full  of 
criticism  of  the  plutocratic  regime,  and  abounding  in  descriptive 
passages  of  the  numerous  humanitarian  bodies. 

Ade,  George.     More  Fables  in  Slang

Susannah  and  One  Elder 

The  central  idea  of  the  story  is  the  plotting  of  a  selfish  elder 
sister,  who,  to  cover  her  indiscretions,  cajoles  her  younger  sister

real  one,  she  endeavours  without  success  to  wreck.

Alexander,  Mrs. 

Admiral's  Ward,  The 

Plot  concerned  with  the  gain  and  unforseen  loss  of  an 
inheritance.     A  quiet  tale  of  every-day  life. 

At  Bay  Cost  of  Her  Pride,  The 

Barbara,  Lady's  Maid  and  Crooked  Path,  A 

Peeress  Executor,  The 

Brown,  V.C.  Fight  with  Fate,  A 

By  Woman's  Wit 

Freres,  The 

The  struggles  of  a  genteel  family  in  a  cheap  London  lodging- 
house  afford  opportunity  for  bringing  out  a  variety  of  good  or 
bad,  or  merely  shallow  and  selfish,  characters  in  the  different 
members  of  the  famil)-,  and  their  English  and  Irish  kin. 

Golden  Autumn,  A 

Her  Dearest  Foe 

The  heroine  receives  a  fortune  from  her  deceased  husband,  but  a 
new  will  is  found  bequeathing  all  to  an  offensive  kinsman.      She 
maintains  herself  giillantly  by  going  into  business,   all  the  while 
gathering  proofs  of  the   new  will's   invalidity.      Curious    events 
bring  her,  under  the  false  name  she  has  assumed,   into  contact 
with  the  successful  kinsman;   they  fall  in  love  and  their  marriage 
completes  the  denouement. 
Look  Before  You  Leap 

An  officer  elopes  witli  a  supposed  heiress,  and  finding  her  wealth 
non-e.\istent,  treats  her  harshly,  and  she  hides  herself  from  him 
for  a  year.       After  some  rather  romantic  incidents  the  pair  are 
satisfactorily  united. 
Maid,  Wife  or  Widow  ? 
Missing  Hero,  A 
Mona's  Choice 

.Vlona   lo\es   an   attractive   but   selfish    man,   but   is  lo\ed  by  and 
rejects  his  friend.      Years  and  changes  in  her  worldl)-  position 
test  the  characters  of  the  two;    and  in  the  sequel  she  rejects  the 
man  she  had  loved  and  gives  herself  to  the  loyal  lover. 
Mrs.  Crichton's  Creditor  Valerie's  Fate 

Second  Life,  A  Which  Shall  It  Be  ? 

Stepmorher,  The  Winning  Hazard,  A 

Stronger  than  Love  Wooing  O't,  The 

Through  Fire  to  Fortune  Yellow  Fiend,  A

Devil's  Die,  The

Ivan  Greet's  Masterpiece 

Ivan  Greet  seeks  among  the  negroes  of  Jamaica  the  leisure  denied 
him  by  a  London  life,  and  there  composes  his  masterpiece  and 
dies.  A  faithful  mulatto  girl  devotes  her  life  tu  his  baby  and  to 
the  task  of  getting  the  manuscript  printed.

Linnet 

Miss  Cayley's  Adventures 

A  comic  narrative,  with  plentj-  of  light  satire;  the  heroine  an 
audacious  Girton  girl,  who  starts  with  a  capital  of  twopence  and 
achieves  a  striking  career. 

Philistia 

A  socialist  novel :  all  the  important  characters  are  socialists 
either  actively  or  passively,  and  the  hero  endures  afflicting  trials 
for  his  convictions  before  he  obtains  a  competence  as  editor  of  a 
socialist  journal. 

Recalled  to  Life 

Scallywag,  The 

Strange  Stories 

Tents  of  Shem,  The 

A  varied  assortment  of  materials  ;  an  awkward  family  complica- 
tion, a  forged  will,  and  love  at  cross-purposes  with  two  pairs  of 
lovers:    the  latter  incidents  occurring  in  Algeria. 

This  Mortal  Coil 

Under  Sealed  Orders 

What's  Bred  in  the  Bone 

Woman  Who  Did,  The 

The  woman  refuses  to  marry  her  lover,  and  enters  with  him  into 
a  free  union  :  the  ultimate  result  is  tragic ;  the  woman  dies  a 
martyr  to  the  author's  gospel  of  free  love.

Anstey,  F.  (Thomas  Anstey  Guthrie) 

Bayard  fi-oni  Bcni>al,  A 

Brass  Bottle,  The 

A  burlesque  fantasy  with  an  unbottlcci  gcnic  for  licro,  who  is 
liberated  b)-  a  young  architect,  and  repays  his  benefactor  by  an 
embarrassing  series  of  miracles. 

Fallen  Idol,  A 

An  extravaganza,  aiming  at  plausibility  in  tlie  details;  a  trencliant, 
though  coN'ert,  satire  on  the  esoteric  Buddliism  rampant  at  the  time. 

Giant's  Robe,  The 

A  comedy  with  a  weak  young  man  for  hero,  who  pretends  to  be 
the  author  of  a  plaj'  sent  to  him  by  a  friend  in  India.  The 
unfolding  of  his  character  under  the  stress  of  such  a  situation, 
and  the  remorse  tliat  dri\es  him  to  confess,  mingle  more  serious 
elements  with  the  comic. 

Lyre  and  Lancet 

A  farce  written  in  a  series  of  scenes  and  dialogues. 

Man  from  Blankley's,  The 

Pariah,  The 

The  pariah  is  the  neglected  son  of  a  parvenu  father  who  wishes 
to  enter  good  societ)-  and  marries  a  widow  of  good  breeding  but 
no  monc}-.  The  unhappj'  boy,  ignorant  and  unpolished,  thus 
finds  himself  in  the  midst  of  a  family  who  hate  and  despise  him. 

Tinted  Venus,  The 

Vice  Versa 

A  ludicrous  story,  blending  the  modern  conte  with  a  motive  from 
the  "Arabian  Nights."    The  droll  situation  of  the  book  is  effected 
by  a  change  of  personality  between  a  schoolboy  and  his  staid  old 
father;  after  that  the  oi-dinary  and  probable  events  of  school  life 
become  extremelj-  funny.

Bagot,  Richard 
Casting  of  Nets 

The  author  is  a  Roman  Catholic,  well  known  as  a  controversialist, 
attacking  priestly  methods  of  securing  converts.  An  agnostic 
peer,  nominally  a  Protestant,  marries  a  Catholic,  whose  relatives 
try  to  convert  him  ;  a  series  of  painful  events  is  made  to  enforce 
the  author's  arguments.

Roman  Mystery,  A 

The  sensational  part  of  the  story  turns  on  a  case  of  "  lupomanoro,"

intrigue  at  Rome  are  the  other  materials.

improbable  and  amusing  situations.

Arden  Massiter 

A  young  English  socialist,  in  Italy  as  a  newspaper  con-cspondent, 
becomes  immersed  in  the  turbulent  strife  of  Camorra,  political 
parties  and  the  various  revolutionary  forces  that  arc  devastating 
and  transforming  the  country.  Full  of  romantic  and  tragic 
episodes. 

Daj'spring,  The 

New  Antigone,  Tiie 

A  doctrinaire  novel,  dealing  with  many  political  and  socialogical 
problems,  and  comprising  a  careful  and  exlTnustivc  investigation 
of  Nihilism  and  the  life  of  Nihilists.  The  New  Antigone  is  the 
daughter  of  a  well-born  Nihilist,  and  her  enthusiasm  for  the 
cause  brings  tragedy  on  her  own  head  and  on  her  lover's. 

Place  of  Dreams 

Two  Standards,  The 

The  wealthy  and  idle  classes  of  to-day,  represented  by  a  group  of 
generic  characters,  and  judged  from  a  high  moral  standpoint. 
Long  descriptive  passages,  dealing  with  scenery,  music,  &c.,  and 
moral  disquisitions,  occupy-  a  large  part  of  the  book. 

Wizard's  Knot,  The 

A  novel  of  Irish  life.  The  tragic  loves  of  a  gentleman  and  a 
peasant  girl  are  the  central  story,  while  the  Irish  peasantry,  with 
their  humours,  superstitions  and  poetry,  are  represented  with 
insight  and  power.

artiste  and  not  the  woman.

Benefactress,  The 

By  author  of  "Elizabeth  and  Her  German  Garden" 

The  benefactress  is  a  young  Englisiiwoman  who  has  a  fortune 
left  her  by  a  German  relative.  She  takes  up  her  property  in 
Germany,  and  her  life  in  the  German  village  is  the  basis  of  tlie 
story,  which  is  told  with  a  good  deal  of  humour. 

Benefits  Forgot  ...  ...  ...  Wolcott  Balestier 

Benham,  Charles.     The  Fourth  Napoleon 

Bennett,  Arnold 

Anna  of  the  Five  Towns 

A  story  of  Methodist  life  in  the  Potteries,  in  its  religious  and 
social  aspects.  Anna  is  a  miser's  daughter,  who,  courted  by  an 
eminently  shrewd  and  respectable  Methodist,  falls  in  love  witli  a 
young  man  whom  her  father  has  driven  to  ruin. 

Gates  of  Wrath,  The 

The  story  of  a  clever  plot  for  a  fortune,  which  was  frustrated 
by  a  girl. 

Grand  Babylon  Hotel 
Benson,  A.  C,     The  Hill  of  Trouble 

Twelve  short  stories  founded  on  folk-lore  and  legend,  and  dealing 
in  magic  and  mystery.

Benson,  E.  F, 

Babe,  B.A.,  The 

Book  of  Months,  The 

Capsina 

Dodo 

A  clever  representation  of  smart  society  of  the  present  day. 
The  heroine  is  alleged  to  be  a  thinly  disguised  sketch  of  a 
well-known  lady. 

Limitations 

Theme  thoroughly  modern — the  limitations  of  life  and  art  treated 
analytically.  Two  exponent  characters — a  woman  who  fails  to 
win  the  man  she  loves,  and  a  sculptor  who  aims  at  the  great 
Hellenic  ideals  but  has  to  live  by  producing  trivial  statuettes. 

Luck  of  the  Vails,  The 

An  ingenious  melodrama,  a  tissue  of  unlikely  events  plausibly 
narrated.  The  villain,  a  great-uncle  who  plots  to  murder  the 
young  heir  of  the  family,  is  interesting  as  a  study  of  the  criminal 
temperament. 

Mammon  &i  Co. 

A  return  to  the  smart,  self-indulgent  society  of  ■  Dodo.'  Aristocratic 
company  promoters,  baccarat  swindlers,  are  dealt  with,  half 
complacently  and  half  satirically. 

Princess  Sophia,  The 

A  fantasia,  mixing  comed}-  and  sensation,  somewhat  after  the 
manner  of  the  "  Prisoner  of  Zenda."  The  Princess  of  the 
imaginary  territory  of  Rhodope  is  a  born  gamester;  she  turns 
her  domain  into  another  Monte  Carlo,  and  obtains  as  much 
congenial  excitement  from  the  intrigues  of  her  petty  diplomats 
as  from  the  roulette  table. 

Rubicon,  The 

Scarlet  and  Hyssop 

Six  Common  Things 

Valkyries,  The 

This  story  is  "an  attempt  ....  to  render  as  closely  as 
possible  into  English  narrative  prose  the  libretto  of  Wagner's 
Valkyries.'''    The  romance  is  founded  on  the  northern  mythology. 

Vintage,  The 

A  novel  concerned  with  the  Greek  War  of  Independence  in  the 
early  decades  of  the  19th  century.  Quiet  idyllic  scenes  of  country 
life  and  manners,  and  scenes  of  violent  adventure.  The  Capsina 
is  a  winsome  Amazon  who  performs  wonders  of  valour,  but  at 
last  perishes  tragically.

Berthet^  E,     Catacombs  of  Paris 

Berwick,  John 

Philosopher's  Romance,  A         Uncle 
Secret  of  St.  Florel 

Besant,  Sir  Walter 

Alabaster  Box,  The 

A  socialogical  novel,  describing  the  work  of  a  settlement  and 
the  objects  of  the  movement.  The  hero  inherits  a  great  fortune 
from  his  father,  who  was  a  money  lender,  and  when  he  finds 
how  many  have  been  ruined  to  amass  his  wealtii  he  devotes  life 
and  fortune  to  repair  the  crime. 

All  in  a  Garden  Fair 

We  are  first  introduced  to  a  suburban  \  illage  iniiabited  chiefly  by 
a  circle  of  once  great  city  men  who  have  failed  for  an  aggregate 
of  millions.  This  is  the  key-note  of  the  satirical  passages.  The 
main  theme,  however,  is  tiie  history  of  a  literai-y  man's  growth, 
his  dreamy  youth,  sincere  stri\  ings  after  his  ideal  in  spite  of 
ad\crsit\-,  and  iiis  well-merited  success.  He  is  contrasted  with 
another  \oung  hero,  an  upright  and  energetic  man  of  affairs  who 
wins  tiic  heroine,  while  the  poet  receives  a  mate  better  fitted  to 
sympathise  with  his  dreams. 

All  Sorts  and  Conditions  of  Men 

"  An  impossible  story."  A  humanitarian  novel,  dealing  with  the 
huge  neglected  city  of  East  London,  depicting  its  miserable  life 
realisticallj-  and  suggesting  somewhat  Utopian  schemes  for 
transforming  it.  The  great  experiment  of  a  wealthy  Girton  girl, 
who  lives  among  the  poor  and  founds  a  Palace  of  Delight  in 
Whitechapel,  has  since  come  to  fruition. 

Armorel  of  Lyonesse 

"A  Romance  of  To-day,"  laid  partlyamidst  the  exquisite  scenery 
of  the  Scilly  Isles,  and  telling  how  a  faithful  and  courageous  girl 
helps  her  lover  to  escape  from  the  clutches  of  a  villain  who 
enriches  himself  by  purchasing  cheaply  the  works  of  needy 
writers  and  painters. 

Bell  of  St.  Paul's,  The 

Beyond  the  Dreams  of  Avarice 

Purpose:  to  expose  the  evils  of  colossal  wealth.  An  immense 
fortune  has  grown  up  around  a  nucleus  created  by  fraud;  and, 
the  last  owner  dying  intestate,  a  flock  of  claimants  appear.  Tlicir 
several  stories  are  told;  but  the  main  interest  centres  in  the  real 
heir,  kept  in  entire  ignorance  of  his  wcaltiiy  prospects  by  a  fatlier 
who  believed  a  curse  to  belong  to  the  ill-gotten  riches.  He  is 
gradually  overcome  by  the  hereditary  passion,  but  ultimately  is 
saved  for  a  higher  destiny. 

Captain's  Room,  The 

Changeling,  The 

Children  of  Gibeon,  The 

Poverty,  social  reform  and  the  influence  of  caste  are  the  principal 
themes.  A  baronet's  and  a  washerwoman's  daughters  are  brought 
up  together  in  ignorance  of  their  different  origin,  as  an  experiment 
to  show  the  effects  of  hereditary  character  and  breeding.  Hoxton 
is  the  scene  of  this  romance,  and  the  life  of  the  poor,  with  the 
various  agencies  for  amelioration,  are  realistically  described. 


114  J/TV^d^    cSoo/i=lovers    Libruny. 


Besant,  Sir  "Walter — continued. 
City  of  Refuge,  The 
Dorothy  Forster 

The  domestic  history  of  the  Forsters  of  Bamborough  Castle 
during  the  unquiet  years  of  Jacobite  intrigue  whicli  culminated 
in  the  fatal  rising  of  1715,  of  which  the  autobiographer's  brother 
was  the  general.  The  tragic  narrative  of  the  rebellion  leads  the 
reader  at  length  up  to  London,  into  Georgian  society,  Newgate 
and  the  Tower. 

For  Faith  and  Freedom 

A  story  of  the  Puritans  in  James  II. 's  reign,  dealing  with 
Monmouth's  rebellion,  the  expulsion  of  the  recusants  and  the  life 
of  the  exiles  in  the  plantations  of  Barbadoes. 

Fourth  Generation,  The 

A  tragi-comedy  with  two  principal  characters,  a  rich  old  squire, 
whose  life  has  been  overshadowed  by  a  mysterious  crime,  and  a 
)Oung  lover,  his  grandson,  who  has  just  been  rejected  bj'  the 
heroine  because  she  thinks  him  nothing  but  a  spoilt  child  of 
fortune.  At  this  juncture  a  series  of  family  sUeletons  comes  to 
light,  threatening  disaster;  but  the  hero's  bold  conduct  in  facing 
adversity,  and  his  success  in  laying  the  ghost  of  the  ancestral 
crime,  are  rewarded,  and  satisfactory  justice  is  executed  in  the 
last  chapter. 

Fountain  Sealed,  A 

Herr  Paulus 

Holy  Rose,  The 

In  Deacon's  Orders 

Title-story  is  a  study  in  religiosity,  which  is  explained  to  mean  a 
sensuous  delight  in  emotionalism  and  ceremonial,  not  necessarily 
allied  with  hypocrisy.  The  hero  has  this  religiosity  in  full  force, 
but  no  morals.     There  are  ten  other  tales. 

Ivory  Gate,  The 

A  psychological  romance  of  a  man  with  two  personalities,  the 
result  of  brain  disease.  Normally  a  staid  and  successful  solicitor 
with  sober  views  on  social  questions,  he  becomes  an  extreme 
socialist  with  wildly  Utopian  aims. 

Lady  of  Lynn,  The 

A  bustling  story  of  incident,  historical  in  time  and  place.  A 
beautiful  heroine,  her  bluff  sailor  lover,  and  a  wicked  peer  who 
conspires  to  get  hold  of  her  money,  are  the  principal  characters. 

Master  Craftsman,  The 

No  Other  Way 

Orange  Girl,  The 

A  picture  of  the  streets,  taverns,  mansions  and  theatres  of 
London  150  years  ago,  painted  by  an  antiquarian.  The  heroine 
is  a  magnanimous  creature,  poor  girl,  great  actress,  fine  lady  and 
convict,  who  saves  the  hero — on  trial  on  a  false  charge — out  of 
friendship,  and  suffers  for  him. 

Rebel  Queen,  The 

Deals  with  womens  rights  and  with  Hebrew  society  from  the 
outside;  the  heroine  a  rich  and  lovely  Jewess,  who  is  a  rebtl 
against  her  husband  and  a  champion  of  women.

Revolt  of  Man,  The 

An  extravagance,  represent  in  j^  a  future  when  women  have  secured 
the  supreme  rule  in  state,  army  and  navy,  and  in  private  life  ; 
hut  the  men  rebel,  overthrow  the  feminine  regime  and  the  religion 
of  the  ideal  woman,  and  —women  are  glad  of  it. 

St.  Katherine's  by  the  Tower 

To  Call  Her  Mine 

Uncle  Jack 

Verbena  Camellia  Stephanotis 

World  Went  Very  Well  Then,  The 

A  story  of  adventure,  love  and  war  in  the  j-ears  1740-60  ;  the 
scene  chiefly  at  Deptford  on  the  Thames  and  on  shipboard. 

Besant,  Sir  Walter  and  James  Rice 

By  Celia's  Arbour 

A  story  of  England  in  the  Crimea  in  1854  55.  \Miilc  the  hero  is 
winning  laurels  at  the  front,  his  sweetheart  at  home  is  enduring 
hardships  and  mourning  his  death. 

Case  of  Mr.  Lucraft,  The 
Chaplain  of  the  Fleet,  The 

A  story  of  the  famous  gaol  in  George  Ill's  reign,  full  of  antiquarian 
lore  about  the  streets,  houses,  theatres,  and  the  society  of  the 
times. 

Golden  Butterfly,  The 

An  American  oil-king  spends  his  millions  on  an  abortive 
.  liumanitarian  enterprise.  This  magnanimous,  yet  egotistical 
fellow,  invincibly  amiable  in  spite  of  discomfiture,  is  of  course 
the  centre  of  interest.  The  minor  characters  are  made  the  butt 
of  keen  satire,  of  dilettantism  and  the  like,  but  incidents  and 
characters  border  on  extravagance. 

Monks  of  Thelema,  The 

A  fanciful  tale  inspired  by  Rabelais  of  a  community  of  clever 
people  living  together  in  monastic  seclusion.  Pleasantly  satirical 
of  the  modern  literary  coterie,  with  its  exclusive  claims  to  the 
possession  of  the  higher  culture. 

Ready-Money  Mortiboy 

Old  Mortiboy  is  a  miser,  and  the  offspring  of  misers,  who  builds 
up  a  huge  fortune  by  grinding  the  poor  and  ruining  the  well-to-do. 
His  son,  an  unscrupulous,  yet  benevolent  young  man,  is  at  once 
Nemesis  to  the  old  Shylock  and  dispenser  of  poetic  justice  to  the 
various  characters,  major  and  minor,  of  the  story. 

This  Son  of  Vulcan 

Early  troubles  and  final  prosperity  of  a  poor  lad  who  is  the  foster 
child  of  a  drunken  pedlar,  and  suffers  calamities  of  many  sorts 
befoi-e  he  comes  to  his  own  little  inheritance.  The  life  of  iron- 
workers is  depicted,  with  a  medley  of  characters  disreputable 
and  the  reverse.  There  are  several  exciting  scenes  of  peril,  and 
serio-comic  love  passages.

'Twas  in  Trafalgar's  Bay  ;  and  other  Stories 

The  first  is  a  prettj-  little  love-story  of  Dorset  in    1805  ;     the  hero

pleasing  sketches  of  Canadian   life  of  to-day  ;    a  city  tale  ;    and 
Le  Chien  d"Or,  a  historical  tale  of  French  Quebec  in  1697.

Love  in  a  Mist 

A  sympathetic  study  of  a  fanatical  devotee  of  socialism,  who

domestic  drama.  The  ill-assorted  inhabitants  of  the  socialist's 
hou'jehold  furnish  iniicli  comedy.

Black,  Clementina 
Agitator,  An 

A  brief  study  of  political  movements  in   the  career  of  a  socialist. 
He    works  as   a  strike  leader,   as  agitator  in   London,   and  as  a 
labour  candidate.     Suffers  imprisonment  on  a  false  charge,  and 
is  eventually  saved  by  the  true  culprit's  confession. 
Pursuit  of  Camilla,  The 

A  very  eventful  love  romance  of  an  Anglo-Italian  girl,  who  is 
abducted  by  a  scoundrelly  Icner,  pursued  by  two  others,  an 
English  and  a  Polish  artist,  and  ultimately  rescued  and  engaged 
to  the  right  man.  Most  of  the  scenes  take  place  in  Northern 
Italy.

John  Oakley 


Alice  Lorraine 

A  romance  of  the  period  of  the  Napoleonic  Wars  ;  full  of  startling 
incident  and  adventure,  the  ancient  house  of  Lorraine  beinjj 
involved  in  disasters  that  have  to  be  drastically  remedied  in  the 
last  chapter.  "A  Tale  of  the  South  Downs;"  the  heroine  a 
modern  Antigone. 

Christowell 

A  beautiful  village  on  the  eastern  edge  of  Dartmoor  is  tiie  scene, 
while  the  sketches  cover  a  large  extent  of  that  lovely  region. 
Placid  village  life,  happy  and  affectionate  family  life  and  homely 
characters,  with  some  episodes  of  terror  as  a  striking  contrast, 
make  up  the  story. 

Clara  Vaughan 

A  melodramatic  tale,  in  which  a  petulant  and  obstinate  iieroine's 
adventures,    before    she    comes    into    her    rightful    iicritage,   are 
mixed    up    with    the    historj-    of    a    Corsican    vendetta, 
landscapes  of  Devon,  Gloucestershire  and  Corsica.

Blackmore,  Richard  "Doddnd^z— continued. 

Cradock  Nowell 

"  A  tale  of  the  Xew  Forest,"  containing  several  rustic  types  and 
eccentrics,  such  as  Parson  Rosedew  and  Dr.  Hutton  ;  the  story 
is  involved  and  abounds  in  sensation. 

Cripps  the  Carrier 

l.ui<e  Sharp,  the  lawyer,  plots  to  abduct  an  heiress,  and  actually 
proves  her  dead  and  buried  ;  but  his  nefarious  plans  are  overset, 
chiefly  by  the  agency  of  old  Cripps.  A  rural  place  in  O.xfordshire 
is  the  scene,  and  most  of  the  persons  introduced  are  homely  and 
rude. 

Dariel 
Erema 

Though  the  chief  characters  are  English  and  Scottish,  tlie  scene 
is  laid  in  California,  New  York  and  \\'ashington. 

Kit  and  Kitty 

The  hero  is  a  poor  market  gardener,  and  the  life  depicted  is  of 
the  lowliest,  but  both  hero  and  heroine  are  lifted  high  by  the 
sweetness  and  nobility  of  their  characters.  A  simple  talc,  with 
a  mystery  that  gives  it  a  dash  of  romance. 

Lorna  Doone 

A  romance  of  Exmoor  in  Stuart  times.  The  hero,  one  of  Black- 
more's  stalwart  yeomen,  simple  and  rude,  but  staunch  and 
chivalrous,  rescues  tiie  captive  Lorna  from  the  robber  Doones. 
Their  history  is  related  from  childhood  to  married  life,  with  a 
great  variety  of  episodes  and  pictures  of  life  in  the  great  world 
outside,  as  well  as  in  the  pristine  homesteads  of  E.\moor. 

Maid  of  Sker,  The 

The  romance  of  a  foundling,  the  missing  daughter  of  a  Devon 
family,  told  by  a  garrulous  old  fisherman,  who  overflows  with 
mother-wit.  Opens  with  sketches  of  life  in  Glamorganshire  a 
hundred  years  ago  ;  passes  then  to  Devon,  where,  in  a  wild  and 
lawless  state  of  society,  the  diabolical  Parson  Chowne  is 
represented  as  the  brutal  despot  of  his  parish.  Thrilling  episodes, 
such  as  a  hiu-ricane  and  a  wreck,  are  frequent. 

Alary  Anerley 

The  story  of  an  old  Yorkshire  family  ;  smuggling  ad\entiu-es  and 
pictures  of  life  on  the  coast  about  Flamborough  compose  the  chief 
part  of  the  romance. 

Perlycross 

A  leisurely  romance  with  a  mystery  at  the  bottom  of  it,  to  which 
\arious  illusory  clues  are  suggested  ;  but  in  the  end  the  mystery 
turns  out  to  be  a  hoa.\.  There  are  scenes  of  rural  life  in  eastern 
Devon  just  before  the  1832  Reform  Bill,  portraits  of  village 
worthies  and  much  descriptive  work. 

Springhaven 

Nelson  and  Napoleon  and  the  contemplated  invasion  of  England 
are  prominent,  w  hile  a  set  of  homelier  interests  and  obscure  figures 
are,  artistically,  more  important.  Types  of  heroism,  fine 
specimens  of  human  kindness,  gossips  and  humorists  are  among 
the  characters,  not  the  least  attractive  being  Admiral  Darling.

Comic  stories  of  China  and  the  Chinese,  which  may  or  may  not 
be  accurate  renderings  of  the  national  character,  but  are  full  of

Brought  Together         ...  ...  ...  "Rita" 

Broughton,  Rhoda 
Alas! 
Beginner,  A 

An  unsopliisticatcd  young  lady  writes  anonymously  a  novel  that 
is  assailed  for  its  impropriety  and  causes  a  world  of  embarrassment 
in  her  relations  with  relatives,  friends  and  lovers.  A  comic 
episode,  with  light  ridicules  of  the  provincial  critic  and  other 
varieties  of  the  species. 

BeHnda 

Belinda,  externally  impassive  and  cold,  burning  with  concealed 
passion,  is  on  the  verge  of  stooping  to  folly,  but  is  saved  in  time. 
The  vulgarly  ironical  Sarah,  her  grandmother  and  her  dogs  are 
humorous  sketches.      Dresden  is  the  scene. 

Cometh  up  as  a  Flower 

The  life  and  the  love  story  of  an  innocent,  impulsive  girl,  told  by 
herself.       She  loves  passionately  and  unreservedly  a  young  Scot 
who  is  poor  ;    but  her  father's  distresses  make  it    advisable  to 
secure  a  rich  husband. 
Dear  Faustina 

Doctor  Cupid 

A  group  of  love  affairs,  prosperous  or  disastrous  in  their  fortunes, 
those  of  two  sisters  being  the  chief,  one  of  whom  dies. 

Foes  in  Law 

The  varied  life  of  a  large  familj-  of  young  people  and  the  strained 
relations  existing  between  various  members.  Two  sisters-in-law 
represent  the  hostile  forces,  a  pair  of  well  contrasted  characters, 
whose  mutual  influence  and  gradual  progress  to  a  better  under- 
standing, is  related  with  vivacity  and  sprightly  humour. 

Game  and  the  Candle,  The 

Asomewhatcj-nicalstoryof  a  woman's  passion  and  disillusionment. 

Good-bye,  Sweetheart ! 

The  story  of  a  headstrong,  coquettish,  and  very  often  foolish 
heroine,  her  casual  conquests,  and  her  own  disastrous  love  for  a 
man  who  shares  her  passion,  a  love  that  is  ruined  by  her  own 
folly  and  ends  in  her  tragic  death. 

Joan 

The  story  of  a  gracious  and  sweet  tempered  girl,  brought  up  in 
refinement  and  then  sent  to  live  with  a  family  of  good  naturcd 
but  vulgar  cousins,  and  of  her  love,  which  runs  far  from  smoothly, 
but  reaches  happiness  in  the  end. 

Lavinia 

Mrs.  Bligh 

Nancy 

The  earlier  part  depicts  the  merry,  undisciplined  life  of  a  neglected 
family  of  youngsters,  whose  father  is  selfish  and  indifferent. 
Among  them  Nancy  grows  up,  at  first  an  unkempt,  but  generous 
and  faithful  girl  ;  then  we  see  her  married,  a  fine  example  of 
womanhood,  with  a  worthy  husband,  with  whom,  however,  she 
is  not  perfectly  mated.  Her  innocent  imprudence  is  partly  the 
cause  of  some  episodes  of  scandal  and  jealousy.

Broughton,  Rhoda — continued. 
Not  Wisely,  but  too  Well 

How  a  girl  of  loving  disposition  threw  away  her  heart  upon  an 
unworthy  man — a  muscular  libertine  of  the  Guy  Livingstone  type, 
and  how,  when  life  had  become  intolerably  dreary,  she  almost 
succumbed  to  a  great  temptation. 

Red  as  a  Rose  is  She 

The  heroine,  a  mixture  of  truth  and  folly,  self  respect  and 
cowardice,  qualities  that  cause  the  bitter  and  the  sweet  vicissitudes 
of  her  love  story.  Her  deceptions  are  paid  for  bj-  suffering,  and 
in  the  end  she  is  restored  to  sincerity-  and  happiness. 

Scylla  or  Charybdis  ? 

Second  Thoughts 

Light  comedy  all  through,  with  a  number  of  differentiated 
character  sketches.  A  spice  of  caricature  in  the  poet  Challoner 
and  some  psychological  art  in  the  main  story — the  taming  of  a 
shrew. 

Brown,  Alice.     The  Mannerings 

A  story  of  New  England  life,  in  which  ordinary  incidents  are 
skilfully  woven  to  portray  striking  and  diverse  characters,  whose 
personalities  make  its  chief  interest.

Buchanan,  Robert 
Andromeda 

Canvey  Island,  at  the  moiitli  of  the  Thames,  is  tlie  scene  of  this 
romantic  play.  The  monster  is  a  rough  and  brutal,  but  not 
wholly  detestable,  sailor  who  returns  to  claim  his  wife,  whom 
he  had  married  when  she  was  almost  a  child,  and  who  has  fallen 
in  love  with  a  more  attractive  man. 

Come  live  with  me  and  be  my  Love 

Founded  on  the  play  "  Squire  Kate."  A  country  tale,  involving 
several  stage  situations  and  appropriate  characters,  e.g.,  a 
faithful  steward  and   a   sliepherd. 

Effie  Hetherington 

An  uncouth  and  savage,  but  truly  heroic  man,  infatuated  with 
a  capricious  and  worthless  girl.  The  story  sets  forth  tragically 
his  pure,  but  unreasoning  devotion. 

Father  Anthony 

Foxglove  Manor 

A  ritualist  clergyman,  whose  character  is  compounded  of 
sensuousness  and  self-deception,  deserts  a  girl  whom  he  has 
seduced,  and  intrigues  with  a  married  woman,  all,  as  he 
professes,  without  any  ill  motive.  On  being  exposed  he  enters 
the  Church  of  Rome.  There  is  a  melodramatic  scene,  in  which 
a  scientist  throws  his  wife  into  a  trance  and  pretends  she  is  dead. 

God  and  the  Man 

A  study  of  the  vanitj-  and  folly  of  individual  hate.  A  man  bitterly 
wronged  by  another,  pursues  the  villain  relentlessly  until  both 
are  face  to  face  with  death  at  the  extremity  of  the  habitable 
world.  Then,  as  it  were  in  the  presence  of  God,  he  pardons  and 
rescues  his  foe. 

Heir  of  Linne,  The 

A  little  melodrama  of  love,  seduction,  and  Nemesis,  played  out  by 
the  Laird  of  Linne  (an  estate  on  the  South  West  coast  of 
Scotland)  ;  his  rightful  heir,  son  of  a  woman  he  betrayed  ;  and 
the  supposed  heir,  an  arrogant  young  scamp,  on  whom  the  tables 
are  turned  in  the  last  act.  An  unfrocked  priest,  a  strange 
mixture  of  drunUard  and  seer,  is  a  prominent  actor. 

Lady  Kilpatrick 
Love  me  for  Ever 

A  romantic  and  emotional  little  story,  embodying  a  version  of  the 

weird  old  legend  of  "  The  Flying  Dutchman." 

Martyrdom  of  Madeline 

As  an  innocent  girl  Madeline  is  ruthlessly  betrayed  by  a  French 
music-master,  and  trapped  into  a  marriage  which  the  villain 
denies  when  he  finds  she  has  no  money.  Her  varied  career  as  an 
actress,  etc.,  her  marriage  with  a  worthy  man,  the  reappearance 
and  persecution  of  the  Frenchman,  and  his  well-deserved  death, 
are  the  subject  of  the  ensuing  romance. 

Master  of  the  Mine,  The 

The  plot  turns  on  the  mysterious  seduction  of  a  girl,  the  guilt  of 
which  is  eventually  brought  home  to  the  master  of  the  mine  by 
the  hero,  who  by  good  fortime  and  resolute  conduct  wins  from 
the  culprit  his  millionaire  sweetheart  and  supplants  himasowner 
of  the  mine.     Cornish  scenes  and  characters.

Moment  After,  The  :  a  Tale  of  the  Unseen 

An   illused   Italian  murders  his  wife  and  her   paramour,  and  is 
hanged,  but  falls  to  the  ground  and  is  reprieved.      These  arc  his 
records  of  what  he  felt  while  in  a  semi-moribund  state.

My  Lady  of  the  Bass 

A  stirring  fiction  founded  on  the  defence  and  ultimate  surrender

usual  love  element  to  lend  additional  piquancy  to  the  narrative.

In  Connection  with  the  de  Willoughby  Claim 

Country  life  in  Tennessee  about  the  time  of  the  Civil  War   (1861). 

Lady  of  Quality,  A 

Supposed  to  be  written  in  the  17th  century.  The  heroine  is  a 
character  of  extraordinary  energy  and  egoism.  As  a  child  she  is 
a  little  dare-devil,  as  a  girl  a  hoyden,  indulging  in  clandestine 
love-passages,  when  marriageable  she  weds  an  aged  earl,  and  on 
his  death  becomes  a  duchess.  In  a  fit  of  fear  and  passion,  she 
murders  an  old  lover,  laying  the  ghost  of  her  past  for  ever  ;  and 
in  spite  of  remorse  and  the  constant  risk  of  discovery,  she  lives 
out  her  life  pleasantly  and  in  outward  honour. 
Little  Lord  Fauntleroy 

A  fairy-tale  in  real  life.  The  seven-year-old  hero,  living  among 
vulgar  people  in  New  York,  suddenly  becomes  heir  to  a  sclHsli 
and  crusty  old  earl,  goes  to  England  and  fascinates  everybody, 
including  his  ogreish  grandsire.

The  life  of  a  little  playwright  and  romancer  of  the  nursery,

That  Lass  o'  Lowrie's 

A   story   of  Lancashire.     The   romantic  plot   tells   how   a    young

miner,  after  she  had  saved  his  life. 
Through  One  Administration 

A  poignant  drama  enacted  amidst  the  brilliant  social  life  and  the 
political  corruption  of  \\'asliington.  A  woman  married  to  a 
politician  of  the  worst  type,  lo\es  and  is  loved  again  by  an  old 
wooer.  Both  are  true  and  conscientious  people,  and  tlie  pathos 
of  tlie  situation  is  that  the  lover  watches  the  husband  using  his 
wife  for  base  political  ends.

Anne  Mauleverer 
Children  of  Circumstance 
He  For  God  Only 

The  story  tells  how  a  curate,  to  support  himself  in   his  detached 
absorption  in  his  work,  drew  vampire-like    upon    the  reserves  of 
his  wife's  love,  until,  by  the  loss  of  their  first-born,  she  also  was 
brought  to  the  brink  of  the  grave. 
Minx,  The  Poor  Max 

Miss  Milne  and  I  Quaker  Grandmother,  A 

Yellow  Aster,  A 

A  study  in  eccentricities  of  whom  the  principal  is  an  abnormal 
girl,  the  offspring  of  two  unparental  scientists.  The  problem  is 
to  show  how  this  "  sfexless  "  creature  becomes  "  sexful  ''  as  the 
result  of  maternity.

Caine,  Hall 

Bondman,  The 

A  kind  of  modern  Saga  of  Iceland  and  the  Isle  of  Man  in  tlieda\s 
of  the  Napoleonic  Wars  ;  a  tale  of  uncurbed,  elemental  natures, 
of  lawless  crimes,  and  illimitable  revenfje.  The  plot  is  of  a 
complicated  nature,  but  the  dominant  situation  results  from  a 
persecuted  son's  life-long  effort  to  avenge  his  mother,  the  object 
of  his  fatal  quest  being  his  own  brother. 

Captain  Davy's  Honeymoon 

Christian,  The 

An  attempt  to  survey  the  religious  life  of  contemporary  London. 
Two  passionate  and  sensuous  beings  are  the  exponents  of  the 
thought  ;  and  as  tlie  emotional  interest  of  the  story  of  their  loves 
and  estrangements  becomes  keener,  the  philosophic  purpose  is 
well-nigh  lost  siglit  of  in  the  fierce  play  of  motive  and  passion. 

Deemster,  The 

Scene. — The  Isle  of  Man,  1775.  A  homicide  is  tried  by  liis 
father,  the  Bishop,  according  to  ancient  usage,  and  is  sentenced 
to  live  solitarily  in  a  desolate  corner  of  .Man.  Here  in  miser\- 
and  affliction  he  works  out  his  own  redemption,  and  in  tliat 
pestilence  offers  his  life  as  atonement  to  the  people. 

Eternal  City,  The 

An  ambitious  propagandist  romance,  the  moral,  religious,  and 
political  tendencies  of  the  contemporary  world  being  typified  in 
the  life  of  Rome  and  Italy.  Personal  interest  centres  in  the 
loves  of  two  characteristic  figures,  the  Socialist  Rossi,  "  a  noble, 
natural  man,'"  endowed  with  irresistible  energy,  and  the  beautiful 
Roma,  who  atones  for  early  frailty  by  heroic  devotion. 

Manxman,  The 

A  good  young  man's  surrender  to  passion,  and  the  retribution 
falling  on  himself  and  others,  are  the  characteristic  themes.  By 
an  ancient  Manx  custom  the  hero  is  left  in  charge  of  his  friend's 
sweetheart,  and  the  pair  fall  in  love  and  betray  trusting  and 
simple-minded  Pete.  The  agony  of  remorse  urges  the  offender 
at  last  to  confess,  and  the  magnanimous  victim  actually  smoothes 
the  way  for  his  betrayer  to  marry  the  divorced  heroine. 

Scapegoat,  The 

Jewish  life  in  Morocco.  Israel  Ben  Oliel  cleanses  himself  from 
the  guilt  of  a  tyrannical  and  extortionate  life  by  abandoning  his 
dignities  amidst  the  jeers  of  the  popidace,  and  living  a  life  of  a 
John  the  Baptist  in  the  wilderness. 

Shadow  of  a  Crime,  The 

A  high-pitched  romance  of  Cumberland  in  Restoration  times.  A 
false  suspicion  of  murder  threatens  a  dalesman's  family  with 
ruin,  and  to  save  his  loved  ones  he  avows  the  crime,  though 
innocent.  He  is  saved  on  the  very  scaffold  by  the  confession  of 
the  real  murderer. 

Son  of  Hagar,  A 

A  romance  of  modern  Cumberland,  the  subject  being  fraternal 
villainy.  A  talented  and  cultured  man,  but  bitterly  passionate 
and  vindictive,  schemes  his  brother's  ruin,  separates  him  frinn 
')iis  wife  and  compasses  his  imprisonment.  Eventually  tlie 
\illain  dies  tragically. 

Caird,  Mrs.  Mona.     The  Daughter  of  Danaus

CairneSf  Captain.     The  Coming  Waterloo 

A  military  romance  of  the  year  1903.  when  it  is  supposed 
England,  Germany  and  Austria  will  be  arrayed  against  France 
and  Russia,  and  a  campaign  in  northern  France  will  be  decided 
by  the  defeat  of  the  French  in  a  great  night  attack.  Very 
realistic  and  up-to-date  in  the  details  of  tactics,  artillery  and 
military  apparatus  generally.

Terror. 
At  a  Winter's  Fire 

A  collection  of  stories  mostly  of  tlie  grim  sort  that  we  associate 
with  Poe.  An  '  Eddj'on  the  Floor'  is  a  peculiar  ghost  story  that 
has  its  mixture  of  horror  and  fascination.  '  Jack  and  Jill '  is  rather 
of  the  kind  invented  by  Munchausen. 

Castle  in  Spain,  A 

A  story  of  Intrigue  and  adventure  during  the  Peninsula  War, 
involving  a  mystery  in  connection  with  the  Royal  House  of 
France  :   it  is  replete  with  incident. 

From  Door  to  Door 

Short  stories  of  an  uncommon  type.  'A  Coward'  :  —  Describes  a 
man's  feelings  when  threatened  with  death  by  fire.  'A  True 
Princess'  : — Records  the  aristocratic  sufferings  of  a  patrician  lady 
who  has  caught  a  cold  from  a  servant  girl. 

Joan  Brotherhood 

The  tragic  history  of  a  foundling  girl,  who  marries  a  parson 
secretly,  goes  on  the  stage  and  is  led  by  her  ambition  to  be  an 
actress,  to  her  own  undoing.  Contains  much  painful  incident, 
and  deals  boldly  with  shady  phases  of  life 

Lake  of  Wine,  The 

An  animated  story  of  an  immensely  valuable  ruby,  and  of  the 
plots  and  counterplots  of  which  it  is  the  occasion.  Gallants  and 
ruffians  are  the  actors,  the  passions  revealed  are  mostly  evil. 

Love  Like  a  Gipsy 

Mill  of  Silence,  The 

Our  Lady  of  Darkness 

A  story  of  intrigue  and  adventure,  with  a  courtesan  and  priestess 
of  anarchy  as  dominant  figure,  and  some  historical  names  among 
the  subordinate  personages,  tlie  historical  events  of  the  French 
Revolution  being  used  as  the  setting.

Alice's  Adventures  in  Wonderland 

Hunting  of  the  Snark,  The 

Sylvie  and  Bruno 

Through  the  Looking  Glass,  and  what  Alice  found  there 

A  new  genre  of  fairy  tale  which  draws  on  modern  science  and  all 
kinds  of  modern  ideas  for  its  materials,  and  finds  its  most 
characteristic  expression  in  droll  irrelevance  and  the  fantastic 
distortion  of  familiar  things.  Though  written  for  children  and 
inspired  by  the  prattle  and  innocent  charm  of  children,  the  wit, 
the  fanciful  humour,  and  the  subtlety  of  many  of  its  comic  under- 
meanings  can  be  appreciated  fully  only  by  educated  adults.

Castle,  Agnes  and  Egerton 
Bath  Comedy,  The 

Gay  and  witty  social  farce,  laid  in  fashionable  Bath  under  the 
Beau  Nash  regime  (middle  18th  century)  ;  the  characters  a  jealous 
husband,  a  doting  wife,  and  a  dashing  widow  who  intervenes 
disastrously  to  reconcile  the  two,  and  so  starts  a  complicated 
train  of  misadventures,  which  in  a  day  or  two  end  farcically  and 
happily.

Castle,  Agnes  and  Y.%zvio\\— continued. 
Pride  of  Jennico,  The 

Passages  in  the  life  of  Capt.  Basil  Jennico,  scion  of  an  ancient 
English  family,  who  inherits  princely  estates  in  Moravia  about 
the  year  1770,  and  meets  with  strange  and  thrilling  adventures 
through  his  courtship  of  a  fascinating  princess,  only  child  of  a 
reigning  house. 

Secret  Orchard,  The 
Star  Dreamer,  The 

The  memories  clustering  round  the  herb  garden  at  Bindon, 
"  when  George  III.  lay  dying,"  and  when  there  came  to  the  old 
alchemist,  who  lived  there  with  his  lord,  his  daughter  EUinor. 

Castle,  Egerton 
Consequences 
"La  Bella" 
Light  of  Scarthey,  The 
Marshfield  the  Observer,  and  the  Death  Dance 

Five  stories,  mostly  related  by  an  eccentric  observer  of  the 
minds  and  emotions  of  men  and  women,  along  with  his  interesting 
comments.  They  are  chiefly  fantastic,  or  ultra-romantic,  stories 
of  unruly  passion,  remorse,  etc.  The  Death  Dance  is  a  spirited 
tale  of  the  war  in  Hungary  in  1849.  Endymion  in  barracks  is  a 
psychological  sUetch  of  a  dreamer  who  takes  to  the  soldier's  life. 

Young  April 

A  sprightly  story  of  a  young  man's  thirty   days  of  crowded  life, 
love  and  adventure.     The  hero  suddenly  learns  he   has  inherited 
the  vast  wealth  and  honours  of  a  dulie,  and   devotes   a   month  of 
freedom  to  the  pursuit  of   unfettered   happiness.      Incidents  and 
characters  express  to  the  fullest  the  joy  and  the  romance  of  youth.

Cholmondeley,  Mary'-'Ccwfittued. 

Diana  Tempest 

A  ruined  uncle  is  tempted  in  an  un\\ai-y  moment  into  a  bet  witli 
a  rogue  that  launclies  a  series  of  min-deroiis  plots  aj»ainst  liis  rich 
nephew.  His  lielpless  and  remorseful  suspense,  the  peril  of  his 
victim,  and  the  melodramatic  justice  that  falls  on  his  own  son, 
are  the  sensational  elements  of  the  story.  Diana  herself  is  a 
chaste  high-spirited  girl,  unspotted  by  the  world  and  superior  to 
the  petty  ambitions  of  fashionable  life,  rejecting  wealth  rather 
than  honour. 

Moth  and  Rust 

Red  Pottage 

A  tragic  novel  comprising  much  searching  analysis  of  motive  and 
conduct  in  arbitrary  and  exacting  circumstances,  and  serious 
criticism  of  modern  life.  The  initial  situation  results  from  a 
duel  by  casting  lots,  and  the  guilty  man's  failure  to  carrj-  out  the 
sentence  on  himself.  Smirched  by  an  unlawful  intrigue,  the 
hero's  character  is  changed  by  a  purer  love,  but  the  issue  is 
calamitous.  Several  dramatic  incidents  are  brought  about  by 
the  plot,  and  the  emotional  stress  is  intense.  The  characters  are 
good  and  bad  ;  an  intolerant  and  narrow-minded  parson  is 
caricatured  and  some  pseudo-literary  people  are  severely  handled. 
Many  features  of  modern  society  are  satirised,  but  censure  falls 
heaviest  on  religious  savagery  and  humbug. 
Sir  Charles  Danvers 

A  plot-novel,  utilising  a  character  from  an  earlier  novel.  The 
Danver's  Jewels,  as  hero.  Satire  directed  against  stupid  people, 
who  are  plentiful  in  the  book,  and  stupid  conventions,  often 
dropping  into  sheer  caricature.

Anglo-Americans 

The  story  of  the  family  of  an  American  millionaire  who  love 
social  ambition. 

As  the  Twig  is  Bent 

A  good  example  of  this  author's  fiction,  which  portrays  realistically 
some  of  the  least  admirable  phases  of  modern  societj'.  Here 
we  are  introduced  to  the  company  of  shady  financiers,  whose 
ways  are  exposed  with  intimate  knowledge  of  technical  detail. 
The  main  story  relates  how  a  yoiuig  man  of  natural!)-  good 
impulses,  grew  up,  through  the  influence  of  surroundings  and 
associates,  to  be  an  accomplished  swindler,  and  how,  after  all, 
he  was  reformed  through  love.

Blue  Lilies

Epicures 

From  Crown  to  Cross 

The  plot  is  laid  in  a  petty  German  Court.  The  King,  a  devotee

Clifford,  Mrs.  W.  K. 

Anyhow  Stories 

Aunt  Anne 

Aunt  Anne  is  a  most  pathetic  figure,  an  old  and  wrinkled  woman, 
sentimental  and  foolish  as  a  young  girl  ;  poor,  yet  extravagant 
and  lavish  with  her  gifts  ;  a  cin-ious  blending  of  diverse  human 
features. 

Flash  of  Summer,  A 
Last  Touches,  The 
Mrs.  Keith's  Crime 

A  loving  mother,  dying  of  a  disease  that  has  also  attacked  her 
child,  fearful  of  leaving  her  little  one  to  the  harsh  mercy  of  the 
world,  ends  its  life  by  giving  it  an  overdose. 
Woman  Alone,  A  ;  three  Stories 

The  first  and  most  important  story  is  the  curious  and  sombre 

history  of  an  ill-assorted  marriage  between  a  reserved  and  selfish 

English  gentleman  and  an  enthusiastic  and  ambitious  Hungarian.

A  trite  dilemma  of  courtship,  two  lovers  being  hurried  by  a 
compromising  incident  into  a  premature  engagement ;  worked 
out  with  dramatic  and  vivacious  dialogue. 

Intriguers,  The 

■'  The  Intriguers  "  is  an  amusing  story  of  a  girl  who  has  two 
lovers,  one  of  whom,  easy  and  confident,  receives  a  set-back  and 
meets  disaster  ;   the  other,  over-diffident,  secures  the  prize. 

Judgment  of  Helen,  The 

Social  comedy  in  one  act ;   a  matchmaker  outwitted  by  her  two 

proteges. 
Lady  Gwendoline

Mr.  Passingham 
Scruples 

A  drawing-room  comedy  of  manners,  with  a  group  of  hesitating 
characters  entangled  in  a  mesh  of  love  at  cross-purposes.  The 
story  of  the  heroine's  scruples  and  the  final  denouement  is  told 
by  means  of  dialogue  characterised  by  continual  sparkle  and 
epigram. 
Severance

of  the  little  drama,  which  is  free  from  scntimentalit}-.

Free  Lance  in  a  Far  Land,  A 

Marvellous  adventures  of  a  free  adventurer  in  India  at  the  end  of 
the  18th  century  ;   eventually  he  becomes  a  petty  king. 

Fury  in  White  N'elvet,  A 

Inimitable  Mrs.  Massingham,  The 

A  romance  of  tlie  18th  ccntur\-,  with  a  beautiful  country  girl  as 
heroine,  who  later  becomes  a  favourite  actress.  The  streets  and 
inhabitants  of  bygone  London,  and  life  on  a  convict  ship,  and  at 
Botany  Bay,  ai'c  vigorously  depicted. 

Palace  of  Spies,  The 

A    romance    of    the    English    Court    in    the    early  years    of   last 
century  (1810),  dealing  with  the  estrangement  betwixt  the  Prince 
of  Wales  (afterwards  George  IV.)  and  the  Princess. 
Scourge  of  the  Sea,  A 

A  story  of  adventure  in  the  South  Seas,  being  the  narrative  of  a 
search  made  b)-  a  young  solicitor's  clerk  for  a  kidnapper  and 
pirate  who  was  heir  to  a  baronetcy. 

Wilful  Way,  The 

The  "wilful  way  "  is  that  taken  by  a  gambler,  which  leads  him 
through  impersonation,  theft  of  a  forged  will,  a  hypnotically 
induced  marriage,  and  a  narrow  escape  from  prison,  to  repentance 
and  reform.

Almayer's  Folly 

"  The  story  of  an  Eastern  River;"  an  imaginative  story  of  a 
European  married  to  a  Malajan  wife,  who  reverts  to  her  ancestral 
savagery.  He  lives  a  miserable  life  among  the  fierce  Malays  and 
Arabs  of  Borneo,  and  the  tragedy  culminates  when  his  beloved 
daughter  forsakes  him  to  marry  a  savage.

Conrad,  Joseph — loji tinned. 

Lord  Jim 

A  psj'chological  study  of  a  strange  problem  of  character.  An 
officer  in  the  mercantile  marine  wrecks  his  career  at  the  outset 
by  a  momentary  act  of  cowardice,  or  rather  of  unpreparedness. 
For  years  he  is  pursued  by  failure,  and  it  is  in  a  life  among  the 
savages  of  Malaysia  that  he  finds  at  last  the  opportunity  for 
rehabilitating  his  character  and  regaining  his  self  esteem. 

Nigger  of  the  Narcissus 

A  realistic  story  of  the  sea  and  of  life  and  personal  character  in 
the  merchant  service,  by  an  author  who  has  been  a  seaman  and 
has  a  knack  for  revealing  alien  modes  of  life  and  strange  aspects 
of  nature. 

Outcast  of  the  Islands,  An 

A  sequel  to  Almaya's  Folly.  Subject :  the  misfortunes  of  a  man 
who  loses  a  good  position  through  dishonesty,  and  goes  to  live 
with  a  native  woman.  The  principal  scene  is  a  trading  station 
on  a  Malay  river,  and  tlie  talc  brings  out  the  depressing  influence 
of  the  dreadful  solitude  and  the  demoralization  wrought  in  weak 
natures  by  the  isolated  life. 

Tales  of  Unrest 

'  Karain '  and  '  Tlie  Lagoon,'  two  little  dramas  illustrative  of  the 
tierce  tropical  character  of  the  Malay.  The  grisly  tale,  'An 
Outpost  of  Progress,'  is  similar  in  subject.  But  'The  Return  '  is 
a  conjugal  tragedy  of  civilized  English  life,  a  psychological 
study  of  the  disastrous  collision  between  two  incompatible 
temperaments. 
Typhoon 

Contains  four  tales  entitled — 'Typhoon,'  'Amy  Foster,'  'Folk,' 
and  'To-Morrow.'  The  first  is  a  realistic  description  of  a  typhoon 
in  the  China  seas.

Eternal  Choice,  The 

A  somewhat  diffuse  novel  turning  on  the  marriage  of  an  orthodox 
young  man  with  an  agnostic.  The  religious  question  is  not  dealt 
with  very  seriously,  and  the  interest  is  rather  in  the  sketches  of 
individual  character,  particularly  those  of  two  delightful  children. 

Fool's  Year,  A

Master  Christian,  The 

The  dedication,  "To  those  Churches  who  quarrel  in  the  name  of 
Christ,"  speaks  for  the  book,  which  is  a  clamorous  invective 
against  the  Churches,  especially  the  Roman  and  High  Anglican 
arraigning  the  clergy,  though  the  finest  character  is  a  cardinal. 
The  finding  of  the  Christ  by  this  unworldly  priest  outside  the 
Cathedral  of  Rouen  is  a  touching  incident,  henceforth  "Manuel" 
attends  the  cardinal  regularly,  and  is  at  IcMigth  brought  into  the 
presence  of  the  Pope.  The  plea  is  that  tlie  Church  should  reform 
her  creed  on  scientific  lines  and  her  ethics  on  socialistic.  Full  of 
multifarious  incident  and  of  interspersed  homilies.

Corelli,  Marie — continued. 
Mighty  Atom,  The 

A  story  protesting  against  ovcr-cdiication  and  the  folly  of  bringing 
up  children  without  a  religious  belief.  The  vulgar,  rich,  and 
tyrannical  father,  whose  wife  has  left  him,  dismisses  the  first 
tutor  because  he  teaches  the  boy  about  God  and  immortality. 
The  next  tutor  believes  in  the  Atom  as  the  beginning  of  creation. 
But  the  boy's  natural  questioning  about  the  whj-  and  the  where- 
fore of  things  is  unsatisfied  by  such  philosophy.  He  hangs 
himself  in  order  that  he  may  see  for  himself  whether  a  little 
friend  has  really  gone  to  heaven. 

Murder  of  Delicia 
Romance  of  Two  Worlds,  A 

Tacks  on  to  an  ordinary  novel  of  everj'^-day  life  the  gospel  of 
electricity,  which  the  writer  professes  to  be  the  outchme  of  her 
psychical  experiences. 

Sorrows  of  Satan,  The 

A  transcendental  melodrama  of  modern  life.  The  Devil  is  hero, 
and  it  is  sought  to  give  a  pathetic  rendering  to  his  character. 

Soul  of  Lilith,  The 
Temporal  Power 
Thelma 

A  romance  of  Norway  and  of  English  society.  Thelma  is 
supposed  to  be  a  daughter  of  Norse  Sea-kings,  and  to  show  her 
regal  nature  by  ruling  as  a  queen  in  society.

very  detailed  manner.

Path  of  a  Star,  The  Voyage  of  Consolation 

Cottage  Folk  ...  ...  ...Mrs.  Comyns  Carr 

Cotterell,  Constance.     Love  is  Not  so  Light 

Couch,  A.  T.  QuiUer-  C'  Q  **) 

Adventures  of  Harry  Revel,  The 

The  story  of  a  foundling,  who  on  leaving  an  orplianagc  becomes 
a  chimney  sweep  and  meets  with  numerous  strange  adventures. 
The  scene  is  laid  at  Plymouth  and  surrounding  districts. 

Blue  Pavilions,  The 

A  romance  of  William  III.'s  reign.  Scenes  :  Harwich  and  Holland, 
chiefly  in  the  year  1691,  a  crowded  episode  in  a  young  man's  life. 
Two  dauntless  old  sea-captains,  eccentric  and  humorous  figures, 
who  quarrel  over  the  guardianship  of  the  hero,  son  of  the 
woman  they  had  loved  and  lost,  are  the  most  striking  and 
pathetic  of  the  dramatis  persona^.  The  villain  is  an  adventurer 
who  has  dealings  with  all  parties,  including  King  James  at  St. 
Germains,  and  King  William,  and  the  future  Duke  of  Marlborough 
appear  in  some  humorous  scenes.    A  sensational  sea-fight  occurs. 

Dead  Man's  Rociv 

A  sanguinary  and  outlandish  romance  of  60  years  ago,  told  bj-  a 
Cornishman — the  quest  for  the  great  ruby  of  Ceylon. 

Delectable  Duchy,  The 

"Stories,  Studies,  and  Sketches  "  of  Cornish  villages,  country 
folk  and  fishermen,  scraps  of  legend,  humour,  romance  and 
tragedy,  slightly  connected,  and  told  in  r.uch  a  mann.er  as,  l^o, 
seem  merely  reported  by  the  author. 

I  Saw  Three  Ships 

A  picturesque  tale  of  the  old  times  of  wreckers  and  preventives 
on  the  Cornish  Coast.  "  Winter's  Tales  '  are  essays  in  sensation 
and  a  ghost  story. 


la 


Love  tale  of  a  strong-natured  and  pretty  Cornish  girl  who  courts 
a  young  preacher,  with  sketches  of  manners  and  characters 
simong  "  the  elect  "  religious  fisher  f()ll<. 


162  J/TV^i^    t/jooA= /overs    I^ihnciry. 


Couch,  A.  T.  Quiller-   {**Cl**)~cont\nned. 

Noughts  and  Crosses 

"Stories,  Studies  and  Sketches"  of  ver\'  diverse  scenes  and 
characters,  but  nearly  all  far-fetched,  bizarre,  and  even  j^ruesomc. 
yet  strangely  powerful.  "Gabriel  Foot"  is  the  self-related 
experience  of  a  fantastic  and  uncanny  criminal.  "The  Omnibus 
and  Fortunio"  are  merely  vignettes,  incisive  little  etchings  of 
character.  "  The  Return  of  Joanna,"  "  From  a  Cottage  in  Troy," 
"The  Mayor  of  Gantick,"  &c.,  are  specimens  of  Cornish  life, 
humorous,  grotesque  and  oddly  pathetic.  "  Bleakirk-on-Sands," 
a  Yorkshire  story  of  a  madman,  is  capable  of  giving  the  reader 
strange  thrills,  and  "The  Countess  of  Bellarmine"  is  a  good 
companion  piece.  "The  Magic  Shadow"  is  a  weird  little 
allegory. 

Old  Fires  and  Profitable  Ghosts 

Imaginative  and  artistic  essays  in  the  ghost-stories,  chiefly  of 
spirits  returning  to  the  scenes  of  their  human  experience.  The 
loc:ilities  and  the  persons  are  mostly  Cornish.  The  talc  of  "la" 
is  abridged  in  "Once  Aboard  the  Lugger. ""  Another  impressive 
tale  has  for  scene  the  Polar  ice-floes,  where  some  shipwrecked 
mariners,  at  the  point  of  death,  are  cheered  by  a  comforting 
presence. 

Ship  of  Stars,  The 

A  discursive  book,  strong  in  its  episodes  ;  the  biography  of  a 
Devon  lad,  a  dreamer,  who  becomes  a  man  of  action,  with  his 
love  story.     A  country  story  full  of  local  charm. 

Splendid  Spur,  The 

A  Royalist  romance  of  the  Great  Civil  \\'ar,  particularly  sti-ong 
on  tiie  history  of  the  campaign  in  Cornwall  and  the  West  of 
England  generally. 

Troy  Town 

A  serio-comic  narrative  of  certain  happenings  in  a  quaint  little 
seaport  of  Cornwall  (really  Polperro,  the  author's  native  place). 
The  racy  characters  are  half-serious,  half-farcical  creations,  and 
the  incidents  are  likewise  mingled,  a  countr\'  courtship  ending 
all. 

Wandering  Heath 

Two  further  collections  of  short  stories  and  sketches,  rac\-  of 
Cornish  nature  and  redolent  of  the  past.  The  "  Roll-Call  of 
the  Reef"  is  an  imaginative  ghost  fantasy,  founded  on  one  of 
those  terrible  legends  of  foundered  warships  that  haunt  the  fatal 
coasts  of  the  Lizard.  "  Humorous  Sketches  of  Village  Politics" 
and  "  Letters  from  Troy  "  are  among  the  other  contents. 

Westcotes,  The 
White  Wolf,  The 

A  volume  of  short  stories.  "The  White  Wolf"  is  the  stor^-  of 
«k  Danish  long-ship  found  in  the  Greenland  ice-pack  with  its  crew 
frozen  to  death.

Captain  Macklin

military  training  college),  recording  iiis  adventures  whilst  serving 
with  the  "  foreign  legion  "  in  Honduras,  Central  America,  during 
one  of  those  revolutions  which  are  almost  incessant  in  that  region.

officer  stationed  at  an  outpost  of  the  U.S.  arm)  ,  "  held  up"  a  stage 
for  abet;  how  a  second  was  "  held  up''  in  earnest  at  the  same 
time,  and  how  all  complications  were  eventually  satisfactorily 
unravelled.

Gallia 

A  societj-  novel  of  the  "fast"  type,  wiiicli  touclies  rather  freely 
on  the  Bohemian  life  of  artistic  Paris  and  on  sex-problems. 

Love  and  His  Mask 

The  thesis  which  the  novelist  seeks  to  prove,  and  does  prove  in 
this  particular  case,  is  that,  in  questions  of  marriage,  passion  is 
everj-thing  and  mere  respect  and  admii-ation  nothing.  With  a 
plot  based  on  this  argument,  the  book  is  bold  in  design  and 
contains  a  set  of  well  drawn  characters  from  high  socict)-.  The 
heroine  is  a  pleasant  Hgure,  nor  is  the  lover  wiiom  she  finally 
chooses  less  attractive.     The  South  African  war  is  introduced.

Doyle,  A.  Conan 

Adventures  of  Sherlock  Hohnes 

A  scries  of  detective  stories  that  form  the  life-history  of  the 
amateur  thief-taker  who  first  appeared  in  "A  Study  in  Scarlet.'' 
He  is  a  man  of  supci'hiiman  powers  of  observation,  inductive 
sagacity  and  combination,  whose  exploits  are  usually  accom- 
panied by  gruesome  and  thrilling  incidents. 

Captani  of  the  '  Polestar,"  The 

Doings  of  Raffles  Haw,  The 

Duet  with  an  Occasional  Chorus,  A 

Exploits  of  Brigadier  Gerard,  The 

Self-related  exploits  of  an  officer  of  the  Grande  Armee,  a  fire- 
eater,  a  braggart,  and  in  many  vays  a  type  of  French  virtues 
and  foibles.  The  eight  stories  are  roughly  connected  into  a 
memoir,  each  centreing  in  some  sensational  deed,  the  perils  and 
prowess  of  which  are  by  no  means  belittled  in  the  mouth  of  the 
genial  narrator,  whose  humour  is  quite  unconscious.  Napoleon, 
Wellington,  and  many  another  celebrity  pass  through  these 
scenes  of  action  and  adventure  all  over  Europe. 

Firm  of  Girdlestone,  The 

A  plot-novel  of  commercial  roguery.  Two  I^ondon  merchants, 
a  sanctimonious  scamp  and  his  ruffianly  son,  plot  to  murder  a 
ward,  whose  wealth  is  to  bolster  up  their  firm,  now  on  the  brink 
of  ruin.  The  true  lover,  after  a  series  of  complex  intrigues  and 
scenes  of  violence,  saves  the  girl,  and  the  \illains  in  different 
ways,  come  to  bad  ends. 

Great  Shadow,  The 

A  little  drama,  in  which  the  heroine  jilts  the  hero,  and  elopes 
with  an  officer  of  the  Imperial  Guard.  The  Napoleonic  terror  is 
the  "  shadow  "  of  the  piece  and  Waterloo  completes  the  climax. 

Green  Flag,  &c.,  The 

A  miscellaneous  collection  of  stories  of  action  and  of  humorous 
incident.  The  title-story,  like  some  others,  is  martial,  and  deals 
with  an  Egyptian  campaign.  "Captain  Sharkey"  is  a  bloodthirsty 
pirate.  The  "  Slapping  Sal  "  also  tells  about  a  valorous  pirate,  and 
some  of  the  others  are  reminiscent  of  Sherlock  Holmes,  the 
Brigadier  Gerard  and  others  of  the  author's  creations. 

Gully  of  Bluemansdyke,  The 

Hound  of  the  Baskervilles 

Memoirs  of  Sherlock  Holmes  (sequel  to  the  Adventures) 

Micah  Clarke 

Told  by  a  humble  adherent  to  the  Duke  of  Monmouth — the  whole 
stoi-y  of  the  rising  in  Somerset,  the  triumphant  advance  towards 
Bristol  and  Bath,  and  the  tragic  rout  at  Sedgemoor  (1685). 

Mystery  of  Cloomber 

Parasite,  The 

Refugees,  The 

A  Huguenot  romance  in  the  reign  of  Louis  Xl\'.,  the  French 
episodes  dealing  with  inti'igue  and  adventure  in  the  style  of 
Dumas  (there  is  a  favourable  portrait  of  Madame  de  Maintenon), 
and  the  American  half  being  after  the  manner  of  Fenimore 
Cooper.

Doyle,  A.  Conan — continued. 
Rodney  Stone 

A  novel  of  incident  and  action  in  tlic  years  1812-16,  picturinj^ 
English  life  in  those  times  and  tlealing  largely  with  the  prize-ring 
and  sport.      Beau  Briimmcl  and  other  bucks  appear. 

Round  the  Red  Lamp 

Sign  of  Four,  The 

Stark  Munro  Letters 

Study  in  Scarlet,  A 

A  sensational  story  in  two  parts  ;  the  first  of  which  deals  with 
adventures  in  Utah  and  the  wrong  committed  by  two  brutal 
Mormons  on  a  girl  and  her  lover  ;  the  second  is  the  history  of  a 
mysterious  double  murder  committed  in  London  and,  b)-  the 
agency  of  Sherlock  Holmes,  shown  to  be  the  work  of  the 
wronged  lover,  who  thus,  after  many  years,  attains  his  revenge. 

Tragedy  of  the  Korosko,  The 

The  disasters  and  hairbreadth  escapes  that  befel  a  party  of 
Europeans  in  a  trip  up  the  Nubian  Nile,  where  they  fell  into  tlie 
hands  of  Baggaras. 

Uncle  Bernac 

A  story  of  the  camp  at  Boulogne  and  the  projected  invasion  of 
England  (1804).  Napoleon  is  powerfully  sketched.  The  hero 
and  narrator  is  an  emigre  noble  who  serves  under  the  emperor  ; 
Bernac  is  the  villain,  a  treacherous  uncle  who  has  usurped  the 
family  estates.  Full  of  intrigue  and  sensational  doings. 
White  Company,  The 

An  episode  of  the  hundred  years'  war  with  France,  the  exploits 
of  a  company  of  English  bowmen  in  France  and  in  Castile  under 
the  Duke  of  Lancaster.  Du  Guesciin,  Chandos  and  other 
paladins  appear  on  the  scene.

Martian,  The 

The  tlicme  of  I^ctcr  Ibbctson,  I-'rance  in  tlic  forties,  provincial 
happy  domestic  life,  Belgian  scenes,  Malines  and  its  dignified 
clerical  societj-,  all  depicted  in  the  style  of  reminiscences.

Mars,  a  sort  of  Egeria,  to  the  hei^o,  giving  him  literary  inspiration.

scope  for  his  figures. 

Peter  Ibbetson 

A  melanchoh',  dreamy  book,  the  leading  motive  of  which  is  the 
supernatural  gift  that  enables  the  hero  to  meet  his  lost  love  in 
the  dreamland  of  the  past.  The  more  realistic  parts  are 
reminiscences  of  a  happy  childhood  spent  at  Passy.  Illustrated 
by  the  author. 

Trilby 

A  discursive  novel,  founded  mainly  on  reminiscences  of  Bohemian 
life  and  character  in  Paris,  with  which  is  entwined  a  story  of 
hypnotic  influence  over  a  beautiful  girl,  the  heroine  of  a  pathetic 
love  idyll.  The  pathos  is  profound,  but  the  book  is  mainly  a 
comedy,  mai'ked  hy  spontaneity  and  bonhomie.  It  was  di-amatised 
in  a  popular  play.
