only  by  her  innocence.  She  scandalises  the  "shady  English" 
of  Morteville,  a  continental  resort  of  the  shabby  genteel,  b}-  her 
tomboyish  manners  and  defiance  of  the  conventions  ;  and  at  last 
escapes  narrowly  the  disastrous  consequences  of  a  wild  adventure 
with  a  young  man,  undertaken  in  perfect  ignorance  of  the  ways 
of  the  world. 

Ball  Room  Repentance,  A 

Girton  Girl,  A 

The  love  troubles  of  a  girl  who  wanted  to  go  to  Girton,  the 
flirtations  and  mischief-making  of  a  married  lady,  a  cynical  and 
selfish  man's  attentions  to  an  innocent  girl — such  are  the 
principal  matters  ;  these  people  are  idlers  amid  the  picturesque 
scenery  of  the  Channel  Isles. 

Leah  :  a  Woman  of  Fashion 

An  English  boarding-liouse  in  the  Rue  Castiglione  affords  a 
comprehensive  character-picture  of  a  set  of  Bohemian  English 
people,  runaway  debtors,  separated  wives,  gamblers,  rakes  and 
persons,  generally  speaking,  of  damaged  reputation. 

Ought  We  to  Visit  Her? 

A  young  wife  of  Bohemian  antecedents,  shunned  by  the 
respectable  people  of  Chalkshire,  and  neglected  by  her  unworthy 
husband,  is  tempted  and  almost  seduced  into  wickedness,  but 
suddenly  awakes  to  conscience.  She  is  by  instinct  gay  and 
coquettish,  but  innocent  at  heart.  The  novel  flirts  with 
suggestive  situations,  without  overtly  transgressing  the 
proprieties. 

Plaster  Saint,  A 

Susan  Fielding 

Susan  is  one  of  the  inxmaculate  heroines  ;  compared  with  her, 
perhaps  as  a  foil,  is  the  more  human  and  more  intelligent  Portia 
Ffrench,  a  type  of  the  ambitious  woman  of  the  world,  far  from 
bad  at  heart,  but  determined  above  everything  on  a  wealthy 
marriage. 

Edwardes,  Amelia  B. 

Barbara's  History 

The  character  and  inner  life  of  a  girl  ;  the  romance  of  her 
courtship  ;  lier  experiences  in  a  German  college  (a  part  related 
with  intimate  and  humorous  sketcliing  of  national  and  local 
traits)  ;   and  her  romantic  marriage. 

Lord  Brackenbury 

Like  Barbara,  comprises  much  portraiture  of  foreign  people  and 
societ)-,  and  studied  descriptions  of  foreign  towns  and  scenery. 


ti,r(^    ,SooA'=/oi'ers    Lihrttnij.  189 


Edwards,  Matilda  Betham- 

Bridget 

The  development  of  Bridgets  character  from  impulsive  and 
thoughtless  girlhood  to  a  mature  and  affectionate  type  of  woman 
is  the  principal  motive  of  this  story,  which  comprises  a  variety 
of  character-studies,  with  their  several  love  tales. 

Brother  Gabriel 

Gabriel  is  a  young  Irish  monU  who  is  expelled  from  his  monastery 
in  Southern  France,  and,  being  forced  by  circumstances  to  live 
with  an  English  girl  as  her  brother  and  protector,  falls  deeply  in 
love.  His  character  is  a  study  of  religious  devotion  battling 
with  desire  of  freedom  and  of  passion  giving  way  to  saintly 
unselfishness. 

Curb  of  Honour,  The 

Dr.  Jacob 

Life  in  Frankfurt  and  certain  little  German  towns  ;  English 
and  German  characters,  the  chief  of  whom,  the  sexagenarian, 
Dr.  Jacob,  of  a  "noble  but  oblique  nature,"  appears  first  as  a 
man  of  brilliant  intellect,  fascinating  manners  and  lofty  aims, 
but  shows  his  inherent  lack  of  principle  in  his  love  passages  with 
some  young  women,  and  subsides  into  the  unhonourcd  old  age  of 
a  selfish  epicurean. 
Dream-Charlotte,  The 

A  village  story  of  Normandy  in  the  times  of  the  Revolution,  of 
which  but  the  faintest  echoes  are  audible,  while  Charlotte  Corday 
hardly  appears  in  person  in  these  memoirs  of  her  kin  and  of  the 
Huguenots,  whose  sufferings  were  ending  with  the  dawn  of  a 
new  order. 

Half-Way 

Humble  Lover,  A 

Lord  of  the  Harvest,  The 

A  quiet  story  of  rural  life  in  Suffolk  before  the  repeal  of  the  Corn 
Laws,  simple  in  its  personal  elements,  and  largely  taken  up  witli 
description  of  local  customs  and  modes  of  life. 

Love  and  Mirage 

North  Country  Comedy,  A 

Romance  of  Dijon,  A 

France  before  1789;  the  simple  love  story  quite  subservient  to  tiie 
careful  study  of  the  feelings  and  attitude  of  the  peasantry  toward 
the  ancient  regime  and  the  earlier  movements  of  the  Revolution. 

Storm -rent  Sky,  A 

Suffolk  Courtship,  A 

A  pleasant  picture  of  life  in  Suffolk  fifty  years  ago,  in  the  happy 
days  of  farming.    Rich  in  local  colour  and  faithful  in  rendering  the 
dialect.     Quiet  town  life  in  Ipswicii  and  farming  in  the  sliire  are 
well  depicted  in  the  course  of  a  narrative  of  several  lo\e  affairs. 
Two  Aunts  and  a  Nephew

Fantasias 

Half-a-dozcn  short  sUetches.  The  Mandrake  \'cniis  is  a  scatliinj» 
denunciation  of  irregular  relations  hetween  men  and  women. 

Keynotes 

Sketches  and  shglit  stories  of  the /t';///;/c  inco)iiprise,  &c.  ;  intro- 
spective studies  of  woman's  impulses,  which  are  acknowledged 
without  false  shame — the  dialogue  often  coarse  in  its  studied 
plainness. 

Rosa  Amorosa 

Very  literary  love  letters.  The  authoress  seems  to  have  embodied 
her  ideal  self  in  the  woman  who  writes,  and  in  these  emotional 
utterances  of  supposed  passion  we  have  an  idealisation  and 
apotiieosis  of  sentiment.  Descriptions  and  reveries  of  all  kinds 
diversify  the  diary  of  sentiments  and  are  expressed  in  sensuous 
and  fanciful  prose. 

Symphonies 

Similar  studies  of  the  feminine  temperament  and  of  erotics,  also 
employing  slang  for  realistic  effect.  Angling  reminiscences  gave 
tiie  outdoor  atmosphere  in  "  Keynotes";  here  the  feeling  for 
nature  is  appealctl  to  by  the  word-painting  of  continental  scenery, 
particularly  of  the  Basque  country.

Falkner,  J.  Meade 

Lost  Stradivari  us,  The 

A  ps}chical  romance  of  half-a-centiiry  ago,  in  whicli  an  old

Moonfleet 

A  tale  of  Hampshire  and  Dorset  in  the  middle  of  the  18th  century. 
The  search  for  a  lost  diamond  and  smuggling  are  the  chief 
episodes,  and  a  deep  and  secret  cavern  and  an  ancient  family 
vault  are  useful  stage  properties.

Farquharson,  A.  C.     St.  Nazarius

Farrar,  Dean 

Darkness  and  Dawn 

Paganism  face  to  face  with  Christianity  in  Nero's  reign — a 
historical  sermon  on  the  irresistible  superiority  of  character 
engendered  by  Christianity  in  social  and  political  life. 

Eric,  or  Little  by  Little 

Gathering  Clouds 

A  similar  didactic  romance,  g'ving  a  view  of  the  Byzantine 
Empire  at  the  time  of  the  pagan  reaction  against  Christianity 
(a.d.  387-410),  the  world  over-mastering  the  church.  It  is  also  a 
popular  history  of  St.  Chrysostom  and  of  his  stand  against  the 
growing  dissoluteness  and  corruption  ;  ends  with  the  capture 
of  Rome. 

Julian  Home 

A  specimen  of  several  stories  of  school  and  college  life  by  the 
author,   written   with    a   didactic    aim.     Julian    is  a  good   young 
man  who  meets  with  impediments  and  sorrows  in  his  college  and 
universitj-  career,  but  emerges  all  the  stronger  and  fitter  for  his 
future  life  as  a  clergjman.     The  characters  who  meet  with  the 
author's  reprobation  are  the  fast  j-oung  men  who  do  not  read, 
and  indulge  in  expensive  dissipation.

It  Came  to  Pass 

On  their  mother's  death,  two  brothers  succeed  to  their  father'.s 
property,  and  the  elder  promises  to  look  after  the  younger,  her 
favourite,  who  is  a  weak,  worthless  spendthrift.  He  carries  out

requited  at  last.

Arcadians,  The 

The  diverting  experiences  of  a  poet,  who,  growing  weary  of  city 
life,  settles  with  his  wife  in  "Arcadia."  In  the  end  he  finds 
country  life  disagree,  and  returns  without  regret  to  his  old 
haunts. 

Builders,  The 

A  story  of  the  growth  and  entry  into  life  of  a  young  man,  of  his 
matrimonial  mistakes  and  the  consequences.  Reflects  incidentally 
on  the  conventional  training  of  youth  in  pious  families.  Scene  : 
Southern  Yorkshire. 

Death  that  Lurks  Unseen,  The 

From  the  Broad  Acres 

More  chronicles  of  the  loves  and  marriages,  trials,  joys,  of  the 
Yorkshire  countryman,  racy  with  the  bucolic  spirit  ;  sadder  than 
most  of  the  preceding  tales. 

Golden  Spur,  The 

Harvesters,  The 

Investigators,  The 

Lucian,  the  Dreamer 

A  psychological  study  of  a  character  who,  being  of  an  artistic 
and  poetic  temperament,  regards  life  as  a  dream  rather  than  a 
reality.  The  scene  is  laid  in  rural  England,  but  the  last  event 
occurs  at  the  surrender  of  Paardeburg. 

Mistress  Spitfire 

Morrison's  Machine 

The  inventor  of  a  valuable  machine  loses  his  memory,  and  his 
employer  yields  to  the  temptation  to  steal  the  invention.  This  is 
the  basis  of  a  melodramatic  plot. 

Mr.  Spivey's  Clerk 

Old  Latimer's  Legacy 

Pasquinado 

Paths  of  the  Prudent,  The 

The  career  of  a  bewitching  and  too  clever  heroine,  who  plays 
with  her  lovers  and  outwits  her  master,  a  genial  and  humorous 
Yorkshire  landlord.

Hon.  Peter  SterHng,  The 

The  rise  and  progress  of  an  ideal  statesman,  who,  in  the 
midst  of  the  corruption  and  intrigues  of  American  politics, 
mamtains  the  virtues  of  disinterested  honour,  iiumano  sympatlny 
with  all  classes,  and  constancy  to  the  "  American  idea." 
Sterling's  college  life,  professional  struggles,  and  success  as  a 
lawyer;  the  disappointment  of  his  first  love  affair,  and  his 
maturer  affection  for  the  old  love's  daughter;  his  life  as  a  pooi- 
man  among  the  poor,  and  his  incorruptible  champi'onship  of  their 
cause,  are  the  notable  points  in  his  career. 

Janice  Meredith 

A  long,  sentimental  romance  of  the  War  of  Independence, 
revolving  round  a  wonderful  heroine,  who  captivates  friends 
and  foes.  The  battles  and  other  historical  incidents,  and  the 
celebrities,  e.g.,  Washington,  Major  Andre  and  the  English 
generals,  are  presented  with  accuracy.

Forrest,  A.     The  Extraordinary  Islanders 

A  romance  of  metaphysics  and  ethics.  Four  young  Englishmen, 
wrecked  between  the  Cape  and  Australia  on  an  island,  discover 
some  peculiar  natives  who  live  on  geometrical  principles  to  the 
exclusion  of  evil.  The  epilogue  shows  evil  to  be  owing  to  food, 
and  records  the  effects  of  various  extractive  fluids  termed 
"  liquefied  sin." 

Forrest,  R.  E. 

Bond  of  Blood,  The 

Founded  entirely  on  the  writer's  acquaintance  with  the  customs 
and  feelings  of  the  Rajputs  ;  embodies  a  motive  quite  foreign  to 
European  ideas  ;  a  man's  life  is  pledged  for  his  master's  debt, 
and  he  brings  down  a  curse  on  the  defaulter's  head  by  slaying 
his  own  mother  as  an  expatiatory  victim. 
Hight  Days 

The  eight  days  of  the  escape  from  Delhi  ;  historically  correct. 
Contains  a  pen-portrait  of  Gen.  Nicholson. 

Sword  of  Azrael,  The 

A  story  of  the  Indian  Mutiny  of  thrilling  interest,  being  the 
personal  narrative  of  an  officer's  adventures  and  escapes, 
culminating  in  a  terrible  massacre  of  the  British  defenders  of  a 
fort  on  an  islet  in  a  river.

Fothergill,  Jessie 
Aldyth 

A  girl  refuses,  from  a  sense  of  duty  to  her  younger  sisters,  to 
emigrate  with  her  lover,  and  ten  years  later  is  treacherously 
supplanted  by  one  of  these.  A  domestic  story  of  the  trials  that 
purify  character. 

Borderland 

First  Violin.  Tiie 

A  sympathetic  pictiu'e  of  German  musical  life,  the  hero  being 
leader  of  an  orchestra  in  Diisseldorf. 

From  Moor  Isles 

A  small  group  of  characters,  male  and  female  ;  Brian,  a  study  of 
the  artistic  temperament,  in  which  the  author  always  shows 
especial  interest,  the  impulsive  Inez  and  the  fine-natured 
daughter  of  the  people,  Alice  Ormerod,  with  her  devotion  and 
unreciprocated  love. 

Healey 

Kith  and  Kin 

The  hero  is  the  grandson  of  a  wealthy  squire,  disinherited  and 
earning  his  living  as  a  clerk.  His  graiidsire  learns  accidentally 
the  true  position  of  the  joung  man,  whom  he  had  supposed  to  be 
iri  comfortable  circumstances.  This  is  the  starting  point  of  the 
plot. 

March  in  the  Ranks,  A 

Oriole's  Daughter 

Probation 

A  story  of  the  Lancashire  cotton  famine  of  1863,  setting  forth  on 
the  one  side  the  distress  of  the  poor  operatives  and  on  the  other 
the  loves  of  a  wealthy  girl,  a  hot  champion  of  women's  rights, 
and  a  clever  man  who  engages  her  energies  and  enthusiasms  in 
really  useful  work.

Angel  and  the  Demon 

Concerning  Isabel  Carnaby 

The  manners  and  the  humours  of  a  Methodist  household,  con- 
trasted with  the  smart  frivoHties  of  London  society.  Isabel,  the 
society  girl,  loves  a  Methodist  tutor,  but  her  arrogance  leads  to 
a  rupture,  which  ultimately  is  made  up  when  he  fathers  a 
scandalous  novel  written  by  her.  Bright  dialogue  and  comic 
epigrams  are  the  distinguishing  features  of  the  booU. 

Cupid's  Garden 

Double  Thread,  A 

A  storj'  of  smart  worldlings  and  dull  provincials  ;  all  the  people 
without  exception  are  given  to  the  utterance  of  sayings.  The 
plot  is  founded  on  the  supposition  that  a  soldier  has  love  affairs 
with  two  girls,  one  poor,  the  other  rich,  who  in  the  denouement 
prove  to  be  the  same  person. 

Farringdons,  The 

The  humours  of  Methodist  society  again  brought  into  piquant 
juxtaposition  with  the  fashionable  world  ;  the  story  is  comparatively 
unimportant,  except  as  a  framework  for  the  sketches  and  carica- 
tures of  people  and  manners.  The  Methodist  heroine  is  the 
centre  of  interest,  but  such  figures  as  Mr.  and  Mrs.  Bateson  are 
more  characteristic.  Their  small  talk  on  religion  and  providence 
and  on  more  frivolous  subjects,  bubbles  over  with  smartness 
and  epigram.  Locality — the  Black  Country  (Silverhampton  is 
the  writer's  own  Wolverhampton,  which  her  father,  the  Right 
Hon.  Sir  Henry  H.  Fowler,  G.C.S.I.,  represents  in   Parliament).

Francis,  M.  E.  (Mrs.  Francis  Blundell) — continued. 

Duenna  of  a  Genius,  The 
Fiander's  Widow 

Fiander  was  an  elderly  farmer  whose  charming  young  widow 
woos  his  old  friend  when  she  finds  she  cannot  manage  the  farm. 
Out  of  this  situation  a  humorous  little  romance  is  developed, 
displaying  with  delicate  comic  touches  the  self-interest,  the 
conservatism,  and  the  cunning  of  tiie  Dorset  rustic. 

Frieze  and  Fustian 

In  a  North  Country  Village 

Sketches  and  actual  episodes  of  agricultural  life,  reported  in 
propria  pcrsond  by  one  of  tlic  quality,  who  has  a  keen  eye  for 
unvarnished  human  nature. 

Maime  o'  the  Corner 

A  touching  scene  of  self-renunciation  among  the  poor  and  lowly. 
Scene  :   rural  Lancashire  and  Liverpool. 

Miss  Erin 

Pastorals  of  Dorset  * 

Slight  stories  and  si<etches  of  Dorset  folk,  that  regard  chiefly  tiie 
pleasant  side  of  local  peculiarities  ;  full  of  humorous  appreciation 
of  character.  The  feelings  excited  among  the  people  at  home  by 
the  war  in  South  Africa  is  a  motive  touchingly  worked  out. 

Story  of  Dan,  The 

A  brief  tale,  told  with  directness  and  tragic  simplicit}-,  <jf  a 
magnanimous  peasant  who  adores  with  infatuation  a  worthless 
girl,  and  sacrifices  himself  blindly  and  uselessly.  Friendly 
portraits  of  Irish  country  people  are  among  the  minor  characters. 

Whither  ? 
Yeoman  Fleetwood 

Country  life  in  southern  Lancashire,  early  in  the  19th  century, 
furnishes  the  environment  and  the  minor  characters.  A  yeoman 
of  faultless  character  loves  a  girl  who  is  above  him  in  social  rank, 
and  who  has  captivated  George,  Prince  of  Wales,  at  Brighton. 
The  denouement  gives  glimpses  of  Mrs.  Fitzherbcrt,  Beau 
Brummel  and  the  dissolute  prince.

Custom  of  the  Country,  The 

Stories  and  pictures  of  new  Japan,  chiefly  of  an  emotional 
character,  e.g.,  the  title -story  and  the  pathetic  story  of  a 
foreigner's  liaison  with  a  Jap.  An  enthusiastic  love  for  things 
Japanese  pervades  the  book.

Frederic*  Harold 
Copperhead,  The 
Gloria  Mundi 

A  posthumous  novel ;    unfinished.     The  character  of  the  )oung 
Christian  Tower,   who  unexpectedly  finds  himself  the  heir  of  a 
duke,  is  the  completest  thing  in  the  book. 
Illumination 

A  subjective  study  of  character,  which  in  America  bears  the 
significant  title,  "The  Damnation  of  Theron  Ware."  Theron  is 
a  young  Methodist  minister  in  a  town  where  there  are  many 
Irish,  and  where  the  Roman  Catholics  are  very  active.  His 
training  has  made  him  earnest  and  energetic  in  the  cause,  and, 
as  he  believes,  intensely  religious  ;  but  it  has  left  him  narrow. 
His  "  Illumination  "  is  wrought  by  a  priest  who  shows  him  how 
religion  is  an  appeal  to  the  emotions,  by  a  learned  agnostic  who 
instructs  him  in  modern  views  as  to  the  authenticity  of  revelation, 
and,  more  powerfully  still,  by  a  captivating  and  unscrupulous  girl 
who  plaj-s  on  his  sensuous  instincts.  From  narrow  orthodo.xy 
the  transition  is  quick  to  doubt  and  unbelief. 

In  the  Valley 

Life  among   the   Dutch   of   the    Mohawk   Valley  ;    the    battle    of 
Oriskany,   &c.    (1757-80).     A  story  told   by  a    Dutchman   deeply 
prejudiced  against  the  British  cause,  the  narrative  burning  with 
racial  hatred. 
Lawton  Girl,  The 

A   small    manufacturing    town    is   described,    with    its    turmoil — 
political,  industrial  and  social. 
Market  Place,  The 

The  career  of  a  daring  and  unscrupulous  financier,  who,  by  a  deal, 
wins  a  colossal  fortune,  chiefly  at  the  expense  of  the  rogues.  The 
study  of  the  methods  and  morals  of  the  London  Stock  Exchange 
is  intimate  and  exhaustive. 

Return  of  the  O'Mahony,  The 
Seth's  Brother's  Wife 

A  minute  delineation  of  country  life  in  an  out-of-the-way  district 
in  New  York  State  ;  the  dull  routine  and  coarseness  of  farming 
are  set  forth  without  palliation,  and  equall)-  realistic  are  the 
accounts  of  local  journalism,  from  editor  down  to  compositor, 
and  of  the  machinery  of  elections.

Gallon,  Tom 

Charity  Ghost,  The:  a  Talc  of  Christmas 

The  story  opens  and  closes  by  the  appearance  of  a  jjhost.  Its 
chief  characters  arc  Faith  Arnold,  a  charity  girl,  and  Jabez  Crang, 
a  villain  who  committed  a  crime  for  which  Faith's  father  was 
imprisoned,  and  who  kept  her  lover  out  of  his  property  by  forging 
a  will.

Lady  of  the  Cameo,  The 

A  young  girl  and  her  brother,  cheated  out  of  tlieir  money  by 
their  guardian,  a  smirking  old  sinner,  retire  into  hiding,  the  boy 
having  been  driven  mad.  The  guardian's  accomplice  persecutes 
her  with  unwelcome  attentions,  but  is  murdered  ;  the  girl  is  tried, 
but  is  saved  by  her  brother's  evidence.  She  finally  marries  her 
friend,  once  her  boy  lover,  to  whom  she  had  given  the  cameo  ring 
years  before.

Gerard,  Dorothea  (Mdme.  Longard  de  Longgarde) — continued. 
Recha 

A  sad  picture  of  Jewish  life  and  character  in  a  miserable  Gahcian 
town,  where  Recha' s  father,  a  fanatical  and  sternly  orthodox

young  Austrians.

Gerard,  E.  and  D. 
Beggar  My  Neighbour 

Scene  :  Poland,  40  years  ago.  Primarily  a  novel  of  character, 
with  a  simple  and  natural  plot. 

Reata  ;  or  What's  in  a  Name  ? 

Reata  is  half  a  Mexican  and  half  a  German,  whose  wild  freak, 
conceived  in  fun  but  continued  in  earnest,  leads  her  into  serious 
embarrassments.     The  Mexican  scenes  are  studied  from  life. 

Sensitive  Plant,  A 

Waters  of  Hercules,  The 

The  action  takes  place  amid  the  wild  scenery  of  the  borders  of 
Hungary  and  Roumania,  and  the  local  characters,  manners  and 
superstitions  are  utilised.  The  plot  turns  on  the  search  for  a 
certain  mysterious  abyss  with  a  legendary  history  attached  to  it, 
and  involves  such  picturesque  scenes  as  an  encounter  with 
brigands  and  a  great  forest  fire. 

Gerard,  Morice  (Rev.  J.  Jessop  Teague) 

Black  Vintage,  A 

Grip  of  the  Wolf,  The 

Man  of  the  Moment,  A 

Man  with  the  White  Face,  The 

"The  Man  with  the  White  Face"  is  the  grandfather  of  the 
heroine,  and  has  been  confined  in  a  French  lunatic  asylum  by 
her  uncle,  who,  having  fraudulently  kept  her  out  of  her  estates, 
endeavours  to  secure  himself  by  forcing  her  to  marry  a  rascally 
peer.     His  purpose  is  defeated  by  the  grandfather's  intervention.

Gissing,  Algernon 

Keys  of  the  House,  The 
Knitters  in  the  Sun 

A  novel  of  country  life  in  which  a  squire,  holdinj;  somewhat 
unusual  views,  marries  one  village  girl,  his  deeper  affections  being 
all  the  time  centred  upon  another. 

Scholar  of  Bygate 

Secret  of  the  North  Sea,  A 

A  melodramatic  story  of  the  farmers  and  fisher  foll<  of  the 
Northumberland  coast,  whose  rude  speech  is  reproduced.  It 
turns  on  the  life-long  enmity  of  two  neighbours,  and  shows 
nothing  but  the  dark  side  of  these  people.  The  scenery  of  sea, 
sky  and  moorland  gives  the  storj-  breadth  and  atmosphere. 

Wealth  of  Mallerstang,  The 

Gissing,  George 
CroAvn  of  Life,  The 
Demos 

Eve's  Ransom 

A  story  dwelling  much  on  the  sordid  aspects  of  lower  middle-class 
life.  Eve's  artistic  and  social  aspirations  are  awakened  by  a 
visit  to  Paris. 

Life's  Morning,  A 

A  melancholy  story  ;  the  heroine  does  indeed  marry  the  lover 
whose  social  position  is  so  much  above  hers,  but  her  father's  fate 
is  representative  of  a  pathetic  group  of  Mr.  Gissing's  characters, 
worthy  and  capable  men,  whose  life  work  is  ruined  by  the  social 
conditions  into  which  they  are  born. 

Nether  World,  The 

The  obscure,  poverty  stricken  multitudesof  Clerkcnwell,  brutalised 
by  the  pressure  of  an  inhuman  struggle  for  bread,  half  of  them 
criminals  or  in  league  with  criminals,  a  nether  world  of  squalor 
and  misery,  realistically  depicted,  but  without  any  grossness. 

New  Grub  Street 

.\  pessimistic  study  of  the  modern  conditions  of  literary  life. 
The  writer,  who  is  primarily  a  business  man,  succeeds,  while 
those  of  superior  talent  and  finer  artistic  conscience  fall  into 
distress  and  ruin. 


218  Bit^^    ^ook'lovers    Clhrary. 


Gissing,  George — continued. 

Our  Friend  the  Charlatan 

A  stLidj-  of  character  and  of  circumstances  moulding  character. 
The  "Charlatan"  is  a  weak  and  egotistical,  but  not  ungiftcd. 
young  Oxford  man,  whose  fair  prospects  in  life  and  moral  nature 
are  bliglited  by  his  lack  of  conscience.  \\'e  see  him  in  relations 
of  courtsliip  with  three  young  women,  and  the  protegee  of  a  rich 
old  lady,  people  with  views  about  life,  whose  views  and  character 
are  examined  with  severe  analysis. 

Private  Papers  of  Henry  Ryecroft 

Thyrza 

The    story    of    a     London    factory-girl,    whose    imaginative    and 
spiritual    disposition    stands    out    against    the    squalor    of    her 
surroundings  :    a  story  chiefly  of  psychological  interest. 
Town  Traveller,  The 

A  group  of  London  people  of  a  common-place,  representative 
kind;  a  good  natured  "  commercial,"  a  cockney  girl,  a  lodging- 
house  keeper,  and  the  like,  in  Kennington. 

Unclassed,  The 

The  first  of  a  series  of  novels,  which  together  form  an  earnest 
endeavour  to  disclose  the  realities  of  modern  life,  particularly  in 
great  towns  like  London,  to  study  the  social  maladies  of  the 
time,  and  to  illustrate  by  artistic  means  the  working  of 
amelioratinginfluences,particularly  of  socialism.  The 'Unclassed' 
are  the  '  daughters  of  joy,'  and  the  author  would  show  that  even 
these  are  not  utterly  lost.  Two  girls  are  rescued,  or  rescue 
themselves,  and  live  an  honest  and  womanly  life  ;  one  of  them, 
a  distinctly  ideal  character,  devoting  herself  nobly  to  the  work  of 
helping  the  poor  and  fallen. 
Whirlpool,  The 

Theme  ;  the  irresistible  attraction  which  London  life,  with  its 
pleasures,  excitements  and  extravagances,  has  for  a  numerous 
class  of  people,  who  may  depart  for  a  season,  but  are  again 
drawn  into  the  fatal  vortex.  Tlie  cultured  life  has  no  time  for 
children,  and  sterility  is  the  common  result.  Is  there  not  better 
hope  even  in  Rudyard  Kipling's  ideal  of  man  as  the  active 
animal  and  fighter. '  This  and  like  questions  arise  in  the  course 
of  the  novel.

fortunate  of  their  people.  An  optimistic  book,  which  does  not 
shirk  realities.

Gould,  Rev.  S.  Baring- 

Arminell 

Bladys  of  the  Stewponey 

A  romance  of  high\va\men  and  rocU-dwellers  in  Shropshire  in 
the  latter  half  of  the  18th  century,  strong  in  local  features. 

Broom  Squire,  The 

Cheap  Jack  Zita 

A  story  of  the  Ely  fens  in  1815. 

Chris  of  All-Sorts 

"  Chris  "  is  an  orphan  girl  left  to  the  tender  mercies  of  two  selfish 
aunts.  Through  a  hunting  episode  her  lover  breaks  off  his 
engagement,  and  she,  going  to  London,  is  able  to  give  him 
information  which  restores  his  estate,  after  which  matters  are 
duly  mended. 

Court  Royal 

The  last  act  in  the  ruin  of  a  ducal  family,  brought  low  by  chronic 
extravagance.  Attention  is  concentrated  on  the  career  of  a  poor 
girl,  pawned  to  the  Jew  who  holds  the  chief  mortgages  and  uses 
them  as  instruments  of  revenge  for  a  personal  outrage.  By  her 
natural  abilities  she  inherits  the  Jew's  wealth,  marries  a  rich 
parvenu,  and  ends  as  mistress  of  the  duke's  late  mansion.  The 
eclipse  of  tlie  aristocratic  house  is  treated  as  a  serious  problem 
of  modern  social  tendencies,  while  Joannas  career  is  all  melo- 
drama.

Gould,  Rev.  S.  Baring continued. 

Dartmoor  Idj-lls 

Stories  of  the  primitive  moorland  folk,  and  of  the  still  life  of  the 
moor,  rich  in  description  of  the  wild  and  barren  hut  ever  varying 
scenerj-,  and  in  local  and  archa'oiogical  lore. 

Domitia 

An  antiquarian's  picture  of  court  life  in  Rome  during  the  Reign 
of  Terror  under  Domitia.  The  heroine  is  the  tj'rant's  unhappy 
wife. 

Eve 

Frobishers,  The 

A  study  of  the  hardships  and  oppressions  of  workers  in  the 
pottery  districts  of  North  Staffordshire,  the  details  evidentlj- 
"  got  up  "  for  the  purpose  of  instructing  public  opinion. 

Gaverocks,  The 

Has  a  sensational  plot  turning  on  wife-murder;  the  characters, 
strongly  accentuated  types  of  Cornish  villagers  in   bygone  times. 

Guavas,  The  Tinner 

A  melodrama  of  passion,  villainy  and  triumphant  virtue,  enacted 
in  the  wildest  part  of  Dartmoor,  and  embodying  much  antiquarian 
lore,  chiefly  about  the  Stannary  Laws,  the  manners  and  customs 
of  the  tin-miners  and  the  superstitions  that  prevailed  down  to  the 
Elizabethan  age  and  even  after. 

In  a  Quiet  Village 

In  the  Roar  of  the  Sea 

The  action  of  the  story  takes  place  on  the  Cornish  coast. 

ilacquetta,  etc. 

John  Herring 

A  powerful,  sombre  and  pessimistic  story  of  village  life  on  the 
borders  of  Devon  and  Cornwall.  Imaginative  description  of 
places  and  atmospheric  effects. 

Kitty  Alone 

Mehalah 

A  powerful  and  imaginative  story  of  peasant  folk  on  the  east 
coast,  in  which  character  and  sensation  are  mixed  in  fairly  equal 
parts.  Mehalah  is  a  country  girl  of  a  strong  and  passionate 
nature,  the  heroine  of  some  painful  episodes ;  there  is  racy 
humour  in  the  talk  and  doings  of  several  rustic  people. 

Miss  Quillet 

Mrs.  Curgenven  of  Curgenven 

Nebo  the  Nailer 

Noemi 

Mediaeval  France  at  the  time  of  the  long  struggle  with  England 
in  the  14th  century;  the  heroine  an  Amazonian  Jewess.  Local 
features,  such  as  the  wondrous  rock-fortress  on  the  Dordogne, 
play  a  considerable  part  in  the  tale. 

Pabo,  the  Priest 

A  story  of  Wales  in  the  time  of  Henry  \.  (1100-1135),  who  was 
trying  to  force  Roman  discipline  on  the  Independent  Welsh 
Church,  with  a  view  to  subjugating  the  people.  Strong  in  local 
colour.

Perpetua 

The  persecutions  of  Christians  at  Ximes,  a.d.  213  ;  a  learned 
archjEological  work. 

Queen  of  Love,  The 

Red  Spider 

Aims  at  picturing  realistically  and  preserving  the  features  of 
village  life  on  the  borders  of  Devon  and  Cornwall  as  it  was  fifty 
years  or  more  ago. 

Richard  Cable 

An  Essex  stoiy  of  a  peasant  who  marries  an  heiress.    The  pathos 

of  humble  life,  tempered  with  farcical  scenes. 
Royal  Georgie 
Urith 

A  tale  turning  partly  on  Monmouth's  rebellion  (1685),  full  of 
stormy  incident  and  acts  of  passion  and  malice  ;  the  scenery  and 
antiquities  of  the  moor,  and  the  manners  and  customs  that 
reigned  among  the  rugged  inhabitants,  are  delineated  with 
antiquarian  knowledge. 

Winefred 

The  heroine,  the  unacknowledged  child  of  a  gentleman  and  a 
smuggler's  daughter,  is  given  a  lady's  education  by  the  energy  of 
her  mother,  a  fierce,  persecuted  woman,  who  curses  her  recreant 
husband,  and  is  reconciled  to  him  only  on  his  death-bed. 
Devonshire  landscape  is  an  important  element  in  the  story. 

Gowing,  Mrs.  Aylmer 
As  CiEsar's  Wife 
By  Thames  and  Tiber 

Relates  how  an  English  lady,  lost  in  the  Catacombs,  has  a  dream 
of  old  Rome — of  the  sufferings  of  the  Christians,    and   of  Roman 
life  in  the  time  of  Nero.

Reminiscences  of  childhood,  related  with  much  humour  antl

Babs,  the  Impossible 

The  history  of  Babs  begins  with  her  precocious  girlhood,  and  is 
carried  on  to  the  age  of  love-making. 

Beth  Book,  The 

The  biography  of  a  girl  who  believes  she  has  genius,  and  who  is 
a  type  of  the  New  Woman.  This  also  is  full  of  moralising 
tirades  about  modern  society,  the  masculine  regime,  &c.,  every- 
thirig  of  the  nature  of  a  story  being  sacrificed  to  vague  preaching. 

Domestic  E.xperiment,  The 

Heavenly  Twins,  The 

A  medley  of  multifarious  incidents  intended  to  advocate  greater 
freedom  for  women.  Full  of  moralising  passages  referring  to 
the  marriage  question,  &c.  Some  of  the  characters  eccentricallj- 
humorous,  others  idealisations  of  the  writer's  views  on  life. 

Ideala 

A    portrait    of    a    woman    of    original    and     wayward  nature,    a 
character    "by    suffering    made    strong;"    one    of    the    earliest 
sketches  of  the  new  woman.

Grantley  Fenton 

Gras,  Felix. 

Reds  of  the  Midi 

Terror,  The 

White  Terror,  The 

Three  romances  of  the  French  Revolution,  translated  from  the 
Provin9al  of  Gras.  Opening  in  1792,  the  first  contains  a  dramatic 
account  of  the  famous  march  to  Paris  of  the  Marscillais,  of  the 
10th  of  August  tragedy,  etc.  The  hero  is  a  peasant  boy,  and  the 
ideas  and  mental  attitude  of  the  peasants  are  maintained 
throughout,  while  the  characters  of  these  rustic  revolutionaries 
are  portrayed  with  sympathetic  insight.  Then  comes  the 
narrative  of  the  Reign  of  Terror,  and  lastly,  the  story  of  the 
Royalist  movement  in  the  South,  with  a  picture  of  the  unhappy 
Avignon  torn  by  factions,  while  the  hero  and  comrade  tell  the 
tale  of  tiie  Retreat  from  Moscow,  of  Waterloo,  and  of  Valmy  and 
Jemappes.  The  long  chronicle  is  epic  in  style,  rambling, 
abounding  in  incident,  and  full  of  patriotic  fervour  and  sympathy 
with  the  Republic.

House  of  Hidden  Treasure,  The 

An  old  fashioned  plot  novel  with  a  villain  of  the  old  school,  who

ofifice.  The  lively  heroine  belongs  to  the  type  of  girl  who  is  at 
once  fascinating  and  exasperating.

Gray,  Maxwell  (Miss  M.  G.  "XxxWliziW— continued. 

Innocent  Impostor,  An 

Tlic  title  story  is  a  farce,  turning  on  an  involuntary  case  of 
impersonation;  'Tiie  Silver  CasUet '  is  a  love  story,  tragically 
interrupted,  and  renewed  in  middle  age;  'The  Mysterious  Guide,' 
a  sensational  Alpine  adventure. 

In  the  Heart  of  the  Storm 

A  novel  in  which  the  events  of  the  Indian  Mutiny  play  a  main 
part,  though  the  author  continually  finds  opportunity  for  talk  on 
Women's  Rights  and  similar  topics. 

Last  Sentence,  The 

A  barrister  having  married  a  girl  of  low  station,  learns  tliat  she 
has  perished  in  afire,  but  she  is  alive  and  accidentally  sees  him 
courting  anotlier  woman,  the  shocli  causing  her  death.  By  a 
strange  series  of  e\ents,  he  condemns  as  judge  his  own  daughter 
for  child  murder,  and  her  innocence  is  proved  only  just  in  time 
to  save  her  from  death. 

Reproach  of  Annesley,  Tlie 

Ribstone  Pippins 

Richard  Rosny 

"Richard  Rosny"  is  a  life  portrait,  drawn  by  a  skilful  hand. 
His  temptations,  sins,  repentance  and  victory  form  the  pivot  of  a 
story  of  exceptional  interest. 

Silence  of  Dean  Maitland,  The 

As  a  young  curate,  a  futiu'c  dignitary  of  the  church  seduces  a 
girl,  commits  manslaughter,  and  allows  his  dearest  friend  to 
suffer  penal  servitude  for  the  crime.  Afterwards  he  lives  a  good 
and  useful  life,  with  occasional  twinges  of  conscience,  till  the 
friend  is  released  from  prison  and  forgives  him,  whereupon  the 
Dean  makes  public  confession  and  dies.

Advanced  Guard,  The 

A  story  of  outpost  life  in  India,  dealing  with  the  pleasures, 
hardships,  and  adventures  of  our  empire  builders;  with  which  is 
interwoven  an  officer's  love  story. 

Crowned  Queen,  A 

His  Excellency's  English  Governess 

In  Furthest  Ind 

The  imaginary  autobiography  of  an  East  India  Company's 
servant,  who  is  persecuted  by  the  Inquisition,  and  visits  the 
court  of  the  Great  Mogul  ;   founded  on  historical  research. 

Kings  of  the  East 

A  sort  of  sequel  to  "  A  Crowned  Queen  ;  "  Mortimer  reappearing 
as  premier  of  Thracia.  The  schemes  of  a  Jewish  "  United  Nation 
Syndicate,"  for  repossessing  the  Holy  Land,  provide  the  cardinal 
motive  of  the  romance.  Mi.xed  up  with  the  affairs  of  princes 
and  diplomatists,  which  are  conceived  on  a  scale  befitting  the 
chancellories  of  Western  Europe,  are  a  variety  of  love  romances, 
happy  or  tragic.

Like  Another  Helen 

India  in  the  18th  century — the  series  of  historical  events  that 
were  connected  with  the  Black  Hole  incident  (1755-57).  The 
storj-  is  told  in  letters  between  two  girls,  one  of  whom  resembles 
strongly  Richardson's  Clarissa  ;  the  language  and  sentiment  of 
the  period  laboriously'  reproduced. 

Peace  with  Honour 

Prince  of  the  Captivity,  The 

Uncrowned  King,  An 

"A  Romance  of  High  Politics,"  and  "The  Romance  of  a  Minister 
of  State;"  imaginative  treatment  of  politics,  diplomacy  and 
palace-intrigue  in  a  fictitious  kingdom  of  Thracia,  supposed  to 
resemble  in  its  conditions  those  unsettled  Balkan  States  that  are 
under  the  thumb  of  Russia.  Love  plays  an  active  part  in  the 
drama.  A  diplomatist  of  vast  pretensions,  Count  Cyril  Mortimer, 
comes  to  the  front  as  the  tales  proceed.

interesting  side-incident  in  the  story  of  a  kidnapped  child.

Haggard,  H,  Rider — continued. 

Lysbeth 

A  story  of  the  Netherlandish  Revolt  against  Philip  II.,  beginning 
at  Leyden  in  1544 — when  Lutheranism  was  just  beginning  to  lay 
hold  of  the  Northern  Netherlands,  and  ending  with  the  siege  of 
that  city  in  1574,  and  with  a  picture  of  the  principal  characters 
living  peacefully  in  the  author's  loved  city  of  Norwich.  Written 
from  the  point  of  view  of  a  strong  Protestant  and  thorough 
Englishman. 

Maiwa's  Revenge 

Montezuma's  Daughter 

A  sensational  romance  of  the  Spanish  Inquisition  and  of  Cortez 
in  Mexico.

People  of  the  Mist,  The 

A  "record  of  barefaced  and  flagrant  adventure,"  the  author 
calls  it.  The  hero  goes  to  Africa,  rescues  a  beautiful  girl  from  a 
detestable  slave-trader,  destroying  the  rascal's  lair;  and  after 
various  thrilling  experiences  goes  in  quest  of  the  wondrous  gems 
in  the  city  of  the  people  of  the  mist. 
She 

Another  wonder  story,  with  realistic  and  gruesome  details 
worked  in.  "She"  is  a  mightj-  queen  and  enchantress  in  an 
imaginary  African  region  ;  she  has  lived  many  centuries  and  had 
amorous  passages  with  the  hero  when,  in  an  earlier  stage  of 
existence,  he  was  a  Greek.

Hardy,  Thomas 

Desperate  Remedies 

A  plot-novel  of  the  Wilkie  Collins  type,  with  glimpses  of  village 
life  and  rustic  gossips,  and  sketches  of  nnture. 

Far  from  the  Madding  Crowd 

The  tragic  comedy  of  country  life  ;  tiie  principal  characters  are 
farmers,  while  their  labourers  take  the  comic  parts.  Tiie  title 
is  ironical,  several  deaths  chequering  the  story,  though  Oak  and 
Bathsheba,  both  typical  characters,  are  wedded  at  the  end. 

Group  of  Noble  Dames 

Hand  of  Ethelberta 

"  A  Comedy  in  Chapters,"  dealing  with  both  Wcssex  and  society 
in  London.  Ethelberta  is  the  author's  Becky  Sharp,  but  differs 
characteristically  from  Thackeray's  adventuress.  A  girl  of  lowly 
birth,  she  flourishes  for  a  time  as  fine  lady  by  means  of  genuine 
talent,  a  successful  crusader  against  caste  prejudice,  but  ends  as 
wife  of  an  aristocratic  debauche.  Her  unconquerable  loyalty  to 
her  needy  family  of  brothers  and  sisters  is  her  redeeming  virtue. 

Jude  the  Obscure 

The  sceptical  views  of  life  and  religion,  and  the  leanings  towards 
French  naturalistic  methods,  which  have  become  more  pronounced 
in  the  later  works,  are  given  full  plaj-  in  this  story  of  a  peasant 
scholar's  foiled  ambition,  which,  from  beginning  to  end,  is  sombre 
and  in  many  of  the  incidents  extremely  painful. 

Laodicean,  A 

Another  Wessex  and  society  novel,  quite  a  minor  work.  The 
heroine  is  a  weaker  Ethelberta  and  the  motive  somewhat  similar, 
i.e.,  feudal  prestige  superseded  by  brains  and  mone3^ 

Life's  Little  Ironies 

Brief  stories  containing  the  quintessence  of  the  author's  fatalistic 
theory  of  life,  with  a  series  of  broadlj'  comic  stories  added  at  the 
end  as  a  sort  of  antidote. 

Mayor  of  Casterbridge,  The 

Virtually  the  life  history  of  one  man,  an  impetuous,  domineering 
personality,  whose  energy  wins  him  worldly  success,  but  whose 
bad  passions  eventually  work  his  ruin.  A  tragedy  of  an 
CEschylean  order,  in  which  misdeeds  evoke  their  own  Nemesis. 

Pair  of  Blue  Eyes,  A 

A  love  storj'  of  two  friends  and  a  Cornish  girl,  who  loves  both 
and  marries  neither,  ending  in  poignant  tragedy.  The  story 
turns  on  the  mutual  misunderstandings  of  the  friends,  and 
similar  sins  of  innocence  on  the  maiden's  part. 

Return  of  the  Native,  The 

A  drama  of  passion  and  Nemesis,  enacted  amidst  tiic  wild  and 
solemn  scenery  of  an  imaginary  heath,  and  animated  pi-ofotmdly 
bv  the  author's  philosophy  of  revolt. 


238  M&>    ^ook=lovers    Cibnary. 


Hardy,  'X^vomdiS —continued. 

Tess  of  the  D'Urbervilles,  The 

Tlie  tragic  history  of  a  betrayed  woman.  Tess  is  the  complete 
portrait  of  the  ideal  woman  sketched  in  the  previous  novels,  a 
daughter  of  the  soil  of  NN'essex,  and  at  the  same  time  an 
exponent  of  the  author's  fatalism.  The  tone  of  the  book  is 
polemical  ;  Tess,  the  author  contends,  is  sinned  against,  but  not 
a  sinner  ;  her  tragedy  is  the  work  of  tyrannical  circumstances 
and  the  evil  deeds  of  others.  The  theme  is  worked  out  with 
austere  simplicity,  a  number  of  personages  tj'pical  of  rustic 
society,  being  strictly  subordinated  to  Tess. 

Trumpet  Major,  The 

A  humorous  and  happy  love  story,  more  soberK-  realistic  than 
the  foregoing  in  its  picture  of  family  life  and  of  the  fickle  sailor 
lover. 

Two  on  a  Tower 

A  slight  story,  fanciful  and   unreal  in  general  character,  the  hero 
an  astronomer  absorbed  in  science. 
Under  the  Greenwood  Tree 

Practically  the  first  of  the  Wessex  novels,  the  common  basis  of 
which  is  intimate  delineation  of  the  people  of  Dorset  and  the 
neighbouring  regions,  and  of  the  natural  life  and  scenery.  The 
local  dialect  is  used  in  a  much  modified  form. 

Well- Beloved,  The 

A  fantastic  story  of  an  artist  in  pursuit  of  his  ideal  woman.  He 
sees  his  vision  embodied  successively  in  three  generations,  and 
last  of  all  woos  the  grand-daughter  of  his  first  love.  Portland  is 
the  scene,  with  a  change  to  London  occasionally,  but  there  is 
less  local  colour  than  usual. 

Wessex  Tales 

Long  short  stories,  comic  or  sad.  '  The  Three  Strangers,'  a  one- 
act  drama,  the  surprising  denouement  of  which  is  a  piece  of 
sardonic  humour.  'The  Withered  Arm,'  a  gruesome  tale  of  the 
supernatural.  '  Interlopers  at  the  Knap'  and  '  Fellow  Townsmen,' 
fatalistic  stories  of  mistaken  marriages,  characteristic  specimens 
of  those  tales  in  which  everything  goes  awry.  'The  Distracted 
Preacher,'  a  love  storj-  of  a  smuggling  parish  seventy  years  ago. 
All  the  stories  depend  more  or  less  on  the  working  out  of 
character,  and  contain  more  genre-painting  of  rustic  life.  The 
first  won  exceedingly  high  praise  from  Louis  Stevenson. 

Woodlanders,  The 

A  sylvan  pastoral,  imbued  with  a  pagan  delight  in  the  joj'S  and 
the  loveliness  of  nature,  the  human  figures  appearing,  as  it  were, 
part  and  parcel  of  the  landscape.

Hilda  Strafford 

In  Varying  Moods 

Ships  that  Pass  in  the  Night 

Adumbrations  of  life  at  an  Alpine  health  resort,  with  a  languid 
and  unconsummated  love  story.  The  leading  motive  is  the 
regeneration  of  the  heroine's  character  by  sights  of  human 
pathos,  and  the  book  teaches  insistently  how  selHsh  and  hollow 
are  the  ways  of  modern  society,  and  how  near  to  our  hands  are 
worth}'  duties. 

Things  will  Take  a  Turn 
Harris,  Frank 

Elder  Conklin  ;  and  other  Stories 

Realistic  sketches  and  episodes  of  rough  life  in  Kansas.  The 
elder,  a  strong  conscientious  Puritan,  deliberate!}-  commits  wrong 
for  the  sake  of  his  daughter,  who,  as  it  happens  ironically,  is  quite 
unworthy  of  his  devotion.  The  little  tale,  with  its  intertwined 
narrative  of  courtsliip,  is  a  searciiing  study  of  motive  and  manners. 

Montes  the  Matador ;  and  other  Stories 

Montes  is  a  bit  of  Spain,  full  of  hot  passion  and  abounding  life; 
a  story  of  love  and  hate.  The  descriptions  of  bull-fighting  are 
given  with  realism  and  the  enthusiasm  of  one  who  enters  into 
the  spirit  of  the  game, 

Harris,  Joel  Chandler

President  Lincoln,  and  divers  very  attractive  portraits  of 
southern  humorists,  daring  scouts,  and  an  inimitable  Anglo- 
Irishman.

In  the  Carquinez  Woods 

A  story  of  the  old  kind,  with  some  melodrama  in  it,  but  most 
powerful  in  its  rendering  of  the  wonder  and  mystery  of  the 
forest  (a  forest  conflagration  and  stampede  of  beasts  form  the 
culminating  scene).  The  heroine,  a  coarse,  rowdy  girl  from 
the  dancing-saloons,  becomes,  under  the  influence  of  love,  a 
refined  and  gentle  woman,  capable  of  suflering  patiently.  Other 
characters  arc  the  half-breed  lover,  of  refined  and  tender  nature, 
and  the  hypocritical  Baptist  minister.

Harte,  Bret — continued. 
In  the  Hollow  of  the  Hills 
Luck  of  Roaring  Camp,  The  ;  and  other  Sketches 

A  new  genre  of  short  story.  The  "Luck"  is  a  babe  whose 
coming  among  the  brutal  and  uncivilised  miners  in  the  Californian 
settlement  makes  their  lives  better  and  more  humane.  "The 
Outcasts  of  Poker  Flat,"  and  many  others,  also  sketch  the 
savagery  and  crime  of  the  gold-digging  communities  of  half-a- 
century  ago. 

Mr.  Jack  Hamlin's  Mediation     Tales  of  the  Pacific  Slope 
Sappho  of  Green  Springs,  A     Tales  of  Trail  and  Town 
Stories  in  Light  and  Shadow     Three  Partners 
Trent's  Trust ;   and  other  Stories 

A  volume  of  seven  short  entertaining  tales. 
Under  the  Redwoods 

Stories  well  representative  of  Bret  Harte's  various  styles,  and 
including  some  of  his  best.  "  Three  Vagabonds  of  Trinidad  "  is 
a  powerful,  touching  little  story  of  an  Indian,  a  China  boy,  and  a 
faithful  dog;  and  "Bohemian  Days"  brings  before  us  vividly 
the  half-veiled  struggle  between  respectability  and  lawlessness 
that  marked  the  early  days  of  San  Francisco.

ills  wife  and  children,  the  penalty  of  death.  The  story  of  a 
nation's  hero.

Hayden,  Ekanor  G.     From  a  Thatched  Cottage 

Hayest  Frederick  W. 
Gwynett  of  Thorn liau^h 
Kent  Squire,  A 

Both  "A  Kent  Squire"  and  '•Gwynett  of  Thornhaugh  "  (its 
sequel),  contain  much  history,  public  and  private,  some  of  it 
founded  on  documents,  relatinj^  to  France,  England,  and  Holland 
about  1712.  The  Court  of  Versailles,  the  intrigues  connected 
with  the  peace  of  Utrecht,  the  squire's  dealings  with  the 
Jacobites,  etc.,  are  the  principal  motives  of  a  wild  and  breathless 
series  of  adventures.  Louis  X\'I.,  the  Regent  and  Marlborough 
are  prominent  figures.

Herrick,  Robert 
Real  World,  The 

An  analytical  study  of  modern  American  society,  which  is 
presented  in  an  odious  light.  The  hero  tests  by  experience 
various  lunds  of  life,  and  is  repelled  by  the  heartlessness  and 
self  indulgence  that  he  sees.  The  aim  is  to  show  how  each 
experience  affects  his  character.  There  is  a  pleasant  glimpse  of 
Havard. 


tA^ook'lovers    uihruni/.  247 


Herrick,  Robert — continued. 
Web  of  Life,  The 

A  realistic,  and  to  a  great  extent,  a  philosophic  study  of  American 
life  ;  the  scene  is  Chicago,  and  the  writer  gives  scarcliing  views  of 
society  there.  The  hero  is  a  doctor,  and  the  organisation  of 
medical  practitioners  is  well  brought  out.  Ha\ing  saved  the  life 
of  a  drunkard  by  an  operation  that  injures  the  brain,  he  falls  in 
love  with  the  man's  wife,  and  the  situation  thus  produced  is  a 
specimen  of  the  problems  that  are  raised.  The  story  of  the 
woman's  futile  effort  to  realise  her  character  in  this  chaos  of 
repressing  forces,  and  her  suicide,  is  tragic,  but  it  is  not 
unwholesome. 

Harvey,  Marie.     Putt's  Notions 

Hester  Morley's  Promise  ...  ...       Hesba  Stretton 

Hetty  Martin's  Trial   ...  ...  ...  Mrs.  Lysaght 

Hewitt,  Edgar.     The  Pi  ettiness  of  Fools 

Hewlett,  Maurice 
Forest  Lovers,  The 
Little  Novels  of  Italy 

Five  stories  of  renaissance  times,  the  work  of  a  scholar  learned 
in  the  history  and  literature  of  the  period.  The  "  Duchess  of 
Nona  "  tells  how  an  Italian  adventurer  brought  home  his 
beautiful  and  stupid  English  bride,  and  tried  to  make  her  a  tool 
in  his  ambitious  schemes,  but  was  checkmated  by  her  simple 
fidelity.  "Madonna  of  the  Fig  Tree"  is  the  apotheosis  of  a  peasant- 
girl,  a  half-poetical  fantasy,  of  which  the  materials  are  taken 
from  ordinary  life  in  mediaeval  cities.  All  reflect  the  swift 
changes  from  comedy  to  tragedy,  the  dramatic  contrasts  of 
exquisite  culture  and  diabolical  crime  that  characterised  the 
epoch.     The  pregnant  and  nervous  prose  resembles  Meredith's. 

New  Canterbury  Tales 

A  collection  of  excellent  stories,  suffused  with  the  renaissance 
spirit,  the  spirit  of  chivalry,  the  worship  of  love,  the  tender 
gracious  devotion  to  women,  combined  with  a  vivid  sense  of  life 
and  love  of  action. 

Richard  Yea  and  Nay 

A  boldly  imaginative  study  of  the  life  and  character  of  Richard 
Coeur-de-Lion,  even  more  poetical  in  stjle  than  "The  Forest 
Lovers."  Richard's  personalit}-  is  very  full}-  and  intimately 
delineated  ;  he  is  shown  as  a  man  of  strong  passions,  fiercely  in 
love,  mutinous  towards  his  father,  Henry  II.,  whose  old  age  is 
portrayed. 

Hichens,  Robert  S. 
Byeways 
Feli.v 
Flames 

A  spiritualistic  plot,  shows  how  a  sort  of  human  Mephistopheles 
seizes  one  man's  personality  and  seduces  another,  and  has  a 
serious  bearing  on  the  problem  of  moral  influence.  It  is  a  weird 
story,  akin  on  one  side  to  "  Dr.  Jekyll  and  Mr.  Hyde,"  and  on 
the  other  to  tiie  realistic  studies  of  low  and  evil  life  by  Zola  and 
his  imitators. 


248 


cSoo/i-looers    Lihranif. 


Hichens,  Robert  S. — continued. 
Folly  of  Eustace,  The 
Green  Carnation,  The 

An  audacious  comic  fantasy,  satirising  the  ways  of  society  and 
parodying  the  mannerisms  of  certain  popular  writers. 

Imaginative  Man,  An 

A  man  of  cynical  and  misanthropic  temperament  gives  way  to 
his  morbid  impulses,  with  the  result  that  he  becomes  a  maniac. 
Scene  :  Cairo,  with  pictorial  descriptions  of  the  desert  and  the 
city,  particularly  of  the  seamy  side  of  native  life. 

Londoners,  The 

A  long  and  farcical  extravaganza,  containing  many  excellent 
examples  of  the  author's  pungent  wit. 

Prophet  of  Berkeley  Square,  The 

Slave,  The 

Another  fantasy,  interwoven  with  real  life ;  the  heroine,  a 
creature  swayed  by  an  overpowering  passion  for  jewels,  whose 
loss  by  theft  of  a  wondrous  emerald  is  a  cardinal  incident,  and 
the  loves  and  sorrows  of  other  personages. 

Tongues  of  Conscience 

Five  gloomy  stories  of  remorse  carried  to  morbid  extremes.  A 
man  haunted  by  the  cry  of  his  dead  child  whom  he  had  neglected, 
a  wife  who  poisons  her  husband  because  he  writes  immoral 
fiction,  such  are  the  gruesome  themes.

Higginson,  E.     Mariella,  of  Out-West

a  curious  study  in  character  ;  later,  at  sixteen,  she  falls  in  love, 
and  later  still,  on  the  return  of  her  old  teacher,  forms  a  stronger 
attachment  :  then  come;  the  renunciation  of  her  first  lover,  who 
carries  out  his  vow  to  love  her  bettci-  than  himself.

Hill,  Headon   (F.  Grainger) 

Beacon  Fires  Caged  !

Duke  Decides,  The 

A  young  Englishman,  impelled  bj-  destitution,  joins  a  gang  of 
criminals  in  New  YorU  ;  soon  after  he  hears  that  he  has  become 
heir  to  a  dukedom.  The  story  describes  the  many  abortive 
efforts  of  the  gang  to  wreak  their  revenge  on  him  for  failing  to 
carry  out  their  scheme. 

Guilty  Gold  Plunder  Ship,  The

Mrs.  A.  D.  T.  Whitney 
Hoare,  Edith  G.     A  Faulty  Courtship 

Hobbes,  John  Oliver  (Mrs.  Craigie) 
Bundle  of  Life,  A 
Gods,  The,  Some  Mortals,  and  Lord  Wickenham 

The  unpleasant  theme  of  a  good  man's  marriage  to  a  woman 
with  a  past,  treated  from  a  loft}-  standpoint.  The  husband's 
story  is  a  pathetic  fable  of  forsaken  ideals.  Lord  W'icUenham, 
confidant  of  the  husband  and  commentator  on  the  events, 
expounds  the  moral  that  a  man  should  marry  the  goddess  that 
he  loves,  and  not  from  mere  weakness  stoop  to  earth.  The  book 
is  severe  on  the  shams  of  high  life,  exposing  the  inner  history  of 
some  shady  characters  among  the  wealthier  classes. 

Herb  Moon,  The 

A  little  love  drama,  whose  main  concern  is  the  prolonged  and 
interrupted  courtship  of  a  supposed  widow,  who  has  a  living 
husband  in  an  asylum,  the  friend  of  her  brother.  She  is  a 
pathetic  type  of  suffering  womenkind  ;  the  man  is  a  strong, 
pure-natured  taciturn  character.  A  high  and  pure  ideal  of  love 
and  wedlock  is  inculcated. 

Love  and  the  Soul  Hunters 

Robert  Orange 


0r^     ,Aioo/i=loi<ers    L.i6rurij.  251 

Hobbes,  John  Oliver  (Mrs.  Craigie)--c^«;^/"'<^^. 

School  for  Saints,  The 

"The  School  for  Saints,"  and  "  Hobcrt  Orange,"  its  sequel,  are 
two  navels,  or  two  parts  of  a  life-history,  that  involve  searching 
and  subtle  consideration  of  the  deeper  problems  of  the  moral 
nature.  The  characters  are  nearly  all  uncommon  in  type,  highly 
intellectual,  lofty  in  their  ideals,  thoroughly  modern.  Real  and 
imaginary  persons  are  mingled  ;  Disraeli  being  a  prominent 
character.  The  development  of  Robert  Orange  is  the  main 
theme.  He  loves  a  beautiful  woman,  whose  husband  proves  to 
be  living,  and  they  part ;  yet,  when  the  husband  dies,  leaving 
them  free,  the  idealist  and  visionary,  born  with  the  mark  of  the 
cloister  on  his  brow,  renounces  love  and  seeks  for  peace  in  a  life 
of  austerity  ;  he  has  already  abandoned  a  brilliant  political  career. 
In  the  earlier  volume  there  are  vivid  sketches  of  Breton  life. 

Serious  Wooing,  The 

A  delineation  of  high  society  life,  castigating  its  pettiness  and 
insincerity  with  delicate  wit.  The  headstrong  heroine,  sacrificing 
all  to  her  forbidden  passion,  appears  well-nigh  respectable  com- 
pared with  the  empty  and  cjnical  worldlings  who  constitute  this 
spurious  aristocracy. 

Tales 

Four  very  actual  and  searching  stories  of  modern  life  and  high 
society,  trenchant  in  their  criticism  of  life  ;  full  of  thought,  often 
epigrammatically  expressed.  "The  Sinner's  Comedy"  is  a  fair 
specimen  ;  the  sinner's  wife  is  an  admirable  woman,  despised 
and  slighted  by  her  husband,  whereas  she  ought  to  have  been  the 
good  genius  of  a  nobler  man,  whose  life  is  a  blank  without  her. 
The  irony  of  fate — or  rather  of  human  society — is  set  forth  with 
bitter  pathos.  This  book  shows  the  fondness  of  aphorism  by 
which  all  the  author's  novels  are  characterised. 

Tales  about  Temperaments 

Hocking,  Joseph 
All  Men  are  Liars 

A  salvation  story  of  pessimism,  by  a  dissenting  minister.  A 
despairing  youth  is  saved  from  misery  and  ruin  by  a  woman 
whom  he  once  rescued  from  depravity. 

"And  Shall  Trelawney  Die?" 

Also  "The  Mist  on  the  Moors."  Melodramas  played  out  in  the 
lonely  homesteads,  with  a  background  of  Cornish  moorland. 
Young  love  and  villainy  are  the  opposing  motives. 

Birthright,  The 

Fields  of  Fair  Renown 

The  characters  are  cliiefly  literary  men,  journalists,  artists  and 
the  like  ;  the  villain  a  thorough-going  realist  who  has  no  true 
mission,  contrasted  with  writers  who  inspire  and  lift  the  soul. 
As  much  sermon  as  romance.     Cornish  local  colouring. 

Flame  of  Fire,  A  Jabez  Easterbrook 

Greater  Love  "  Lest  We  Forget  "

Change  of  Air,  A 

A  revolutionary  poet,  being  received  in  county  society,  and  falling 
in  love  with  the  daughter  of  a  country  squire,  modifies  his  old

pointed  and  vivacious  dialogue,  and  of  his  admirable  pictures  of 
society.

Hope,  Anthony   (A*  H.  Hawkins)   -ff);/^/»»fr/. 

Dolly  Dialogues 

Smart  and  witty  chat  between  a  society  lady  and  her  admirer, 
whom  she  is  alleged  to  have  jilted  ;  characters  lightly  sketched 
and  connected  in  a  slight  tale. 

Father  Stafford 

A  countr\-  house  comed}-.  Situation  :  a  square  party  in  love  with 
each  other,  but  the  wrong  couple  betrothed.  To  set  this  state  of 
things  right  with  proper  regard  for  the  convenances  and  for  the 
feelings  of  the  young  ladies,  demands  a  great  deal  of  diplomacy 
and  finesse.  The  comedy  works  itself  out  with  chat  and  repartee, 
and  with  satire  that  brushes  over  the  surface  of  things,  while 
deeper  emotions  are  stirred  in  the  case  of  the  sworn  celibate, 
whose  life  is  well-nigh  wrecked  by  love  for  the  witty  heroine. 

God  in  the  Car,  The 

Deals  with  smart,  up-to-date  society.  The  leading  character, 
Juggernaut,  a  man  of  exaggerated  will,  promotes  a  company  to 
take  over  vast  estates  in  South  Africa. 

Half  a  Hero 

Heart  of  Princess  Osra,  The 

Indiscretion  of  the  Duchess,  The 

Intrusions  of  Pegg3^  The 

King's  Mirror,  The 

A  romance  concerning  itself  more  with  character,  yet  still  purely 
imaginary  character,  in  a  purely  imaginary  world.  The  auto- 
biography of  a  young  German  potentate — how  he  frees  himself 
from  tutelage,  and  iiow  liis  pleasure-lo\ing  nature  has  to  give 
way  to  the  duties  of  his  position,  to  which  even  his  youthful 
passion  must  be  surrendered.  The  courtiers  and  politicians, 
princesses  and  women  of  the  world  who  are  the  minor  characters, 
are,  with  their  actions  and  talk,  all  conceived  in  the  same  half- 
serious,  half-comic  spirit. 

Man  of  Mark,  A 

The  farcical  inner  history  of  a  revolution  in  the  imaginarj-  Soutli 
American  Republic  of  Aureataland. 

Mrs.  Witt's  Widow 

Phroso 

Adventures  of  an  English  nobleman  on  a  Greelt  island — sensational 
fighting,  murdering  and  love-making  in  rapid  succession. 

Prisoner  of  Zenda,  The 

In  "The  Prisoner  of  Zenda"  and  "Rupert  of  Hentzau,"  its 
sequel,  are  presented  two  memoirs  of  the  career  of  Rudolf 
Rassendyll,  and  his  extraordinary  adventures  in  an  imaginary 
kingdom  in  Austrian  Tyrol.  Duels  and  hairbreadth  escapes, 
palace  intrigues  and  conspiracies,  and  two  episodes  in  which  the 
hero  takes  advantage  of  his  likeness  to  impersonate  the  king,  fill 
the  two  volumes  with  romantic  action.  The  characterisation 
also  is  romantic — the  chivalry  of  the  hero,  the  pr-ncely  nature 
of  the  heroine,  and  the  cool  energy  of  the  villain.  Rapid  and 
witty  conversation  is  a  distinctive  feature  of  the  books  ;  but  the 
characteristic  effect  results  from  placing  a  thoroughgoing  romance 
of  cape  and  swoi'd  in  a  19tli  ccntiu'y  environment,  and  this,  with 
the  device  of  the  imaginary  state,  has  been  the  keynote  of 
numerous  imitations.

Hope,  Anthony   (A.  H.  Hawkins) — continued. 

Ouisante 

"  Quisante  "  is  a  type  of  the  political  adventurer  ;  a  knave  in  his 
dealings,  yet  at  times  rising  to  the  plane  of  the  born  statesman  ; 
strong  in  intellect,  weak  in  bodj-,  with  an  indomitable  will.  A 
beautiful  aristocrat,  fascinated  with  his  brilliant  personality, 
throws  in  her  lot  with  his,  rejecting  the  gentlemanly  and  honest 
Marchmont.  The  book  is  mainly  an  elaborate  development  of 
this  situation  and  its  consequences  to  each. 

Rupert  of  Hentzau 

Simon  Dale 

A  study  of  Restoration  manners,  showing  the  same  characteristics 
of  brilliant  talk  and  social  intrigue.  The  hero,  a  country  squire, 
and  Charles  II.  are  rivals  for  the  favours  of  Nell  Gwj-nne. 

Tristram  of  Blent 

To  be  classed  among  the  author's  more  serious  work ;  another 
comedy  of  high  society,  the  manners  and  tone  of  which  are 
reproduced  with  realism,  and  at  the  same  time  a  facile  mastery 
of  its  humours.  History  of  a  curious  cause  celebre,  the  initial 
situation  of  the  plot  being  the  existence  of  two  claimants  to  the 
peerage  and  estates  of  Blent,  the  accredited  heir  and  his  beautiful 
cousin,  who  are  tacitly  in  love  with  each  other. 

Hope,  Ascott  R. 
All  Astray 
Hero  and  Heroine 
Young  Travellers'  Tales 

Hope,  Graham 

Cardinal  and  His  Conscience,  A 

My  Lord  Winchenden 

Triumph  of  Count  Ostermann,  The 

A  historical  story,  dealing  with  Russian  life  in  the  time  of  Peter 
the  Great  {circa  1725).  The  hero  is  a  German,  who,  through 
good  and  ill  report,  served  faithfully  his  master  and  his  adopted 
country,  and  gained  his  truest  tiMumph  in  his  final  disgrace.  A 
well-written  story  of  Court  intrigue.

Boss  of  Taroomha,  The 

The  "  Boss  "  is  a  dashing  \()img  woman,  the  owner  of  Taroomba, 
who  rules  her  men  on  an  Australian  sheep-farm  with  spirit. 
The  story  is  short — the  arrival  of  a  German  piano-tuner,  a  young 
man  with  ambitions,  his  chats  with  the  sympathising  "  Boss,"" 
and  a  scene  of  violence  and  bravery  when  tiie  pair  defend  a 
storehouse  against  a  gang  of  bushrangers. 

Bride  from  the  Bush,  A 

The  governing  situation  of  this  social  comed)-  is  tlie  awiiward 
position  of  an  uncultured  bride  from  Australia  on  her  introduction 
to  her  husband's  family  circle  and  to  society  in  Enghuul. 

Dead  Men  Tell  no  Tales 
No  Hero 

Describes  how  an  invalided  soldier  goes,  to  oblige  an  old  sweet- 
heart, to  rescue  her  son,  who  is  believed  to  be  in  the  clutches  of 
an  "  undesirable  "  widow.  He  succeeds,  and  marries  the  widow 
himself. 

Peccavi ! 

A  High  Church  clergyman's  life-long  penance  for  a  crime  that 
has  ruined  his  career — a  penance  that  involves  almost  sujier- 
human  self-sacrifice,  and  finally  the  offering  of  his  life -workcii 
out  with  unrelieved  tragedy-  Scene  :  a  country  pai-isii  in 
England. 

Rogues  March,  The 

The  theme  is  unpleasant ;  a  vigorous  narrative  of  con\ict  life  in 
early  New  South  Wales.

Young  Blood 

The  resolute  struggle  for  a  living  and  the  ultimate  success  of  a 
young  man  in  London  whose  father  has  been  ruined  and  dis- 
graced by  bankruptcy.  The  boy  tries  to  get  work  in  the  city, 
has  a  rough  experience  as  teacher  in  a  private  school,  and 
succeeds  as  a  magazine  contributor.  A  mystery-plot  is  worked 
out  concurrently,  in  which  a  farcical  company-promoter  and  a 
melodramatic  Nillain  take  the  leading  roles.

Annie  Kilburn 

Town  and  country  life  depicted  in  low  tones  ;  the  lifeliUe  heroine,

more  serviceable  than  a  tract.  In  the  '  Quality  of  Mercy'  sevcruJ 
of  tlie  characters  reappear.

Howells,   W.  D, — coiit'nnied. 
April  Hopes 

The  April  time  of  courtship  :  the  quarrels,  reconciliations,  and 
all  the  vicissitudes  of  corned}-  and  tragedy  that  chequer  the  loves 
of  two  young  people. 

Chance  Acquaintance,  A 

One  of  his  most  serious  stories.  A  highly  educated  gentleman 
from  Boston,  a  courtly  prig,  attaches  himself  to  a  party  of  tourists 
from  the  West,  and  wins  the  heart  of  a  romantic  and  unsophis- 
ticated girl.  Their  mutual  attraction  and  incompatibility  are 
subtly    exhibited.  The    Bostonian's    exclusiveness    and    over- 

refinement  keep  him  hesitating,  and  a  happy  accident  reveals  to 
the  girl  his  real  character. 

Dr.  Breen's  Practice 

Dr.  Breen,  a  young  lady  who  has  adopted  the  medical  profession, 
after  an  unfortunate  love  affair,  practises  in  a  small  seaside  town 
in  Maine.  She  is  an  example  of  New  England  Puritanism, 
strongly  developed  on  the  moral  rather  than  the  religious  side. 
The  story  of  her  love  and  marriage  fiu-nishes  pictures  of  summer 
boarding-house  life. 

Flight  of  Pony  Baker,  The 

Hazard  of  New  Fortunesf,  A.     2  vols. 

Mr.  and  Mrs.  March  come  to  New  York,  where  he  is  to  conduct 
a  magazine;  their  experiences  in  search  of  family  apartments 
are  a  main  episode.  The  audacious  and  versatile  Fulkerston, 
overflowing  with  vitality  and  humour;  the  mean  and  aimless 
man  of  talent,  Beeton,  and  the  family  who  have  been  enriciied  by 
a  boom  and  are  trying  to  be  fashionable,  are  the  most  stril<ing 
characters. 

Kentons,  The 

Minister's  Charge ;  or  Apprenticeship  of  Lemuel  Barker,  The 
Lemuel,  an  awkward,  countrified  boy,  but  upright  and  sincere, 
is  persuaded  to  go  to  Boston  by  the  city  clergyman's  praise  of 
his  verses.  His  homely  nature  is  repelled  by  the  conventions 
and  flippancy  of  society  ;  and  discouraged  in  his  literary  ambition 
he  goes  through  various  humble  employments,  settling  down  as 
a  country  teacher.  In  his  boarding-house  experiences  he  falls 
into  temptation,  but  all  through  his  Boston  life  his  honest 
character  asserts  itself.  The  Minister,  with  his  efforts  to  do  right 
by  his  flock,  and  his  amiable  fibs,  is  a  verj-  different  character. 

Quality  of  Mercy,  The 

A  crowd  of  diverse  characters,  a  defalcating  treasurer  of  a 
company,  whose  flight  and  return  are  the  basis  of  the  story,  his 
two  daughters,  the  company's  chairman,  and  a  pair  of  young 
pressmen. 

Questionable  Shapes 

Three  "  modern  "    ghost    stories   dealing   with  the  psychological 
aspect  of  apparitions  ;  the  mystery  does  not  attach  to  what  was 
seen,  but  to  how  it  came  to  be  seen. 
Ragged  Lady 

The  life  of  a  New  England  girl  from  childhood  to  her  second 
marriage.  Round  her  are  grouped  a  variety  of  Americans,  wiio 
are  nearer  to  a  comic  type.     Scenes  :   America  and  Italy, 


iJjoo/i.= lovers    l,ihrcLny.

Howells,   "W.  D. — contiimed. 

Rise  of  Silas  Laphani,  The 

Business  here  occupies  much  the  same  place  that  love  does  in 
the  average  novel ;  at  all  events,  it  marks  the  beginning  of  his 
later  manner,  the  New  Realism,  in  which  he  approaches  Henry- 
James  most  nearly  ;  a  realism  characterised  by  extreme  minute- 
ness of  detail  and  careful  delineation  of  trivialities  and  common- 
places, tlic  aim  being  to  represent  life  rather  than  to  tell  a  story. 
This  book  is  a  faithful,  though  purely  external,  delineation  of  the 
whole  life  of  a  village  in  Maine.  The  central  figures  are  a 
journalist,  an  unprincipled  fellow,  and  his  wife,  whose  smartness 
leads  him  eventually  into  evil  courses.  He  is  a  type  of  modern 
decadence,  while  she  is  a  more  eccentric  character,  one  in  whom 
religious  and  moral  ideas  have  turned  into  cast-iron  rules. 

Story  of  a  Play,  The 

Their  Silver  Wedding  Journey 

A  sequel  to  Their  Wedding  Journey.  The  middle-aged  Marches 
sail  to  Europe  and  wander  through  Germany  ;  the  incidents  of 
the  voyage  and  the  sights  they  see  in  the  Continental  cities 
being  described  in  a  chatty,  impressionist  style.  Among  their 
fellow-travellers  are  a  young  couple,  over  whose  incipient  love 
affair  Mrs.  March  watches  with  maternal  fondness. 

World  of  Chance,  The 

A  shadowy  story,  touching  on  the  regeneration  of  Society,  and 
embodying  the  teachings  of  Tolstoy.  Peace  Hughes,  the  daughter 
of  a  cranky  socialist,  a  strong,  self-reliant  girl,  represents  an 
unusual  type  among  the  autiior's  heroines.

Coin  of  Edward  VII.,  A 

A  detective  story.  A  young  lady  is  murdered  and  another  is 
suspected  of  the  crime,  jealousy  being  assigned  as  the  motive. 
The  development  of  the  plot  fixes  the  crime  on  the  leader  of  a 
gang  of  burglars,  who  conducts  his  operations  from  a  yacht 
called  the  "  Dark  Horse."

Golden  Wang- Ho,  The 
Jade  Eye,  The 

A  certain  Chinese  idol  had  its  eye,  "  The  Jade  Eye,"  stolen  for 
the  sake  of  the  valuable  diamond  it  contained.  Ultimately,  by 
different  means,  both  the  eye  and  the  idol  were  brought  to 
England.  The  story  records  the  exciting  incidents  connected 
with  the  re-fitting  of  the  eye  to  its  socket,  and  with  the  fate  of 
the  treasure  revealed  by  that  act.

planned  crime  after  crime,  escaping  the  gallows  by  good  luck, 
but  punished  by  the  contempt  of  her  only  child.

Affairs  of  the  Heart 

Love  episodes,  comic  or  sad,  of  which  the  salient  characteristic

Hard  Woman,  A 

"  A  Story  in  Scenes,"  a  series  of  lively  dialogues  developing  the 
story  of  a  clever,  but  shallow  and  sclfisli  woman,  and  her  gradual 
moral  descent. 

Human  Interest,  The 

"A  Study  in  Incompatibilities,"  farcical  in  plot,  and  in  the 
drawing  of  the  characters  a  satire  on  some  modern  types,  the 
literary  woman  innocent  of  worldly  knowledge,  the  egotistic 
painter,  etc. 

Maiden's  Progress,  The 

"  A  Novel  in  Dialogue" — the  social  career  of  Moderna,  a  blasee

'Bert  Edward,  the  Golf  Caddie,

Crowborough  Beacon 

A  romance  of  a  hundred  years  ago,  recoimting  the  strange

scaffold.

Little  Lady  Mary ;  and  Her  Best  Friend 

Two  tales  of  self-sacrifice.  Lady  Mary  is  an  audacious  and

a  group  of  lovable  characters.

Hutton,  Edward.     Frederic  Uvedale 

A  sketch  of  a  beautiful  unworldly  character,  a  modern  knight  of 
the  Graal.  He  is  a  young  Roman  Catholic,  in  whose  soid  two 
alien  impulses  are  at  war — a  pagan  love  of  nature  and  life,  and 
a  mysticism  that  urges  him  towards  the  monastic  ideal. 

Hyne,  C.  J.  Cutcliffe

feats  and  humorous  incidents,  of  which  the  hero  is  a  truculent 
but  diverting  skippei'.

Jackson,  Wilfred  S.     Nine  Points  of  the  Law 

Some  burglars  liidc  their  swag  in  Windsor  Forest.  There  it  is 
found  by  a  bani<  clerit,  who,  after  some  adventures,  restores  it  to 
its  owner,  his  employer.  He  finds  in  the  latter's  daughter  a 
second  treasure,  more  desirable  than  the  first. 

Jaco  Treloar  ...  ...  ...  J.  H.  Pearce 

Jacob  Faithful  ...  ...  ...     Captain  Marryat 

Jacob,  Violet  (Mrs.  Arthur  Jacob).     Sheep-Stealers,  The 

Jacobs,  William  Wymark 

Lady  of  the  Barge,  The 

Light  Freights 

Some  stories  of  landlubbers  and  yoUels  are  added  to  the  customary 
anecdotes  of  the  coasting  trade  ;  both  are  up  to  the  former  lc\cl 
of  fluent  wit  and  humour. 

Many  Cargoes 

Miscellaneous  yarns  in  sailor's  lingo  ;  love  scenes  on  shipboard 
"A  Love  Passage,"  histories  of  practical  jokes  "A  Change  of 
Treatment,"  and  funny  misadventures  "In  Mid  Atlantic;" 
portraits  of  old  salts,  bigamous  captains  "  Mated,"  love-lorn 
mates  and  the  like. 

Master  of  Craft,  A 

Ludicrous  comedy  aboard  a  coasting  schooner  ;  the  skipper  finds 
himself  engaged  to  three  young  women  at  once,  and  has  to 
extricate  himself  by  a  sham  suicide. 

Sea  Urchins 

Droll  and  racy  stories  of  life  in  the  mercantile  marine,  very  salty 
in  flavour.  The  boy  who  fondly  believed  he  was  aboard  a  pirate 
is  a  diverting  specimen  of  these  urchins. 

Skipper's  Wooing,   I  he 

Broad  comedy  from  beginning  to  end.  The  skipper  and  his  crew 
go  from  port  to  port  in  search  of  a  missing  man,  his  sweetheart's 
father,  and  meet  with  diverting  adventures.  Another  short  tale, 
"The  Brown  Man's  Servant"  is  appended. 

Jacobite,  The  ...  ...  ...  Harry  Lindsay 

Jacobite  Exile,  A  ...  ...  ...  G.  A.  Henty
