James,  Henry,  Junr, 
American,  The 

A  scientifically  cold  study  of  the  differences  of  character — a  type 
of  the  gi'eater  part  of  Mr.  James's  fiction,  also  a  good  example  of 
the  emigre  novel.  A  self-made  American  goes  to  Europe  to 
enjoy  his  "pile"  and  becomes  engaged  to  a  French  widow  of 
noble  famih'.  The  match  is  a  good  one  for  both  parties,  but 
wiien  her  people  realise  the  immense  social  gulf  that  sunders 
them,  they  break  off  the  engagement.  The  pride  and  meanness 
of  the  old  nobility  are  sharply  contrasted  with  the  American's 
pluck  and  good  nature. 

Awkward  Age,  The 

Unique,  even  among  his  own  works,  as  an  example  of  psycho- 
logical analysis  ;  portrays  the  manners,  speech  and  characters 
of  a  coterie  of  smart  degenerates.  The  incidents  are  slight,  the 
action  intellectual  and  emotional,  the  dialogue  so  allusive  that  an 
effort  is  needed  to  follow  it. 

Better  Sort,  The 

Short  stories  recording  the  misses  and  short-dropping  arrows  of 
emotion  and  adventure  among  the  "better  sort"  of  people. 
Readers  may  judge  as  to  the  author's  ironic  intention. 

Daisy  Miller;  a  Comedy 

A  sprightly  American  girl  on  a  holiday  in  Europe,  whose  ignorance 
of  the  conventions  and  high  spirits  get  her  into  risky  situations 
and  scandalise  her  compatriots.     The  end  is  tragic. 

Embarrassments 

Europeans,  The 

A  brother  and  sister  from  Europe,  Americans  by  extraction,  come 
to  seek  their  fortune  among  relatives  near  Boston.  This 
situation  enables  the  author  to  show  how  the  serious  and 
colourless  life  of  tiiese  descendants  of  the  Puritans  appears  to 
outsiders. 

In  the  Cage 

A  telegraph  girl,  a  charming  character,  takes  a  keen  interest  in 
the  love  affair  of  two  people,  and  out  of  the  telegrams  which 
they  send,  pieces  together  a  romance.  It  is  a  subtle  web  of 
guess-work,  and  both  the  ingenuity  of  the  ideas  and  the  language 
are  particularly  elaborate. 

Lesson  of  the  Master,  The 

Other  House,  The 

Rose  Armiger  is  a  tragic  character,  a  nature  of  repressed  but 
passionate  energy  ;  the  story  culminates  in  a  burst  of  powerful 
emotion. 

Passionate  Pilgrim,  A 

The  title-story  is  about  an  American  hcii"  to  an  English  estate,  a 
much  idealised  hero,  in  whom  Anglomania  is  converted  into  a 
dreamy  poetic  passion.  Madame  de  Mauves  is  an  American  girl 
married  to  a  Frencli  nobleman.  She  believed  that  ancient 
lineage  meant  everything  that  was  noble  and  refined,  but  is 
cruelly  disenchanted  ;  her  pure  and  womanly  character  is  the 
strength  of  the  story. 


4^^^     .^Book=lovers     Cifyrary. 


273 


James,  Henry,  ]\xnv,—conthined. 

Roderick  Hudson 

Roderick  is  a  resume  in  little  of  the  strength  nnd  weakness  of 
cvenius,  an  example  of  the  artistic  temperament.  He  is  a  young 
American  sculptor  taken  to  Italv  by  one  of  Mr.  James's  rich 
virtuosi  ;  and  there,  after  spasmodic  production  of  a  few  master- 
pieces, the  fame  of  his  genius  expires.  He  outrages  the  love  ot 
his  betrothed,  and  flouts  his  benefactor  ;  but  is  felt  to  be  hardly 
blameworthy  since  he  has  no  conscience. 

Sacred  Fount,  The 

Soft  Side,  The  ,    ,        , 

More  studies  of  psychical  phenomena  and  of  the  abnormal. 
"John  Delaroy  "  contrasts  the  vulgar  attitude  of  an  editor  anxious 
to  exploit  a  dead  writer's  life,  with  the  reverent  affection  of  his 
sister.  "  Europe  "  anatomises  the  feelings  of  three  ladies  who  are 
obliged  to  postpone  their  tour  on  the  Continent  because  their 
acved  mother  will  not  die.  "The  Great  Good  Place"  is  a  study  of 
an  intangible  phase  of  consciousness,  dreamland  and  its  con- 
nections with  wakingthougnts.  "Paste"  describes  the  suspicions 
aroused  through  the  discoverv  of  a  valuable  necklace  among  the 
effects  of  a  deceased  lady  of  moderate  means.  Some  psychical 
and  fantastic  stories  arc  of  a  more  esoteric  kind  than  these. 

Spoils  of  Poynton,  The 

A  domestic  comedy  hingeing  on  the  destinies  of  a  fine  old 
country  house,  full  of  articles  of  virtu,  which  an  elderly  lady 
who  is  passionately  fond  of  the  place  schemes  to  retam  when 
her  son,  the  actual  owner,  gets  married.  The  heartburnings  and 
the  squabbles  are  terminated  by  a  very  ironical  catastrophe. 

Terminations 

"The  Death  of  the  Lion,"  a  comic  sketch,  satirises  a  clique  of 
admirers  gathered  round  a  decadent  novelist,  who  dies  of  a  cold 
while  they  are  waiting  to  hear  him  read  his  MS.  "  The  Middle 
Years"  portrays  a  young  novelist  of  promise  who  is  dying,  and, 
in  spite  of  a  friend's  devotion,  expires  with  a  prayer  on  his  hps 
for  another  chance  to  carry  out  his  life's  work.  "The  Altar  of  the 
Dead"  is  a  strange,  perhaps  unique,  work  of  psychological 
imagination.  The  initial  idea  is  that  a  man  founds  an  altar  tor 
his  dead  friends  and  institutes  a  kind  of  mystical  service.  1  He 
denouement  has  a  surprising  turn. 

Tragic  Muse,  The  ,     ,     a 

A  good  example  of  the  author's  technique  ;  a  problem  of  art  and 
love,  worked  out  psychologically  ;  the  characters,  English  people 
living  in  Paris. 

Two  Magics,  The 

"The  Turn  of  the  Screw,"  a  ghost  story  of  a  novel  kind,  for 
which  Sir  Edmund  Orne  was  a  study.  It  relates  the  attempts  of 
two  foul  spirits  to  ruin  the  souls  of  a  pair  of  children.  "Covering 
End,"  the  companion  story,  is  a  little  comedy  concerned  with 
the  doings  of  a  captivating  American,  who  makes  a  raid  on  an 
old  country  house  after  the  manner  of  her  compatriots.  Both 
written  in  the  author's  most  advanced  style. 

Washington  Square 


274  ^^^^    6Boo/i=lovers    J^iS 


rari^. 


James,  Henry,  ]\xnv.— continued. 
What  Maisie  Knew 

The  subject  is  an  unpleasant  domestic  intrigue,  of  which  an 
innocent  girl  is  an  involuntary  observer.  The  sordid  details  are 
only  hinted  at,  and  the  heroine's  soul  is  kept  unspotted  ;  but  the

By  Order  of  the  Company 

A  Virginian  romance  of  tlic  reign  of  James  1.,  concerned  with  the 
fortunes  of  a  beautiful  maid-of-honour  who  flees  to  the  Colonics, 
and  is  saved  by  a  valiant  husband  from  the  clutches  of  a  libertine 
nobleman.  Duels,  shipwrecks,  seiges.  escapes  from  poisoners, 
adventures  with  pirates  and  Ked  Indians,  follow  each  other  with 
inexhaustible  rapidity. 

Old  Dominion,  The 

A  highly-coloured  romance  of  Virginia  in  I'Jestoration  times,  when 
the  Colony  was  seething  with  disaffection  caused  by  the  sendin}* 
of  rebels  to  the  plantations.  The  hero  is  one  of  the  convicts  sokl 
into  this  slavery.  He  joins  the  rebellion  led  by  Sir  .lohn  HerUeley. 
His  love  for  iiis  master's  daughter  leads  to  a  series  of  sensational 
events.  Much  description  of  the  landscapes  and  the  stately  homes 
of  Virginia.

Kipling,  Rudyard 

Captains  Courageous 

The  life  of  New  England  fislicrmcn  on  the  hif^li  seas,  depicted 
with  realism,  the  harsh  lingo  and  the  uncouth  technicalities 
being  quite  a  stumbling-block  to  the  reader.  The  story  is  how 
an  invertebrate  and  pampered  young  millionaire  was  accidently 
torn  from  his  mother,  and  made  a  man  of,  b\'  drastic  treatment 
at  the  hands  of  a  rough  but  iiindly  sl<ipper. 

Day's  Work,  'i'he 

From  Sea  to  Sea 

Jungle  Book,  The,  and  the  Second  Jungle  Book 

Two  collections  of  fables  of  man  and  beast  in  India,  which  are 
poetical  rather  than  allegorical  in  motive,  the  fabulist  aiming  at 
interpreting  the  actual  character  and  mode  of  reasoning  of  the 
animals,  so  far  as  they  can  be  learned  by  observation. 

Just  SO  Stories 

Kim 

Kim  is  a  street  Arab  from  Lahore,  dcrelect  child  of  an  Irish 
soldier  ;  an  alert  precocious  little  vagabond,  whose  apprenticeship 
to  the  Secret  Service  of  the  Indian  Government  educates  him 
prematureU"  in  the  shady  walks  of  Anglo-Indian  life. 

Life's  Handicap 

Light  that  Failed,  The 

A  narrative  of  Bohemian  life  ;    intensely  realistic. 

Many  Inventions 

"  My  Lord  the  Elephant,"  and  "  In  the  Rukh,"  foreshadow  the 
studies  of  animal-character  and  of  jungle  life,  later  published  in 
the  Jungle  Books.  The  "  Finest  Story  in  the  World,"  is  an 
imaginative  tale  of  metempsychosis,  "  Brugglesmith  "  is  a 
character-farce.  In  "His  Private  Honour,"  and  "Love  o' 
Women,"  the  three  privates  I'cappear,  but  the  themes  are  of 
more  serious  import,  tliough  the  manner  is  as  boisterous  as  ever. 
Badalia  Herodsioot  is  a  realistic  picture  of  the  worst  kind  of 
Whitechapel  life.  The  "  Children  of  Zodiac,"  an  allegory,  and 
various  other  records  of  life  in  the  East  and  elsewhere,  add  to 
the  diversity  of  the  collection. 

Plain  Tales  from  the  Hills 

Pictures  of  Anglo-Indian  life  and  manners,  and  superficial 
sketches  of  the  natives. 

Soldier  Tales 


286 


tJjook= lovers    L.ihra.ry. 


Kipling*  Rudyard  — continued. 
Soldiers  Three 

Episodes  in  the  Indian  life  of  three  British  privates,  a  swaggering 
Irishman  of  drunken  proclivities  and  humorous  tongue,  a 
phlegmatic  YorUshireman,  and  an  irascihle  little  Cockney,  a  trio 
of  fast  friends  who  stand  shoulder  to  shoulder  in  many  perils 
and  escapades,  and  in  the  not  less  trying  ordeals  of  peace. 
Stalky  c^  Co. 

Episodes  in  the'  lives  of  three  boys  in  a  private  college 
(VV'estward  Ho  !  in  Devon),  where  boys  are  prepared  for  the 
Army.  The  three  are  very  unlike  other  boys;r"  their  exclusiveness 
and  knowingness,  but  Stalky  is  of  the  stuff  oi'  which  heroes 
are  made. 

Wee  Willie  Winkie 

The  prevailing  theme  of  the  first  series  is  an  ugly  one,  "  men 
and  women  playing  at  tennis  with  the  seventh  commandment ;  " 
the  unspeakable  Mrs.  Hawksbee  is  a  prominent  figure.  Wee 
Willie  Winkie  shows  the  author's  love  for  and  insight  into 
infantile  ways.  The  "Rickshaw"  is  a  psychical  story  of  an 
Englishwoman  who  had  been  cruelly  misused  by  her  lover,  and 
returns  to  haunt  him  in  the  very  streets  of  Simla.

Law  and  the  Lady,  The  ...  ...         WilUie  CoUins 

Law  Breakers,  The      ...  ...  ...  E.  Spender 

Lawless,  Hon.  Emily 

Grania 

A  cluster  of  barren,  melancholy  isles  off  the  Gahvay  coast, 
peopled  by  a  primitive  race  of  fishermen,  whose  dreamy  and 
humorous  character,  with  its  strain  of  gloom,  is  delineated  with 
deep  sympathy.  This  is  the  stage  on  which  is  played  out  a 
tragedy,  the  actors  being  a  pure  and  lofty-minded  fisher-girl,  a 
true  child  of  nature,  her  saintly  sister,  and  the  lover,  typically 
Irish  in  his  goodness  and  in  his  vices.  A  very  pathetic  story, 
worked  out  with  patient  care. 

Hurrish 

Maelcho 

A  scholarly  and  vivid  account  of  the  natives  beyond  the  Pale,  and 
their  struggles  with  the  English  in  the  times  of  the  Desmonds 
(Tudor  period),  the  stories  of  English  cruelties  being  fully 
emphasised.     The  whole  narrative  is  unutterably  melancholy. 

Major  Lawrence,  F.L.S. 

Traits  and  Confidences 

With  Esse.x  in  Ireland 

A  journal  of  the  expedition  of  Lord  Essex  to  suppress  the 
rebellion  in  Tyrone  ;  supposed  to  be  written  by  his  secretary. 
As  in  "  Esmond,"  the  contemporary  language  and  modes  of 
thought  are  reproduced.  The  narrative  is  based  on  historical 
documents,  presents  a  vivid  picture  of  the  miserable  state  in 
Ireland,  and  recounts  day  by  day  the  ambushes,  battles  and 
skirmishes  that  beset  the  progress  of  the  expeditioii.  On  the 
whole,  a  mournful  and  terrible  narrative,  rising  at  times  to  a 
poetic  intensity,  as  in  the  supernatural  episodes.  Essex  is  the 
dominant  character.      Period  :    1599. 

Lawrence  Clavering     ...  ...  ...     A.  E.  \V.  Mason 

Lawson,  Elsworth.     From  the  Unvarying  Star 

A  Northumbrian  story,  dealing  with  the  difficulties  and  unfolding 
the  love  story  of  a  Free  Church  minister,  who,  in  spite  of  under- 
going a  severe  ordeal,  finally  achieves  happiness. 

Lawson,  Henry 

Country  I  Come  From,  The 

Short  sketches  of  life  in  the  bush,  and  concise  character-portraits 
— in  their  masculine  vigour  and  terse  expressiveness,  as  in  their 
sharpness  of  contrast  and  grim  touches  of  reality,  not  unlike  the 
best  of  Bret  Harte. 

In  the  Days  when  the  World  was  Wide 

Joe  Wilson  and  His  Mates 

While  the  Billy  Boils 

A  characteristic  set  of  painfully  realistic  sketches  of  the  hardships 
of  the  life  of  a  settler  in  tiie  bush.  "The  Drover"  s  Wife  "  and 
"  His  Father's  Mate"  are  the  best.

Derelicts 

The  principal  character  suffered  imprisonment  for  fraud,  but 
patiently  endeavours  to  live  a  new  life  in  spite  of  the  ostracism 
of  society.  His  hopeless  love  for  a  lad)-,  who  through  exceptional 
events  is  flung  derelict  upon  the  world,  and  his  rivalry  with  his 
straight-laced  cousin,  an  upright  biit  pharasaical  clergyman,  are 
the  main  lines  of  a  piteous  story  that  nevertheless  ends  in 
happiness.

Machinations  of  the  Myo-oU,  The 

An  amusing  account  of  Burmese  life  by  a  writer  who  knows  the 
country  thoroughly'.  The  Myo-ok  are  the  district  officers  of 
Burma,  and  the  plot  turns  on  the  stealing  of  a  box  of  rupees 
from  a  safe  in  charge  of  one  of  them.

Seventeen  short  stories.  The  first,  "The  Regeneration  of  Dan,"

Derrick  Vaiighan 

A  similar  idealistic  portrait  of  character,  with  much  revelation  of 
the  authoress  in  Derrick's  aspirations  and  disappointments. 

Donovan 

In  "Donovan"  and  "We  Two"  (its  sequel)  we  have  two  novels, 
mainly  comprising  a  study  of  the  character  and  the  life  of  a 
supposed  agnostic,  who  is  estranged  from  religion  b}-  adversity 
rather  than  disbelief.

A  love  story  of  Cumberland,  concerned  with  Jacobite  plots  in  the 
time  of  William  the  Third  (1689). 

In  Spite  of  All

A  tale  chiefly  concerned  with  the  home  life  of  Algernon  Sydney 
at     Knowle     Park,     in    "Good     King    Charles's    golden    days" 
(about  1682). 
Knight   Errant 

Sentimental  idealism.  The  hero  sacrifices  love  and  happiness  in 
order  to  secure  his  sister's  honour  and  prosperity. 

Max  Hereford's  Dream 
To  Right  the  Wrong 

A  tale  of  the  Civil  War  epoch,  in  which  the  author  labours  to 
achieve  a  sympathetic  but  temperate  portrayal  of  John  Hampden 
and  his  career.

Maartens,  Maarten  (J.  H.  W.  van  de  Porten) 
God's  Fool 

A  study  of  a  strong  young  man  whose  intellect  is  that  of  a  child, 
and  who  has  lost  three  senses  by  an  accident  in  childhood, 
analysing  the  mental  states  and  difficulties  of  this  sensitive  and 
emotional  young  man,  who  finds  himself  shut  away  from  his 
fellow  creatures  when  he  desires  ardently  to  help  them  with 
his  wealth. 


SBoo^=/ovens    L.i6nant/.  307 


Maartens,  Maarten  (J.  H.  W.  van  de  Porten) — cont'uuied. 
Greater  Glory,  The 

A  no%-el  that  constitutes  an  indictment  of  "  infamy  in  higli  places, 
contrasting  worldly  power  witli  the  greater  glorj-  of  great 
thoughts  and  unseltislincss." 

Her  Memorj' 

The  subject  is  a  bereaved  luisband's  sorrow,  ending  only  with  his 
next   wedding.       A  society    story,    with   n   good   deal    of  ironical 
comedy  and  analysis  of  sentiment. 
My  Lady  Nobody 

A  delineation  of  Dutch  society  that  comes  nearer  perhaps  than 
any  other  novel  to  Thackeray's  manner  as  exemplified  in  "  Vanity 
Fair"  and  "Pendennis" — realism  that  portrays  human  beings 
neither  better  nor  worse  than  they  are  ;  a  running  commentary 
by  the  author. 

Old  Maid's  Love,  An 

An  unsophisticated  Dutch  student  is  in  love  with  a  Dutch  girl, 
while  a  bewitching  French  vicomtesse  loves  him  and  carries  him 
off  to  a  little  Eden  on  the  shores  of  Lake  Maggiore.  There  are 
vivid  pictures  of  life  and  character. 

Question  of  Taste,  A 

Some  pages  from  the  quiet,   humdrum   life  of  a  group  of  Dutch 
people  of  the  middle  class  ;    thoroughly  true  to  tlie  commonplace 
interests  of  average  family  life. 
Sin  of  Joost  Avelingh,  The 

This,  like  the  preceding  novels,  is  written  in  forcible  English,  and 
is  a  truthful  genre-painting  of  homely  Dutch  life,   with  life-like 
studies  of  character,   and  a  psychological  study  of  the  quixotic 
Joost.     The  plot  pivots  on  a  murder.

Dear  Lady  Disdain 

"A  free,  friendl)',  half  bo)ish  sort  of  nature,  wiiicli  seemed  to 
turn  with  impatience  and  even  contempt  from  sentimentalisms 
and  love  making."  Such  is  the  heroine,  and  there  are  other  fresh 
and  attractive  characters,  as  well  as  sundry  eccentrics.  The  plot 
moves  on  with  little  complication  to  the  happy  conclusion.

McCarthy,  Justin — continued. 

Fair  Saxon,  A 

The  fair  Saxon's  lover  is  an  Irish  M.P.,  whose  chivalry  leads  him 
into  embarrassing  entanglements  with  the  Fenians,  and  brings 
his  political  career  to  an  abrupt  close.  Dashing  and  incisive 
sketches  of  men  and  women,  often  satirical,  are  the  prominent 
features  of  the  booU. 
Linley  Rochford  Mononia 

Maid  of  Athens  My  Enemy's  Daughter 

Miss  Misanthrope  Riddle  Ring,  The 

Waterdale  Neighbours,  The 

A  novel  of  character.  The  contrasted  life-story  of  two  men  :  one 
a  manly  and  upright  worker  ;  thR  other  a  political  turncoat — a 
Chartist  who  joins  the  Tories  for  the  sake  of  personal  success, 
and  when  ruin  stares  him  in  the  face,  commits  an  act  of  treachery 
that  is  quickly  followed  by  retribution.  The  wife  of  the  latter, 
"a  sugar-plum"  the  author  calls  her,  forms  another  contrast 
with  high-souled   Myra.

A  story  of  the  great  Civil  \\'ar.  "  Cornet  Strong,"  the  hero  of 
many  fights,  turns  out  to  be  a  woman. 

London  Roses 

The  story  of  the  loss  and  recovery  of  a  manuscript  stolen  from 
the  British  Museum,  and  of  the  influence  of  that  event  upon  the 
love-making  of  four  young  readers  at  that  venerable  institution. 

Rupert,  by  the  Grace  of  God 

A  story  of  intrigue,  war,  and  adventure,  hingcing  on  a  plot  for 
placing  Prince  Rupert  on  the  English  throne.

Making  of  a  Schoolgirl,  The       ...  ...  ...  E.  Sharp 

Making  of  Christopher  Ferringham  ...  B.  M.  Dix 

Malcolm         ...  ..  ...  George  MacDonald 

Malet,  Lucas  (Mrs.  Mary  St.  Leger  Harrison) 
Carissima,  The 

A  domestic  drama  enacted  in  a  Genevese  hotel,  wherein  some 
consciences  are  brought  to  the  test. 

Colonel  Enderby's  Wife 

A  pessimistic  study  of  real  life  in  all  its  disappointments — the 
tragedy  of  an  ill-assorted  marriage  between  a  trusting  middle- 
aged  man  and  a  heartless  and  selfish  woman. 

Counsel  of   Perfection,  A 

A  study  of  character  and  motive  in  a  love  episode  of  very 
unusual  shape.  The  heroine  is  verging  on  middle  age,  and  has 
devoted  her  life  to  her  father,  a  selfish  and  unsympathetic  book- 
worm. Suddenly  she  is  awakened  to  affection  by  a  man  who  is 
not  worthy  of  her  ;  hopes  of  a  new  life  inspire  her  with  feelings 
like  those  of  youth  ;  but  when  her  lover  is  at  her  feet,  she 
renounces  the  temptation.  There  are  descriptive  passages  of 
English  country  life  and  Swiss  travel  sketches. 

Gateless  Barrier,  The 

A    spiritualistic   romance    founded  on  the  idea  of  bringing  back 
the  dead  by  will-power  in  conjunction  with  intense  lo\c. 
History  of  Sir   Richard  Calmady,  The 

The  life  history  of  a  man  terribly  deformed  from  birtii,  but 
otherwise  well  endowed  in  mind,  rank  and  possessions.  The 
effect  on  his  moral  nature  of  this  calamity  is  worked  out  at  great 
length  with  calm,  unshrinking  logic,  and  powerful  imagination. 
The  whole  history  is  painted  on  a  broad  canvas  :  the  characters 
are  many  and  various,  and  the  life  of  a  wealthy  English  squire 
is  amply  rendered. 

Little  Peter 

"A  Christmas  moralitj-  for  children  of  any  age." 

Mrs.  Lorimer 
Wages  of  Sin,  The 

A  stern  narrative  of  transgression  and  inexorable  Nemesis. 

Malice  of  Grace  Wentworth,  The  ...R.  H.  Heppenstall 

Malicious  Fortune        ...  ...  ...    Stella  M.  During

Mallock,  W.  H. 

Human  Document,  A 

Individualist,  The 

A  doctrinaire  novel,  intended  to  deride  the  belief  that  the  poorer 
classes  can  be  benefited  by  culture.  This  object  is  pursued  by 
means  of  burlesques  of  social  reforms,  lialf-disguised  tr:i\esties  of 
real  persons,  and  exaggerations  of  the  failings  and  vulgarities  of 
ill-bred  people.

The  history  of  two  children,  who,  liUe  "  John  Halifax,"  are  loyal

Parish  of  Hilby,  The 

Patten  Experiment,  The 

A  comedy  of  character,  with  a  farcical  plot.  A  wealthy  family 
try  to  live  for  a  week  on  a  labourer's  wages,  and  meet  with 
unexpected  difficulties. 

Susannah 

A  young  lady  in  reduced  circumstances  goes  out  as  general 
servant,  so  that  she  may  be  able  to  nurse  her  dying  brother. 
She  meets  with  some  comic  experiences  of  life,  and  some  severe 
ones;  and  the  upshot  is  that  she  loses  a  doubtful  lover  and  gains 
a  true  one.

Marriott,  Charles 

Column,  The 

A  high-flown  and  bizarre  story,  with  a  mystical  heroine  and  other 
characters  who  are  not  very  hUc  average  human  beings,  but 
have,  for  the  reader  who  cares  to  understand  them,  much  interest 
of  a  purely  intellectual  kind. 

House  on  the  Sands,  The 

Here  is  a  real  "  house  on  the  sands  "  (in  which  live  two  of  the 
characters  concerned  in  this  story)  and  a  figurative  one,  which  is 
the  political  fabric  reared  by  a  Cornish  M.P.,  whose  collapse 
justifies  its  title.

Death  Whistle,  The 

Whilst  in  gaol,  a  young  scion  of  the  nobility  meets  with  the 
leader  of  a  band  of  criminals,  who,  dying,  makes  him  heir  to  the 
"  swag."  He  takes  possession,  and  the  interest  of  the  story  is 
centred  in  the  efforts  of  the  band  to  regain  the  property  they  had 
stolen.

Goddess,  a  Demon,  The 

Magnetic  Girl,  The 

The  heroine,  having  rejected  a  slightly  deformed  suitor, 
indignantly  wishes  that  every  man  she  meets  may  fall  in  love 
with  her.  Her  wish  is  gratified  for  a  day,  and  she  passes 
through  some  diverting  experiences,  after  which  she  wisely 
reconsiders  her  decision  and  is  happy.

Mason,  A.  E.  "W. 

Clementina 

Courtship  of  Morrice  Buckler 

A  semi-historical  romance  after  the  stj-Ie  of  Dumas.  Opens 
tragically  with  a  story  of  Monmouth's  Rebellion  (1685),  and 
passes,  with  episodes  of  exciting  and  unexpected  incident,  from 
England  to  the  Tyrol.     The  heroine  is  Tyrolese. 

Fnsign  Knightley,  etc. 

Four  Feathers,  The 

Lawrence  Clavering 

A  modern  romance  of  love  and  intrigue,  sin  and  expiation,  with 
iiistorical  scenery,  the  Lake  District  and  Carlisle  at  tiie  time  of 
the  Jacobite  Rebellion  of  1715. 


320  y^^^    SBook=lovers    Cibrart/. 


Mason,  A.  E.  W. — continued. 
Miranda  of  the  Balcony 

An  intricate  story  of  rascality,  love,  and  adventure.  Compared 
with  the  former  works  it  shows  an  advance  towards  the  modern 
form  of  novel — the  social  comedy.  Sketches  of  life  in  Scilly, 
Tangier,  Spain,  and  Devon  give  the  action  a  changing  environ- 
ment. 

Philanderers,  The 

Watchers,  The 

An  entangled  web  of  adventure,  amorous  and  otherwise,  located 
in  18th  century  ScilU'. 

Mason,  A.  E.  "W.,  and  A.  Lang.     Parson  Kelly 

The  intrigues  tiiat  followed  the  Jicobite  Rebellion  of  1715  provide 
the  external  motives  of  this  novel,  which  is  really  a  comedy  of 
manners,  with  fine  ladies  and  theii*  lovers,  as  well  as  conspirators, 
for  the  dramatis  persona:.  Manj-  of  these  are  historical 
characters  ;  but  the  chief  personage,  the  witty  and  coquettish 
Lad\-  Oxford,  is  purely  fictitious.

Meredith,  George 

Adventures  of  Harry  Richmond,  The 

The  typical  example  of  Mr.  Meredith's  poetic  comedy  ;  incidents 
and  characters,  though  in  nature  and  grouping  often  wildly 
romantic,  being  justified  by  the  psychology.  The  story  is 
kaleidoscopic — changing  from  country  to  town,  from  England 
to  Germany. 

Amazing  Marriage,  The 

A  garrulous  old  houscUeeper's  story.  The  humorous  x)ld  sea-dog 
is  probably  a  likeness  of  Dundonald.  His  daughter  is  married 
precipitately,  and  then  neglected,  by  a  cynical  young  carl  whose 
remorse  and  unavailing  efforts  to  win  her  back  are  a  main  part  of 
the  tragic  results. 

Beauchamp's  Career 

Beauchamp  is  a  modern  Gracchus,  an  impetuous  and  disinterested 
champion  of  the  oppressed  ;  and  his  history  entails  a  broad  view 
of  the  politics  of  mid-century  England  in  the  light  of  Carlyie's 
teaching. 

Diana  of  the  Crossways 

The  story  of  a  woman  of  genius,  suggested  in  the  main  lines  of 
her  character  by  the  Hon.  Caroline  Norton,  though  the  famous 
episode  of  the  selling  of  the  secret  is  not  authentic. 

Egoist,  The 

\  psychological  narrative,  mercilessly  laying  bare  the  soul  of  a 
spoiled  child  of  fortune,  outwardly  a  pattern  of  conventional 
virtue,  inwardly  a  thrall  to  selfishness;  tragical  in  its  exposure 
of  the  secret  egoism  that  is  in  all  men. 

Evan  Harrington 

A  social  comedy.  Evan,  the  son  of  a  fashionable  tailor,  brought 
up  in  the  tastes  and  associations  of  a  class  higher  than  his  own, 
suddenly  finds  himself  confronted  with  the  responsibility  of  his 
father's  debts.  On  the  one  side  is  duty,  on  the  other  are  his  love 
for  a  well-born  maiden  and  natural  inclination.  How  Evan  solves 
the  problem,  happily,  yet  without  abjuring  his  manhood,  is  the 
business  of  the  plot. 

Lord  Ormont  and  His  Aminta 


326  Jd^^    ^oo/i=lovens    Cihnary. 


Meredith,  George — continued. 
One  of  Our  Conquerors 

Full  of  tliought,  humour  and  criticism  of  our  present  conditions 
of  life.  The  story  of  a  millionaire  who  began  his  career  by 
marrying  a  rich  old  widow.  From  her  he  ran  away  with  her 
young  companion,  and  everything  prospers  with  him,  though  he 
can  never  get  into  "  Society." 

Ordeal  of  Richard  Feverel,  The 

The  philosopher  father's  abstract  system  of  education  proves  too 
rigid  and  unsympathetic  when  Richard  arrives  at  the  threshold 
of  manhood  and  falls  in  love — hence  the  ordeal.  A  profoundly 
tragic  story,  bringing  unutterable  woe  to  father  and  son  as  the 
ultimate  fruit  of  blindness  and  error. 

Rhoda  Fleming 

The  heroine  is  daughter  of  a  yeoman  ;  tiie  hero  a  farmer.  In  the 
rustic  scenes,  farmers  and  their  women-folk,  pot-house  company 
and  over-fed  labourers,  provide  a  good  deal  of  humour  :  but  the 
main  action  is  serious,  culminating  in  scenes  of  heartfelt  tragedy, 
even  though  the  dramatic  stress  is  alleviated  at  the  end. 

Sandra  Belloni ;  or  Emilia  in  England 

Shaving  of  Shagpat,  and  Farina 

Tale  of  Chloe,  The 

Tragic  Comedians,  The 

An    imaginative,    yet    essentially    truthful    version    of    the    later 
episodes  in  the  life  of  Ferdinand   Lassalle  (1825-64)  ;  the  actors 
real,  the  names  alone  altered. 
Vittoria 

Meredith,  Isabel.     A  Girl  Among  the  Anarchists 

There  is  nothing  whatever  in  "A  Girl  Among  the  Anarchists" 
which  is  invented.  The  whole  thing  is  an  experience  told  very 
simply,  but  convincingly.  The  book  is  a  sympathetic  study  of 
a  curiously  interesting  class  of  people. 

Merejkowski,  Dmitri 

Death  of  the  Gods,  The 

Forerunner,  The

Merriman,  Henry  Seton  (Hugh  Stowell  Scott) 
Barlasch  of  the  Guard 

Plot  and  intrigue  figure  largely  in  this,  the  latest,  of  Mr. 
Merriman,  and,  with  Napoleon's  Russian  campaign  for  his  period, 
the  author  has  woven  a  story  of  powerful  interest.  The  plot  is 
of  that  in\'olved  type  which  this  author  loves  to  portray,  and  the 
characters  such  as  he  can  only  draw,  Barlasch  making  a  noble 
centre-piece.

Grey  Lady,  The 

In  Kedar's  Tents 

The  adventures  of  an  Irishman,  who  enters  the  Spanish  service 
against  the  Carhsts,  witli  the  inner  history  of  a  desperate  plot  to

Isle  of  Unrest,  The 

A  complicated  romance  of  Corsica  in  the  j^ears  just  preceding  the 
Franco-German  War. 

Roden's  Corner 

Account  of  a  huge  commercial  plot,  with  descriptions  of  industrial 
life;  animated  by  indignation  against  the  abuse  of  trusts  and 
monopolies,  and  of  certain  fashionable  forms  of  organised  charity. 

Slave  of  the  Lamp,  The 

Sowers,  The 

The  conventional  "  Russian  novel  '"  reproduced  with  excellent 
realistic  studies  of  the  countrj-,  and  of  the  miserable  condition  of 
the  peasant  class. 

Velvet  Glove,  The 
Vultures,  The 
With  Edged  Tools 

The  pair  of  heroes,  both  in  love  with  the  same  girl,  go  to  Africa, 
the  west  coast  and  the  interior,  meet  with  extraordinary  adventures 
and  win  large  fortunes.

Mitchell,  S.  Weir 

Adventures  of  Fran(;ois 

A  romance  of  the  French  Re\()liition  ;  tlie  hero  (compared  to 
Dumas'  famous  jester  Chicot),  a  little  Ishmaelite  adrift  in  the 
Paris  streets  diirinj:»  the  Terror,  a  ligiit-hearted,  irresponsible 
rascal,  tells  his  astonishing  history.  Among  the  other  characters 
must  be  mentioned  the  dog  Toto,  and  the  .\iarquis  de  Ste.  Luce, 
a  fascinating  old  repi'obatc  whose  fortunes  are  mixed  up  witli 
those  of  Francois. 

Autobiography  of  a  Quack,  The 

Life  of  a  rascally  doctor  ;  a  death  bed  narrative.  A  curious 
study  of  the  mind  of  a  professional  cheat  ;  much  of  it  deals  with 
the  War  time.  The  medical  details  are  handled  realistically,  the 
author  himself  being  an  accomplished  physician  ;  and  scientific 
knowledge  is  shown  again  in  the  companion  storj-,  "The  Case  of 
George  Dedlow,"  a  realistic  tale  of  a  soldier  who  had  all  his  limbs 
amputated  and  nearly  lost  his  sense  of  identitj'.  The  tale  was 
widely  accepted  as  genuine  when  the  tale  appeared,  and  sub- 
scriptions are  said  to  have  been  sent  in. 

Circumstance 

Characteristics 

In  "Characteristics"  and  "Dr.  North  and  His  Friends"  (its 
sequel)  we  have  selections  from  the  table-talU  of  an  intimate 
coterie  of  highly  cultivated  men  and  women,  who  meet  constantly 
at  each  other's  houses  and  discuss  books,  art,  religion,  ethics  and 
themselves. 

Far  in  the  Forest 

A  tale  of  the  great  Pennsylvanian  forest  before  the  War,  when 
life  in  that  wild  region  was  of  a  heroic  kind.  Not  so  much  a 
romance  as  a  story  of  character  and  the  interaction  of  character. 
Two  personages  stand  out  above  the  rest,  the  heroine,  who  is 
introduced  to  us  at  the  bedside  of  her  dying  husband,  and  the 
German  scientist  whom  fate  brings  into  her  life  at  this  crisis. 
Hugh  Wynne 

The  men  and  the  events  of  the  War  of  Independence,  battles, 
duels,  plots,  escapes,  supposed  to  be  recorded  by  an  aged 
Quaker.  Washington  and  Lafayette,  Major  Andre  and  Dr. 
Rush,  are  drawn  at  length.  The  contrast  between  the  old 
Quaker  society,  with  its  operative  ideals  of  brotherly  love,  and 
the  universal  strife,  is  a  dominant  motive. 

Roland  Blake 

The  earlier  part  is  a  story  of  action,  camps  and  battles  in  the 
Civil  War ;  the  latter  a  love  idjll.

benefit  when,  as  King  of  his  Zulu  tribe,  he  rescued  Haviland  fi'om 
his  savage  warriors.

Montgomery,  Florence 

Misunderstood 

A  study  of  child-character  and  the  education  of  children  as 
exemplified  in  two  little  motherless  boys  who  are  neglected  by 
their  father. 

Prejudged 

A  love  idyll,  passing  in  a  French  peiisiojt,  an  English  girl  falling 
in  love  with  a  mysterious  young  man,  towards  whom  at  first  she 
had  conceived  an  instinctive  antipathy. 

Seaforth 

Thrown  Together 

A  sensitive  girl,  unappreciated  by  her  parents,  and  a  petted 
boj',  are  thrown  together,  and  their  mutual  influence  is  not  only 
good  for  them,  but  brings  their  respective  parents  to  a  better 
understanding  of  their  children's  dispositions  and  their  own 
responsibilities. 

Transformed 

Montresor,  F.  F. 

Alien,  The 

A  mother's  conspiracy  to  give  her  illegitimate  son  the  inheritance 
of  another  ;  but,  though  the  incidents  are  absorbing,  plot  and 
mystery  are  of  less  importance  than  the  revelation  of  character 
in  a  number  of  very  different  men  and  women. 

At  the  Cross  Roads 

The  heroine  is  a  very  modern  figure,  hard  grained  but  capable  of 
intense  passion,  showing  her  strength  of  devotion  by  waiting 
seven  years  for  her  convict  lover  and  then  exerting  herself  to 
prove  his  innocence. 


~JA^^    ^ook=-lovers    I^ihrary.  335 

Montresor,  F.  F.     continued. 

False  Coin  or  True 

A  nameless  orphan,  rescued  from  domestic  driidj^ery  by  a  French 
mesmerist,  acts  for  him  as  mediimi,  and  wins  him  fame  and 
profit.  She  falls  in  love  with  an  honest  but  thick-headed  Scot, 
and  when  love  becUons  one  way,  and  duty  to  her  invalided 
guardian  another,  she  shows  herself  capable  of  true  gratitude. 
The  Frenchman  is  a  witty  satirist  of  British  cant  and  pseudo- 
Christianity. 

Into  the  Highways  and  Hedges 

The  impressionable,  cultivated  Margaret  weds  the  street  preacher 
Thorpe,  and  afterwards  learns  to  love  him  for  his  fortitude  and 
unmerited  sufferings.  He  is  unlearned,  illogical,  quixotic,  but 
full  of  faith  and  energy  in  the  pursuit  of  his  ideal ;  his  rival  for 
Margaret's  affection  being  a  hard-headed  Jew,  with  keen  insight 
into  human  nature  and  a  dominating  will. 

One  Who  Looked  On,  The 

A    chapter    of    family    historj-    told    by    a    motherly    Irish    aunt. 

.Among  tlie  ciiaracter  sketclics  is  that  of  an  English  Q.C.,  a  hard, 

unemotional    man    externally,   wiiose    heart  is  found  to  be  truly 

benevolent  and  capable  of  deep  affection.

Eyes  of  Light,  The 

Knight  Punctilious,  The 

An  eccentric  old  lady,  under  compidsion,  leaves  her  property  by 
several  duplicated  wills  to  two  young  men,  cousins,  in  such  a 
way  as  to  test  them  each  in  turn,  appointing  her  young  niece  to 
carry  out  her  wishes.  The  one  proves  selfish  and  unprincipled, 
the  other — "The  Knight  Punctilious'' — is  strictly  honourable, 
and  gets  his  due  reward  by  marrying  the  pretty  niece.

Moore,  F.  Frankfort 
According  to  Plato 

A  facetious  extravagance,  in  which  various  phases  of  modern 
life  are  travestied;  such  as  the  young  lady  with  her  Platonic 
amours,  the  School  of  Literature,  audacious  advertising,  financial 
business,   &c.

campaign  in  Ireland  :   written  from  the  Irish  point  of  view.

Moore,  F.  Frankfort — continued. 
Fatal  Gift,  The 

A  novel  of  manners  and  eighteenth  century  society.  The 
famous  Irish  beauties,  the  Misses  Gunning,  are  the  leading 
characters. 

Gray  Eye  or  So,  A 

A  love  tale,  comprising  facetious  pictures  of  house  part}'  life 
in  Ireland. 

I  Forbid  tiie  Banns 

A  comic  and  flippant  treatment  of  the  marriage  question,  a 
circumstantial  reductio  ad  ahsiirdum  of  a  woman's  rebellion 
against  legal  marriage. 

Impudent  Comedian,  The 

Jessamy  Bride,  The 

A  novel  founded  on  the  actual  story  of  the  eighteenth  century, 
introducing  Goldsmith,  Dr.  Johnson,  Burke,  and  Garrick. 

Millionaires,  The 

Nell  Gwyn,  Comedian 

Court  life  in  Restoration  times,  the  chief  parts  being  sustained 
by  Nell  Gwyn,  Lady  Castlemain,  the  Duchess  of  Portsmouth, 
Sir  Charles  Sedley,  and  Jack  Churchill,  afterwards  Duke  of 
Marlborough. 

Nest  of  Linnets,  A  They  Called  it   Love 

Phyllis  of  Philistia  Well,  After  All  ! 

Sojourners  Together 

Moore,  George 
Celibates 
Esther  Waters 

A  study  of  low  life  in  England,  especially  that  of  people  connected 
with  horse-racing.  The  liousehold  of  a  racing  squire,  described 
as  it  appears  to  Esther  Waters,  the  scullerj'-maid,  is,  on  the 
squire's  bankruptcy,  broken  up,  and  the  crowd  of  servants, 
jockeys,  and  stablemen  disperse,  most  of  them  to  get  a  living  on 
the  racecourse  or  in  shad\'  business  in  London.  Esther,  being 
ruined,  suffers  tlie  miseries  of  a  lying-in  hospital.  After  many 
troubles  she  marries  her  old  lover,  who  now  keeps  a  low  public- 
house  and  is  a  bookmaker. 

Evelyn  Innes 

Also  realistic,  almost  photographic,  in  its  detailed  picture  of 
squalid  life.  Evelj-n  is  a  musical  genius,  who  falls  under  the 
influence  successively  of  an  agnostic  and  man  of  the  world,  of  an 
artist  and  mystic,  and  of  a  Roman  Catholic  priest.  When  her 
conscience  has  driven  her  to  abandon  an  immoral  life,  she  enters 
a  sisterhood  and  devotes  the  rest  of  her  life  to  penitence. 

Mike  Fletcher 

Sister  Teresa 

Continues  Evelyn's  spiritual  history  after  she  has  renounced  the 
worldly  life  for  what  is  to  lier  the  only  alternative,  asceticism. 
The  technical  knowledge,  the  grasp  of  complex  experience,  and 
the  creative  energy  displayed  in  the  musical  episodes,  are 
devoted  here  to  portraying  the  conventual  life.

Journey  to  Nature,  A 

A  New  York  stockbroker,  threatened  with  heart  disease,  is  sent 
by  his  doctor  into  the  country  to  lead  a  natural  existence — in 
other  words,  "to  cease  to  live"  for  a  year.  This  is  the  record 
of  the  semi-animal  life  of  himself  and  his  son,  his  talks  with  a 
rough  and  hearty  old  doctor,  and  his  love  passages  with  a 
country  girl.

A  romantic,  heart-stirring  story,  opening  in  the  year  1627,  of  plot 
and  counter-plot  ;  of  murder,  bloodshed  and  ghastly  ruin,  and  of

the  Fens  district,  and  is  a  vivid  portrayal  of  the  manners  and 
customs  of  the  natives  of  that  day.

Mrs.  Hungerford 

Mrs.  Hungerford 

Anna  Howarth 


Emily  M.  Bryant 


The  camaraderie  of  a  young  journalist  and  a  girl  of  strong, 
wholesome  character  develops  by  insensible  degrees  into  a  closer 
affection.  No  plot  beyond  this,  but  an  account  of  ordinary  lives 
in  California  and  New  York. 


,/^ooA'=/ot>e/'s    Uifyfctfu/.  347 


Norris,  YrAvsk  -continued. 
Man's  Woman,  A 

Studies  of  character,  chiefly  in  a  virile  heroine  and  a  pair  of 
Arctic  explorers  :  a  glorification  of  strength  and  fortitude  and 
modern  enterprise. 

McTeague 

A  strong,  melodramatic  story,  realistically  delineating  Californian 
life  in  its  more  commonplace  and  depressing  aspects. 

Octopus,  The 

First  part  of  a  "  Trilogy"  that  is  to  treat  of  tiie  production  and 
distribution  of  food  in  America  ;  this  is  called  "  the  Epic  of  the 
Wheat,"  and  the  scene  is  California.  The  incessant  warfare 
between  the  wheat-growers  and  the  "Octopus" — the  great 
Railway  Trust  having  this  food  traffic  in  its  grip — is  the  present 
subject,  worked  out  with  a  multitude  of  characters  and  great 
detail. 

Pit,  The 

"  The  Pit  "  is  the  second  story  of  the  Epic  of  the  Wheat,  of 
which  "The  Octopus"  is  the  first.  The  latter  story  describes 
the  contest  betwean  the  Californian  wheat  growers  and  the 
Railroad  Trust,  whilst  the  latter — ^"  The  Pit" — is  a  story  of  a 
"  deal  "  in  the  Chicago  wheat  market. 

Shangaied 

Norris,  W.  E. 
Adrian  Vidal 

A  study  of  modern  life  and  character,  the  tale  dealing  with  the 
conjugal  infelicities  and  ordeals  experienced  by  a  young  novelist 
of  somewhat  shallow,  pleasure-loving  disposition,  and  the  eventual 
reconciliation  with  his  wife. 

Bachelor's  Blunder 

Billy  Bellevv 

The  hero  is  a  simple,  almost  silly,  young  man,  but  unselfish  and 
amiable,  who  eventually  meets  with  a  fate  too  heavy  for  his 
deserts. 

Chris 

The  love  affairs  of  a  young  heiress  and  her  trio  of  suitors,  ending 
pleasantly  after  a  series  of  doubts  and  perplexities. 

Clarissa  Furiosa  Deplorable  Affair,  A 

Countess  Radna,  The  Despotic  Lady,  The 

Credit  of  the  County,  The  Embarrassing  Orphan,  The 

Dancer  in  Yellow 
Fight  for  the  Crown,  The 

The  humours  of  political  differences  between  a  Conservative 
husband  and  a  Liberal  wife.  Makes  use  of  the  Home  Rule 
controversy  of  a  few  years  ago. 

Flower  of  the  Flock,  The 

A  rich  and  lu.xurious  young  man.  amiably  selfish,  reckless  and 
incompetent,  but  id(jlised  by  his  mother  and  bowed  down  to  by 
his  kin,  is  played  with  and  landed  by  a  rich  and  knowing  American 
widow,  and  thus  saved  from  the  consequences  of  an  act  that,  in 
a  more  responsible  person,  would  be  severely  reprobated. 


348  ^"^     ^ooA'=/oi>ars    Cifyranif. 


Noms»  W.  E. — continued. 

Giles  Ingilby 

Like  the  foregoing,  a  colourless,  unemotional  story,  depending 
for  interest  on  the  minute  drawing  of  characters  who  represent 
just  the  average  mediocrities  of  everyday  life. 

Heaps  of  Money 

His  Grace 

His  Own  Father 

A  story  of  the  ups  and  downs  of  courtship,  with  touches  of 
melodrama,  as  in  the  account  of  a  missing  father  who  reappears 
to  untie  the  tangled  skein  of  difficulties. 

Jack's  Father 

Lord  Leonard  the  Luckless 

A  society  novel,  purporting  to  be  extracts  from  tlie  diary  of  a 
nobleman  who  is  so  imfortunate  as  always  to  tell  the  truth. 

Mademoiselle  de  Mersac 

A  sober,  realistic  story,  with  careful  development  of  not  unusual 
types  of  French  and  Englisli  character,  faithful  pictures  of 
leisurely  life  and  social  doings  in  Algiers  and  England,  and 
word-landscapes  of  Algeria. 

Major  and  Minor 

The  old  story  of  a  man's  worthy  affection  for  a  girl  above  him  in 
wealth  and  station,  and  the  discomfiture  of  a  less  worthy  rival. 

Marcia 

The  married  life  of  two  people  who  are  not  sympathetic;  the 
man  honest,  but  hard,  and  the  woman  selfish  and  frivolous. 

Marietta's  Marriage 

Matrimony 

As  usual  with  this  writer,  a  slight  tale,  with  plenty  of  natural 
dialogue  and  character  drawing  of  the  external  kind. 

Matthew  Austin 

Misadventure 

jMiss  Shafto 

A  series  of  character  sketches,  talks,  love-making  and  the  like, 
together  forming  a  humorous  picture  of  the  ordinary  intercourse 
of  people  in  good  society. 

Miss  W'entworth's  Idea 
No  New  Thing 

The  fortunes  of  an  Anglo-Indian  lad,  of  a  sharp,  unscrupulous 
and  superficial  nature,  whose  character  is  a  contrast  to  the 
stronger  and  simpler  English  people  with  whom  he  is  brought 
into  contact,  e.g.,  the  kind-hearted  woman  who  befriends  him. 

Octave,  An 
Rogue,  The 

The  same  realistic  delineation  of  character  and  manners,  with  a 
strong  dash  of  comedy  and  good-natured  satire.  The  "  Rogue  " 
is  an  easy-going  fellow,  who  has  been  a  scamp,  and  tries  to  pass 
himself  off  as  a  decent  and  respectable  member  of  society. 

Thirlby  Hall 

Victim  of  Good  Luck,  A 

Widower,  The

Norway,  Arthur  H.     Parson  Peter 

A  romance  of  Devon.  Parson  Peter  is  a  representative  of  the 
old  order,  a  keen  sportsman  and  a  lover  of  the  sea,  consort  of 
smugglers  and  contemner  of  the  law,  a  generous  and  amiable 
personality.  The  operations  of  smugglers  and  preventives,  a 
little  love  romance,  and  an  undercurrent  of  tragedy,  furnish  the 
action.

Oliphant,  P.  L.     The  Little  Red  Fish 

The  heroine  is  an  Enghsh  baronet's  dauglitcr,  descendant  of  an 
Indian  Ranee,  who  has  in  her  possession  the  "Little  Red  Fish," 
which  is  the  venerated  symbol  of  authority  among  her  people. 
Going  to  India,  she  attempts  to  regain  the  throne  of  her  ancestors, 
but  is  foiled  by  the  man  whom  she  had  sought  to  compel  to  aid  her.

Oppenheim,  E.  Phillips 

As  a  Man  Lives  Man  and  His  Kingdom,  The 

Daughter  of  the  Marionis  Master  of  Men 

False  Evidence  Monk  of  Cruta,  A 

Great  Avs'akening,  The 

Millionaire  of  Yesterday,  A 

An  intricate  plot,  dealing  with  the  financial  schemes  of  a  man 
who  won  a  rich  concession  in  West  Africa,  but  who  deserted  his 
partner,  and  has  to  face  damaging  allegations  in  consequence. 

Mystery  of  Mr.  Bernard  Brown,  The 

Postmaster  of  Market  Deignton,  The 

Prince  of  Sinners,  A 

A  novel  dealing  with  political  and  social  subjects,  chiefly 
Protection  versus  Free  Trade.  The  "  Prince  of  Sinners  "  is  a 
nobleman  who  for  years  drained  the  dregs  of  evil,  and  who 
showed  its  effects  in  a  selfishness  and  cynicism  which  ultimately 
yielded  to  the  influence  of  his  son,  whose  career  as  a  rising 
young  solicitor  forms  the  main  interest  of  the  story. 

Survivor,  The 

The  "  Sin-vivor  "  is  an  ambitious  poet,  and  the  character  next  in 
importance  is  a  beautiful  siren,  who  would  fain  pose  as  his 
patroness.  The  plot  is  fertile  in  sensations  but  there  are  natural 
and  life-like  character-drawing,  and  sympathetic  pictures  of  nature 
amid  the  woods  and  the  fells. 

Traitors,  The 

World's  Great  Snare,  The 

A  romance  of  adventure  and  love  in  an  American  mining  camp 
and  in  England.

"Ouida**  (Louise  de  la  Ramec) 
Altruist,  An 
Ariadne 

A  "child  of  nature,"  innocent,  impulsive  and  profoundlj- 
passionate. 

Bimbi 

Cecil  Castlemaine's  Gage 

Chandos 

A  gushing  and  highly  coloured  romance,  with  a  glorified  libertine 
as  hero,  who  is  likened  to  Goethe  "  because  the  list  of  his  loves 
is  long."' 
Dog  of  Flanders ;  and  other  Stories,  A 

Story   of    a    dog,    whose    fortitude   and  affection   are  scathingly 
contrasted  with  the  cruelty  and  meanness  of  the  human  beings 
who  maltreat  him  as  a  poor  beast  of  burden. 
Don  Gesualdo  Guilderoy 

Folle  Farine  Held  in  Bondage 

Friendship  Idalia 

Frescoes  In  a  Winter  City 

In  Maremma 

Musa,  unknown  to  herself,  the  daughter  of  a  ferocious  brigand,  is 
again  a  child  of  nature,  who  makes  a  solitary  home  in  an 
Etruscan  tomb,  amid  the  wilds  of  Maremona.  She  comes  into 
contact  with  other  human  beings,  only  to  suffer  treachei'y, 
robberj',  and  martyrdom. 

La  Strega  Moths 

Massarenes  Othmar 

Pascarel 

Highly  coloured  descriptions  of  Morence  and  other  historic 
Italian  cities  and  lovely  landscapes.  Pascarel  is  a  born  actor, 
and  a  son  of  the  people,  who  plays  to  simple  villagers,  and  shims 
the  applause  of  cities. 

Pipistrello 


356  y3^^    MOo/c=/overs    dhrary. 

"Ouida**  (Louise  de  la  Ramee) — continued. 

Princess  Napraxine 

Puck 

The  autobiography  of  a  lap-dog,  horn  in  low  company,  and 
passing  tiirough  various  strata  of  society,  which  the  dog 
describes. 

Rainy  June;  and  Don  Gesualdo,  A 

Ruffino 

Santa  Barbara 

Signa 

Signa  is  another  foster-child  of  Nature,  a  boy  with  a  natural 
talent  for  art  and  music,  in  whose  mind  bold  and  striking  thoughts 
on  life  and  God  are  instinctive.  Son  of  a  girl  cast  out  by  her 
brothers,  he  is  brought  up  by  them  and  cruelly  treated  by  the 
younger,  a  time-serving  rascal. 

Strathmore 

Represents  fairly  a  large  section  of  her  novels — garish  and 
sensational  romances.  The  cold-hearted,  gifted,  and  immensch- 
strong  hero  falls,  at  last,  a  victim  to  the  charms  of  an  imscrupulous 
beauty,  to  whom  he  sacrifices  everything,  and  in  a  fit  of  misguided 
jealous}-  kills  his  bosom  friend  in  a  duel. 

Street  Dust 

Sj'rlin 

Tower  of  Taddeo 

Tricotrin 

"  Trictorin  "  is  an  agnostic  and  a  Bohemian,  but  his  virtues  are 
near  perfection  ;  he  is  handsome,  a  mus'cian  and  an  artist, 
learned  and  wise.  The  heroine  is  a  castaway  whom  he  rescues 
from  poverty. 

Two  Little  Wooden  Shoes 

An  innocent  little  Brabant  girl  is  petted  and  almost  loved  bj-  a 
rich  painter.  He  leaves  her  to  her  peasant  lover,  but  she, 
hearing  he  is  sick,  walks  on  foot  to  Paris  to  offer  succour  and 
love,  and  finding  him  sunk  in  debauchery,  flies  home  and  dies. 

Two  Offenders 

Under  Two  Flags 

The  author's  best  novel,  good  in  spite  of  the  extravagance  of  her 
descriptions  and  the  unreality  of  the  world  depicted.  The 
numerous  episodes  of  exciting  action,  fox-hunts  in  the  shires, 
battles  of  French  and  Kabyles  in  Algeria,  ai-e  well  described,  and 
the  book  is  full  of  imagination  of  an  oriental  kind. 

Village  Commune,  A 

A  modern  Italian  village  under  the  regime  of  liberty,  so-called. 
Practically,  the  syndic's  secretary,  a  political  adventurer,  rules 
the  community  as  an  irresponsible  autocrat,  grinding  the  face 
of  the  poor  with  impositions,  rooting  out  every  cherished  custom 
or  survival  of  the  past,  utilising  public  pretexts  to  serve  his 
own  ends. 

Wanda 

Waters  of  Edera,  The 

At  once  a  picture  of  the  squalid  inhabitants  of  an  Italian  district, 
and  a  denunciation  of  the  corruption  and  tyranny  of  the 
government.

Red  Rock 

A  story  of  the  Civil  War  and  of  Reconstruction,  centreing  in  the 
history  of  the  Red  Rock  Plantation  and  its  strange  vicissitudes 
t>f  ownership.  A  crowd  of  characters  ;  the  unhappy  Southern

to  which  the  Federal  Government  was  privy,  appear  in  an  odious 
light.

Parker,  Eric.     The  Sinner  and  the  Prohlem 

The  life  of  a  small  group  (jf  people  closely  observed  and  playfidly

Parker,  Sir  Gilbert 

Adventurer  of  the  North,  An 

Being  ii  continuation  of  the  personal  histories  of  '■  Pierre  and  his 
People,""  and  the  last  existing  records  of  Pretty  Pierre. 

Battle  of  the  Strong,  The 

Opens  wich  the  battle  of  Jersey  and  is  continued  into  tiie  great 
war  between  England  and  France  at  the  close  of  the  18th 
century,  with  episodic  glimpses  of  the  Revolution  and  the 
"Vendean  tragedy.  The  scene  is  chiefly  in  Jersey,  sometimes  in 
Brittany  ;  the  local  colour  is  carefully  studied,  and  the  admixture 
of  the  Jersey  patois  ma\'  be  compared  with  Cable's  broken 
English. 

Lane  that  Had  No  Turning,  The 

T\\ent\-six  stories  and  sketches  of  the  author's  compatriots,  the 
French-Canadians,  comic  and  tragic,  melodramatic  and  grotesque, 
in  their  \arious  motives.  The  first  and  longest  is  the  story  of  a 
seigneur  who  is  all  but  driven  mad  by  an  hereditary  malady,  and 
at  length  commits  a  murder.  The  principal  figure  however  is 
his  noble  wife,  wlio  shields  and  fosters  him  to  the  last. 

Mrs.  Falchion 

Pierre  and  His  People 

Stories  of  love  and  ad\enturc,  chiefly  from  the  Hudson  Bay 
Territory  ;  a  presentment  of  native  character  and  of  life  in 
Northern  Canada,  by  one  who  knows  it  intimately.  Pierre  is  an 
unpleasant  character,  finely  drawn. 

Pomp  of  the  Lavilettes,  The 

A  Canadian  village  story.  The  chief  motive  of  the  plot  is  the 
progress  of  an  incontinent  man,  and  his  penitence. 

Right  of  Way,  The 

The  most  striking  of  many  romantic  episodes  is  the  absolutely 
new  life  led  by  a  man  who  for  a  time  lost  his  memorj'  through 
an  accident.  "  Beauty  Steele,"  a  Montreal  barrister,  talented 
and  eloquent,  but  a  dipsomaniac,  belongs  to  a  favourite  type  of 
the  author's.  Contains  as  much  melodrama  as  tragedy,  and  as 
usual  many  incidents  are  irrelevant  ;  but  the  simple  life  and 
unsophisticated  characters  of  a  French-Canadian  village  are  well 
portrayed. 

Seats  of  the  Mighty,  The 

An  historical  romance,  embodying  several  chapters  of  the  great 
struggle,  culminating  in  the  battle  of  Quebec,  which  dispossessed 
France  and  enthroned  England  in  North  America.  Wolfe  and 
other  famous  characters  appear.

An  American  story  of  literary  life,  the  hero  being  a  novelist  with

Peacock,  F.  M.     From  Reveille  to  Lights  Out 
Peacock,  Thomas  Love.     Gryll  Grange 
Peake,  Elmore  Elliott.     The  Darlingtons 

Typical  of  the  latest  Amei'ican  realism,  honest  and  scrupulously 
faithful  to  everyday  life,  minutely  biographical,  often  tedious,  and 
not  much  concerned  with  literary  form.  A  railroad  centre 
in  the  West  is  the  scene,  and  the  principal  characters  are  the 
family  of  a  railroad  president  immersed  in  railway  business.  In 
one  son  an  interesting  case  of  dipsomania  is  studied  ;  but  the 
most  attractive  personalities  are  the  spirited,  self-reliant  daughter 
and  her  lover,  a  Methodist  minister,  a  "character  "  in  every  sense 
of  the  word.

Adventures  in  the  Franco-German  War,  particularly  during  the

House  Under  the  Sea,  The 

I  Crown  Thee  King 

Impregnable  City,  The 

An  imaginary  romance,  in  a  style  midway  between  Dumas  and 
Jules  Verne,  describing  a  series  of  thrilling  adventures  centreing 
in  the  fortunes  of  an  impregnable  city  built  on  a  Pacific  island. 
After  repulsing  the  French  and  Russian  fleets,  the  city  is 
captured  by  the  escaped  prisoners,  and  the  hero  is  rewarded 
with  his  bride. 

Iron  Pirate,  The 

An  exciting  story  of  wholesale  plundering  by  a  formidable  vessel, 
driven  by  electricity  and  manned  by  a  rough  and  motley  crew. 
A  sensational  story  in  Jules  \'erne's  style,  without  his  scientific 
imagination.

Ghost,  The 

The  earlier  chapters  of  this  story  are  laid  in  Australia,  the  latter 
in  London.  They  sketch  the  career  of  a  fair  plagiarist,  who, 
receiving  from  an  admirer  the  MS.  of  a  story,  and  afterwards 
believing  him  to  be  dead,  publishes  it  as  her  own.  He  reappears 
as  "The  Ghost"  and  brings  on  the  denouement.

Mrs.  Tregaskiss

love,  the  cenVral  figure  being  an  imaginative  woman  of  nervous 
temperament,  who  revolts  from  a  dull  and  commonplace  husband, 
a  bushman.  Bi-ings  out  forcibly  the  influence,  on  such  a  mind, 
of  the  dreary  environment  and  the  rude,  uncivilised  life. 
Nulma 

NCdma  is  an  innocent  and  beautiful  Australian  in  the  first  bloom 
of  womanhood,  who  is  brought  into  contact  with  a  group  of 
decadent  people  from  the  society  of  England,  and,  unfortunately, 
learns  to  love  an  unworthy  man.  The  circle  of  1-Znglish  gathered 
round  the  old  governor,  with  their  discreditable  intrigues,  are 
not  portrayed  attractively 

Other  Mrs.  Jacobs,  The  ' 

The  plot  developes  upon  the  consequences  of  a  couple  of  bigamous 
marriages,  unwittingly  entered  into.     It  is  unravelled  by  the  death 
of  one  of  the  husbands.

Poison  of  Asps,  The 

Tragic  without  the  least  sensational  incident,  the  three  persons

the  pettiness,  the  love  of  scandal  and  iiatred  of  the  unconventional, 
that  characterise  the  society  of  a  little  country  town. 

Voysey

liaison  between  \'oysey  and  a  sentimental,  discontented  wife. 
The  author's  metiiod  is  exhaustive  analysis  after  the  manner  of 
Henry  James,  while  the  franUncss  of  his  realism  in  certain  scenes 
suggests  Zola.  The  characters  are  dull,  poor-spirited  people,  who 
in  themsehes  have  little  interest.

Pugh,  Edwin 

Man  of  Straw,  The 
Mother — Sister 

The  heroine  of  this  story  of  low  life  is  a  staunch  and  self-reliant 
girl,  daughter  of  an  old  prize-fighter  and  gaol-bird,  who  is  more 
than  a  mother  to  her  brothers  and  sisters  ;  one  of  these  is  a 
ne'er-do-weel,  another  a  factory-girl  who  runs  away  with  a 
rascally  book-maker,  and  so  on. 

Street  in  Suburbia,  A 

Impressionist  sketches  of  people  and  manners  in  a  Cockney 
neighbourhood  ;    the  patois  faithfully  reproduced. 

Stumbling  Block,  The 

A  rather  unusual  love  story.  The  heroine  is  a  wild,  elfish  girl, 
whose  moods  and  tenses,  skilfully  worked  out,  cause  much 
perturbation  to  her  somewhat  dull  straightforward  lover.  The 
heroine's  character  is  consistently  sketched  from  beginning  to  end. 

Tony  Drum 

A  slight  story  founded  on  obserx'ations  of  London  characters. 
Tony  and  othei-  denizens  of  the  slums  are  drawn  realisticalh-  in 
their  squalid  sm-roundings,  but  the  sympathy  and  himioiu'  of  tlie 
author  show  also  what  is  pleasant  and  estimable  in  tlieir  life. 

Pugh,  E.  and  G.  Burchett.     The  Heritage 

A  gloomy  study  of  tlie  effects  of  hereditary  alcoholism  on  the 
several  members  of  a  family.  Thougli  its  influence  acts  in  diverse 
ways,  the  taint  in  the  blood  is  shown  to  be  the  overmastering 
agent  of  their  destinies,  and  tlie  philosophy  of  the  book  is  very 
pessimistic.

Below  the  Salt 

Kindly  and  discerning  sl<etclies  of  life  below-stairs,  mostly 
farcical,  but  sometimes  of  a  depressing  pathos. 

George  Mandeville's  Husband 

Pure  comed\-  up  to  a  certain  point,  where  it  turns  unexpectedly 
into  doleful  tragedy.  There  is  satire  on  some  recent  features  of 
society  ;  the  egotistic  and  empty  authoress  who  terrorises  her 
spouse  with  her  vanity  and  absurd  belief  in  her  mission,  perhaps 
drawn  from  life. 

New  JMoon,  The 

A  pathetic  version  of  the  not  unhackneyed  situation  where  a 
clever  man,  married  prematurely  to  a  silly  wife,  finds,  too  late,  a 
woman  he  can  love.  Botii  the  lovers  are  strong  and  self-restrained, 
but  their  struggle  to  be  true  to  their  principles  is  almost  too 
great  for  human  nature.  However,  a  tragic  accident  cuts  the 
Gordian  knot. 

Open  Question,  The:    a  Tale  of  Two  Temperaments 

A  study  of  character  and  heredit)-,  and  a  sad  love  story.  The 
dramatic  problem  arises  from  the  love  of  two  cousins  who  are 
each  marked  out  as  victims  to  consumption.  Life  and  Society 
in  the  defeated  Southern  States  is  delineated,  and  Mrs.  Gano, 
though  a  subordinate  character,  is  one  of  those  racy  creations 
with  whom  one's  memories  of  a  book  often  become  identified. 

Rainbow  Feather,  The  ...  ...  Fergus  Hume

By  Berwen  Banks 

Comprises  idyllic  pictures  of  the  Welsh  people,  w'ith  some  humour 
in  the  portraiture.  The  plot  entails  some  melodramatic  interest 
in  which  persons  of  a  different  class  are  involved.

Garthowen 

The  heroine  is  a  waif  saved  from  a  shipwreck  through  the  warn-

storms  and  simshine  of  the  coast,  form  a  large  part  of  the

Randal,  John 

Aunt  Bethia's  Button 

Belonging  to  the  Hawley  family  are  three  valuable  ruby  and 
diamond  buttons,  one  of  which  is  stolen  ;  its  recovery  is 
complicated  by  the  existence  of  the  two  others,  but  after  giving 
rise  to  some  interesting  scenes,  it  is  restored  to  its  rightful  owner. 

Pacifico 

An  "  up-to-date"  romance,  the  scene  being  an  island  republic  in 
the  Ionian  Sea,  which  has  its  extradition  treaty  and  financial 
schemes  of  an  extravagant  kind  furnishing  the  motives  ;  desperate 
adventures,  and  picturesque  personages  are  woven  into  an 
ingenious  narrative.

Rawson,  Mrs.  Stepney.  A  Lady  of  the  Regency 

The  heroine  of  the  love  roninnce,  which  ahnost  forms  a  subsidiary 
plot  to  the  historical  study,  is  an  affectionate  servant  of  Queen 
Caroline  ;  and  here  we  have  a  complete  recital  of  the  unhappy 
Queen's  life  and  misfortunes  down  to  her  death,  with  pictures  of

the  cause  celebrc.

Charity  Chance 

.4  Io\e  comedy  enacted  in  a  little  Somersetshire  watering-place 
on  tlic  Bristol  Channel. 

Fortune's  Darling 

Love  and  a  Quiet  Life 

Somerset  idylls,  delineating  the  bucolic  life  of  mid-Somerset  half 
a  century  ago.  The  dialect  is  woven  not  only  into  the  talk  but 
into  the  narrative,  for  artistic  effect. 

No  Soul  Above  Money 

Village  life  and  character  in  the  reign  of  Queen  Anne.  A  tragic 
story,  of  which  ttie  catastrophe  is  the  murder  of  a  brother, 
followed  by  a  terrible  expiation  and  unutterable  sorrow  for  the 
bereaved  mother. 

Tryphena  in  Love 

A  fanciful,  but  simple  idyll  of  agricultural  life  in  an  old-world 
village.  Mrs.  Pettigrew-  is  a  milder  Mrs.  Poyser,  and  the 
tomboyish,  tender-hearted  Tryphena,  with  her  alleged  lack  of 
"feelings,"  is  delicately  contrasted  with  the  rich  young  lady  who 
captivates  her  lover  without  meaning  it.

Cloister  and  the  Hearth,  The 

A  huge  romance  of  the  middle  ages,  which,  by  taking  the  hero 
from  the  Netherlands  through  Germany  and  France  to  Italy  and 
Rome,  depicts  the  state  of  all  tiiese  countries.  Based  on  an 
exhaustive  study  of  mediaeval  history  and  literature;  a  vivid 
reconstruction  of  the  whole  life  of  the  time.

Riddell,  Mrs.  J.  H,— continued. 
Race  for  Wealth,  The 

A  novel  based  essentially  on  the  study  of  character  and  of  the 
conduct  of  life.  The  race  for  wealth  is  personified  in  Forbes  and 
Barbour,  the  one  a  kind,  affectionate  and  upright  man,  who 
advances  slowly  to  moderate  success,  the  other  a  strong  and 
rapid  man,  who  meets  with  disaster  before  the  goal  is  won. 
Their  love  matters  are  likewise  dealt  with — Forbes'  long-repressed 
affection  for  his  friend's  wife,  a  dangerous  situation  treated 
harmlessly,  the  other's  lawless  and  calamitous  surrender  to 
passion. 

Rich  Husband,  The  Struggle  for  Fame,  A 

Rich  Man's  Daughter,  A  Susan  Drummond

Breaker  of  Laws,  A 

A  sympathetic  studj-  of  Cockney  character  ;  tiie  hero,  a  rascal 
of  an  engaging  kind,  good-humoured,  and  full  of  mother-wit  ;

simple  Devonshire  lass ;  but  he  goes  astray  again  to  his  own 
undoing,  the  end  being  deeplj-  tragic.

London  Only 

Sympathetic  sketches  of  the  most  commonplace  varieties  of  life 
in  London  ;  the  life  of  the  streets,  the  shops,  the  middle-class  and 
lower-class  homes. 

Mord  Em'ly 

Slum  life  in  South  London  as  seen  by  a  facetious  observer,  wlio 
views  everything  through  a  rose-coloured  atmosphere  of  optimism. 

Outside  the  Radius 

Fifteen  "Stories  of  a  London  Suburb,"  portraying  middle-class 
people,  domestic  serv^ants,  etc.  ;  the  themes  mos^tly  light  and 
facetious.  Full  of  observation  of  character  and  manners,  and 
animated  by  a  cheerful  optimism. 

Son  of  the  State,  A 

The  life  of  a  joung  street  Arab  from  Hoxton,  who  goes  through  a 
reformatory,  training-ship,  etc.,  dcNcloping  in  due  course  into  a 
gallant  sailor.

Wreck  of  the  "Grosvenor,"  The 

A  nautical  story  by  an  ex-officer  in  the  mercantile  marine.       It 
may  be  taUen  as  the  type  of  a  numerous  series  by  the  author,

Russian  Vagabond,  A  ...  ...  ...         Fred  Whishaw

Rutherford,  Mark  (William  Hall  White) 
Clara  Hopgood 

England  and  Italy  fifty  years  ago  ;  Mazzini  is  a  minor  character. 
Some  of  the  author's  more  heterodox  opinions  on  ethics  and 
religion  are  propounded  by  means  of  c^iaracter- development 
and  incident.  In  Baruch  Cohen,  for  example,  the  theology  has 
advanced  into  a  phase  of  Pantheism,  and  among  the  political 
theorists  are  many  disbelievers  in  Christianity.  The  heroine 
bears  shame  rather  than  marry  the  unworthy  lover  who  has 
betrayed  her,  and  Baruch,  the  believer  in  free  love,  eventually 
gives  a  home  to  her  and  her  child. 
Revolution  in  Tanner's  Lane,  The 

A  fragmentary  story,  beginning  in  1814  and  ending  in  the  forties, 
a  period  when  Radicalism  was  a  crime.  The  change  of  ministers 
in  an  obscure  dissenting  chapel,  and  the  intrigues  and  abortive 
revolts  that  ensue,  are  the  basis  of  a  huge  part  of  the  story; 
then  follow  more  poignant  interests  arising  from  the  gradual 
disillusionment  and  estrangement  of  newly  married  people.  But 
religious  and  ethical  questions,  the  government  of  the  universe, 
the  meaning  of  human  suffei'ing,  faith  and  infidelity,  are  the 
topics,  and  these  are  illustrated  in  the  lives  of  the  Socialist 
Colman  and  of  other  persons,  who  have  suffered  intolerable 
wrongs  from   society. 

Rutledge        ...  ...  ...  ...  S.  Harris 

Rynd,  Evelyne  Elsye 

Mrs.  Green 

Mrs.  Green  is  an  entertainingold  charwoman,  quite  a  "character," 
who,  in  a  series  of  conversations,  propounds  her  views  of  things  in 
general,  and  sketches  in  forcible  colours  tlie  characters  of  friends 
and  acquaintances.  She  is  obviously  a  relative  of  Mrs.  Gamp, 
and  a  similar  Cockney  dialect  seasons  her  gossip. 

Riggleses  and  Others,  The 

A  series  of  humorous  sketches  of  country  life  ;  the  autlior  allows 
the  lights  and  shades  of  fancy  to  play  upon  the  little  dramas  of  the 
village  world  witli  pleasing  elfect.

Sand,  George  (Madame  Dudevant) 

Consuelo 

Opens  in  Venice,  where  the  heroine  is  introduced  as  a  child  of  the 
streets,  yet  a  girl  of  noble  heart,  chaste  instincts,  and  great 
artistic  powers.  Her  career  borders  on  the  marvellous  ;  loving 
and  beloved  by  a  young  Count,  she  weds  him,  onl\-  to  be  divorced 
by  death,  a  wife  only  in  name.  Ne.xt  we  find  her  at  tiie  Court  of 
Frederick  II.,  and  then  involved  in  a  series  of  adventures  ; 
mysteries,  fantastic  journeys,  imprisonments,  abduction,  escapes, 
follow  each  other  with  startling  rapidity  ;  the  book  closes  with  an 
allegorical  vision  of  humanitarian  theosophy.  "The  Countess  of 
Rudolstadt"  is  the  sequel  to  this  work. 

Countess  of  Rudolstadt 

Mauprat 

A  story  of  provincial  aristocracy  in  the  latter  half  of  the  18th 
century.  A  refined  girl  married  to  a  boorish  cousin,  sets  herself 
the  task  of  civilising  him,  and  succeeds  in  making  him  an 
estimable  man  by  drawing  out  his  better  feelings.  The  situation 
is  handled  in  a  forcible  manner,  and  the  book  is  full  of  ideas  on  life.

Golden  Rapids  of  High  Life,  The 

A    story  of    diplomatic    life    at    the    time    of    the    late    Hispano- 
American  war,  describing  a  plot  to  ruin  a  Yankee  diplomat  with  a 
roving  commission,  and  so  rob  him  of  his  heiress  lover;    the  plot 
temporarily  succeeded,  but  finally  failed.

Schreiner,  Olive  (**  Ralph  Iron  ") 

Dreams 

Story  of  an  African  Farm,  The 

A  very  unconventional  story.  Amid  the  solitary  plains  of  South 
Africa,  on  an  ostrich  farm,  a  group  of  singular  and  striking 
characters  have  to  work  out  for  themselves  the  urgent  problems 
of  religion,  of  sex,  and  of  woman's  mission.  An  unhappy  story, 
semi-autobiographical,  the  plaintive  utterance  of  a  lonely  soul 
puzzled  by  the  riddle  of  the  imiverse.  Very  outspoken  in  its 
criticisms  of  life  and  aspirations  towards  a  new  order. 

Trooper  Peter  Halket  of  Mashonaland 

A  tract  on  the  black  and  white  problem  in  South  Africa,  in  which 
Jesus  Christ,  coming  in  the  flesh  to  Mashonaland,  is  the  principal 
spokesman.

Twillford  Mystery,  The 

A  detective  story.  Five  friends  meet  together  and  discuss  murder, 
illustrating  by  an  example.  One  of  them  is  afterwards  murdered 
by  another  in  such  a  waj'  as  to  throw  suspicion  upon  each  of  the 
others  in  turn.

Confounding  of  Camelia,  The 

A  love  comedy,  of  which  the  principal  motive  is  the  evolution  of 
a  girl's  character  from  flagrant  egoism  to  sincerity  and  humility  : 
very  frank  in  its  analysis  of  feminine  foibles,  and  containing 
some  strong  emotional  situations  and  humorous  dialogue.

Sergeant,  Adeline 

Anthea's  Way 

Self-sacrifice  was  "Anthea's  way,"  and  in  the  pursuit  of  the 
happiness  of  others  she  found  at  last  the  complete  fulfilment  of 
her  own.     A  pleasant  story. 

Barbara's  Money 

Beneath  the  Veil 

The  plot  developes  the  characters  of  two  half-sisters,  the  elder 
bad,  the  younger  good.  The  former,  however,  substituted  herself 
as  bride  at  the  latter's  wedding,  in  order  to  prevent  her  from 
marrying  a  man  she  did  not  love. 

Blake  of  Oriel 

The  story  of  a  university  don,  outwardly  a  cultured  scholar  and 
polished  gentleman,  but  really  a  cad  and  a  thief,  who  is  successful 
in  his  evil  practices  to  the  last,  not  being  found  out  till  after 
his  death. 

Caspar  Brooke's  Daughter 

Claim  of  Anthony  Lockhart,  The

Sergeant,  Adeline — continued. 
Common  Lot,  The 

Typicnl  of  tliis  prolific  author  :  quiet,  domestic  memoirs,  chiefly 
feminine  in  interest,  saturated  with  pious  belief  in  a  poetic  justice 
enforced  b\-  Providence.  How  a  stronj^.  good-natured  girl  forsakes 
her  ambitions  and  accepts  the  common  lot  in  maintaining  a  family 
of  ill-conditioned  and  ungrateful  relatives,  being  rewarded  in  the 
end  by  the  love  of  a  wortliy  man. 
Conscience  of  Gilbert  Pollard,  The 

An  average  specimen  of  the  author's  sentimental  domestic  fiction. 
The  plot  hangs  on  an  understanding,  omitted  in  his  father's  will, 
that   Gilbert    should  give   his  scapegrace   brother  a  considerable 
share  of  the  old  man's  monej'.     Gilbert  feels  justified  in  refusing 
to  do  so. 
Dau nay's  Tower 
Doctor  Endicott's  Experiment 
Esther  Denison 

An  earnest  story,  aiming  at  moral  edification,  and  belonging  to 
the  unambitious  class  of  domestic  fiction,  a  girl  coming  between 
two  lovers  furnishing  the   text. 
Future  of  Phyllis  In  the  Wilderness 

Great  Lady,  A  In  Vallombrosa

Love  Story  of  Margaret  Wynne,  The 

Margaret  loyally  believes  in  Bayard's  truth  and  innocence,  in  spite 
of  accusations  and  appearances;    and  she  is  justified  in  the  end. 
Love  That  Overcame,  The 

Describes   how  "by   simple   skill   of   loving   to  the  end,"  a  maid 
enabled  a  man  to  conquer  the  gambling  habit,  and  was  herself 
prevented  from  growing  hard,  stern  and  unwomanly.

Jose

A  doctor,   who   claims   prophetic   knowledge,   finds   a  dupe   who

speculations.     The  doctor's  greed  leads  him  to  murder  one  of  his 
victims,  and  also  his  accomplice,  whom   he  mistook  for  the  lover 
of  his  ward.

Cold  Steel 

A  rapid  melodramatic  story  of  violent  action,  fighting,  love- 
making,  and  intrigue  in  the  England  of  Henry  Vlll.  That  jovial 
monarch  and  Anne  Bolcjn  appear,  but  the  interest  is  not 
particularly  historical. 

Contraband  of  War 

Lord  of  the  Sea,  The 

Another  impossible  romance  in  which  modern  inventions  ana 
scientific  marvels  are  dexterously  used  for  sensational  effect. 
The  hero  is  a  Jewish  Napoleon,  who  undertakes  to  subjugate  the 
sea,  as  the  land  is  subjugated  under  the  present  regime.  The 
events  b\'  which  he  makes  himself  a  mighty  ruler  in  the  earth, 
are  revealed  with  vigorous,  if  often  crude,  realism. 

]\Ian  Stealers,  The 

A  frankly  sensational  narrative  of  a  French  plot  to  kidnap  the 
Duke  of  Wellington  and  avenge  the  exile  of  Napoleon;  impossible 
events  simulating  truth  by  sheer  rapidity  of  narrative. 

Prince  Zaleski  Shapes  in  the  Fire 

Purple  Cloud,  The  Weird  o'  It 

Yellow  Danger,  The 

A  sensational  romance  of  the  near  future  and  a  glorification  of 
jingoism  on  the  largest  scale.  The  Japanese  and  Chinese  set  the 
nations  of  Europe  by  the  ears  with  the  object  of  aggrandising 
themselves  when  their  enemies  are  exhausted.  Battles  and  sea- 
fights  of  a  monstrous  and  sanguinary  kind  succeed  each  other 
with  bewildering  rapidit}'. 

Shieldt  A,     Squire  of  Wandales 

Shield  of  His  Honour...  ...  Richard  Henry  Savage 

Ship  of  Stars,  The         ...  ...  A.  T.  Ouiller-Couch 

Shipley,  M.  E.     Philippa  in  Youth  and  Middle  Age 

Ship's  Adventure,  The  ...  ...    W.  Clark  Russell 

Ships  that  Pass  in  the  Night,  The  Beatrice  Harraden 

Shipton,  Helen 

Strong  God  Circumstance,  The 

The  idea  is  to  show  several  characters  brought  to  severe  tests  of 
conduct  by  circumstances  beyond  their  own  control.  Unselfish- 
ness is  a  prevalent  trait  of  the  characters,  who  are  chiefl)- 
university  people,  tutors  and  students,  and  their  respective  lady- 
loves. 

Touchstone,  The 


f/^ook-'/oifens    Ui/jrctrif.  409 


Shirley  ...  ...  ...  ...    Charlotte  Bronte 

Shireen  and  Her  Friends  ...  ...       Gordon  Stables 

vShoes  of  Fortune,  The  ...  ...  Neil  Munro 

Shorthouse,  Joseph  Henry 

Blanche,  Lady  Falaise 

Countess  Eve,  The 

A  spiritual  romance,  in  whicli  the  evil  one,  who  appears  visibly, 
is  foiled  in  his  attempt  to  seduce  two  human  beings  ;  a  seaixhing 
study  of  human  infirmities  and  pronencss  to  temptation. 

John  Inglesant 

A  philosophical  romance,  of  which  the  hero  was  a  servant  of 
Charles  I.,  and  afterwards  acts  as  go-between  to  the  Anglican 
and  Romanist  ecclesiastical  parties.  Historical  events  are 
subordinate  to  the  subjective  biograph)-.  In  .Vlolincs  and  the 
Quietists,  Inglesant  becomes  acquainted  with  the  mysticism  that 
was  a  common  philosopliy  to  certain  Romanists  and  Anglicans  ; 
and  on  the  exposition  of  this  doctrine,  which  reproduced  itself  in 
the  Tractarian  movement  of  our  own  times,  the  author  con- 
centrates his  eloquence. 

Little  Schoolmaster  Mark,  The 

Borrows  material  from  H.  Jung-Stilling's  autobiography.  Very 
mystical. 

Sir  Percival 

A  storj-,  ostensibly  related  by  a  woman,  of  a  19th  century  seeker 
after  the  Grail,  an  idealised  young  Englishman,  with  delineations 
of  an  idealised  and  beautiful  societ)-. 

Teacher  of  the  Violin,  A 

Short  stories  embodying  similar  themes  of  spiritual  aspiration, 
refined  ideals  of  conduct,  and  the  ennobling  influences  of  the 
chivalrous  past.  The  title-story  is  about  a  music-loving  boy  in 
Germany  (1787)  and  his  communings  with  Nature.  Of  the  four 
others,  the  "  Marquis  de  Saint  Pelaye,"  is  thoroughly 
characteristic  of  the  author.

Inner  Shrine,  The 

Portraiture  of  some  various  characters,  an  innocent  and  high- 
minded  girl,  a  very  vulgar  and  pretentious  married  couple,  a  bright 
little  boy,  and  others.  Placed  in  a  difficult  situation,  this 
engay.ing'  type  of  loyal  girlhood,  loved  by  a  married  man,  and 
compromised  by  the  two  scandal-mongers,  suffers  no  loss  of 
dignity  or  of  truth  to  the  inner  shrine.

A  gloomy  story,  with  some  melodramatic  scenes.  A  mad  uncle,

liim  is  onlj-  friendship,  and  when  slie  wakes  up  out  of  the  morbid,

man.  The  scene  is  laid  in  the  poor  quarters  of  East  London.

Her  Ladyship 

A  story  of  the  early  part  of  last  century  founded  on  the 
mesalliance  of  a  noble  woman  with  a  villain.  Their  son  takes 
after  the  father,  and  with  his  aid  seeks  to  prove  his  title  to  a 
peerage,  but  fails,  the  father  at  last  being  shot  as  a  highwayman.

Sport  of  Chance,  The 

Of  three  young  lady  friends,  one,  an  heiress,  sinks  her  identity 
for  a  year  and  persuades  another  to  impersonate  her,  after  which 
she  loses  her  memory  by  an  accident.  Tlie  storj'  describes  tiicir 
adventures  in  Cupid's  domain  until  two  are  happily  married  and 
the  third  left  a  stranded  old  maid.
