Spender,  E.     The  Law  Breakers. 

A  story  mainly  of  life  in  an  English  provincial  town.  The  law 
breakers  are  an  English  girl,  who,  to  benefit  her  aunt,  destroyed 
her  grandmother's  will,  and  a  young  Italian  officer,  who,  in 
defence  of  a  peasant  girl,  fought  a  duel.

Flower  of  Forgiveness,  The 

Sixteen  short  stories  and  siietches  of  the  life,  habits  and 
spiritualistic  lore  of  the  natives  of  India,  written  with  touches  of 
S}m pathetic  mjsticism,  with  humour  and  with  pathos. 

From  the  Five  Rivers 

Eij^ht  short  stories  of  Hindu  and  iMoiiammedan  life  in  tiie  Punjab, 
intimate  delineation  of  rural  conditions,  Kastern  customs,  liabits, 
ideas  and  prejudices,  lool<ed  at,  as  far  as  possible,  tlirouj^li 
liastern  eyes.      Some  songs  are  added. 

Hosts  of  the  Lord,  The 

A  similar  romance,  located  principidly  in  an  old  dynastic  city  of 
India,  and  dealing  seriously  with  the  great  problem  of  "the 
mutual  assimilation  of  East  and  West  without  injury  to  either." 
The  interests  and  the  characters  are  as  multitudinous  as  ever; 
Indians,  Anglo-Indians,  and  people  of  mixed  blood  ;  soldiers,  civil 
officials,  missionaries  (Protestant  and  Roman  Catholic),  and 
natives  of  all  grades  and  conditions  are  engaged  in  the  complex 
action,  the  central  theme  being  the  ill-starred  love  of  an 
Englishman  for  a  half-Indian  Begum,  v.hicli  brings  him  into 
collision  with  a  turbulent  young  Mohammedan. 

In  the  Guardianship  of  God 

In  the  Permanent  Way 

Miscellaneous  stories  and  sketches  of  life  in  India,  native  and 
European  ;    the  characters  being  types  rathei-  tlian  living  people. 

In  the  Tideway 

Miss  Stuart's  Legacy 

Anglo-Indian  life.  Belle  Stuart's  love  affairs  and  lier  m.trriage  in 
mistaivc  form  the  dramatic  interest,  with  studies  of  native  life  and 
character,  the  Mohammendan  soldier,  the  Hindu  usurer,  the 
barbarous  Patlian,  etc. 


420  J/T^^     ^oo/i=/ooers    dhnary. 


Steel,  Mrs.  ^Iora  Annie — continued. 
On  the  Face  of  the  Waters 

An  elaborate  historical  storj'  of  the  Indian  Mutinj'  ;  minutely 
accurate,  fiction  never  interfering  with  fact.  Full  of  terrible 
scenes,  like  the  massacre  at  Merut,  and  the  storming  of  the  Delhi 
gate  ;  with  careful  studies  of  various  native  types,  and  portraits 
of  English  officers  and  civilians.  Pays  much  attention  to  sex 
problems,  Ibsenism  and  other  modern  fashions. 

Potter's  Thtimb,  The 

Anglo-Indian  society  and  native  life.  The  plot  liinges  on  the 
efforts  of  an  Indian  inagnate  to  get  possession  of  tlie  keys  of  the 
canal  sluice-gates  by  the  agency  of  an  unprincipled  English 
woman  ;  the  brave  joung  custodian  of  the  keys  is  brought  to 
despair  and  death.  The  native  characters  are  drawn  with 
intimate  knowledge. 

Red  Rowans 

A  Scottish  novel,  comprising  good  specimens  of  the  writer's 
character-drawing,  and  descriptions  of  the  west  Highland  scenery, 
with  a  tragic  catastrophe. 

Tales  of  the  Punjaub 

A  collection  of  folk-tales  in  prose  and  verse,  collected  with  care 
and  industry. 

Voices  in  the  Night 

A  thread  of  plot  is  concerned  with  the  good  and  evil  fortunes  of  a 
young  Englishman  in  the  Indian  Civil  Service,  and  his  love  for  a 
very  modern  governess.  More  important  are  the  intimate  studies 
of  native  life,  descriptions  of  the  plague,  thoughts  on  caste, 
sanitation,  intercourse  of  soldiers  and  natives,  and  other  political 
and  social  problems.  The  episodes  illustrating  these  are  some- 
times very  sensational.

Adventures  of  Captain  Horn,  The 

Story  of  adventures  met  with  in  a  quest  for  a  treasure  of  the 
Incas.       Much  of  the  interest  depends  on  the  eharacterdrawing. 

Afield  and  Afloat 

Eleven  short  stories  characteristic  of  .VI i-.  StocUton  in  their 
mi.xture  of  fantasy  and  realism.  Three  ai-e  ghost  stories.  These 
and  the  others  sketch  American  characters  and  manners  in  a  life- 
like way.  The  "  Bullcr-Peddington  Compact"  puts  before  us  a 
pair  of  crochetj'  friends,  and  the  mule  story  gives  a  graphic 
picture  of  old  New  Orleans.

Stockton,  Frank  R. — continued. 

Associate  Hermits,  Tiie 

An  animated  comed\-  representing  many  droll  adventures  of  an 
elderly  couple  and  a  pretty  girl  who  spend  a  holiday  camping  out. 

Bicycle  of  Cathay,  A 

An  amusing  story  of  a  village  schoolmaster  who  goes  on  a 
cycling  tour  and  falls  in  love  with  every  girl  he  meets,  with  other 
amusing  adventures.  Slightly  resembles  Mr.  Wells'  "  Wheels 
of  Chanci." 

Casting  Away  of  Mrs.  Leeks,  The 

The  wildly  absurd  Crusoe  experiences  of  two  prosaic  matrons 
from  New  England  ;  their  methodical  life  on  a  desert  isle,  where 
they  l<eep  house  as  calmlj-  and  composedly  as  if  they  were  at 
home,  is  set  forth  in  a  matter-of-fact  manner  that  is  verj-  divert- 
ing.     It  reads  like  a  parody  of  Defoe. 

Girl  at  Cobhurst,  The 

Great  Stone  of  Sardis,  The 

Hundredth  Man,  The 

A  combination  of  fun  and  seriousness  ;  two  narratives  conjoined — 
one  farcical,  being  concerned  with  a  waiters'  strike  in  a  New- 
York  restaurant,  the  other,  in  places  very  pathetic,  dealing  with 
a  social  theorist's  deliberate  and  effectual  attempt  to  break  off  an 
engagement  between  a  pretty  girl  and  a  man  he  thinks  unworthy 
of  her. 

John  Gayther's  Garden 

A  volume  of  short  stories,  various  and  interesting,  told  in  "  John 
Gayther's  Garden,"  by  several  narrators,  and  ranging  from 
adventure  to  scientific  romance. 

Lady  or  the  Tiger 

Late  Mrs.  Ntill,  The 

Another  absurd  story,  told  with  a  matter-of-fact  gravity  that 
ignores  the  illogical  nature  of  the  characters  and  tlieir  relations 
to  each  other.  The  parts  dealing  with  negro  life  are  most 
spontaneous  in  their  humour,  and  "  Aunt  Patsy  "  and  the 
"  Jerusalem  Jump  "  are  inimitable. 

Mrs.  Cliff's  Yacht 

Deals  with  the  fate  of  that  portion  of  the  treasure  which  fell  to 
the  Peruvian  Government  and  to  Mrs.  Cliff.  This  New  England 
woman  with  her  matter-of-fact  wajs  recalls  the  humorous 
incongruities  of  Mrs.  Leeks  and  Mrs.  Aleshine. 

Pomona's  Travels 

The  late  handmaid  of  the  Rudder  Grangers  marries  and  goes  on 
a  wedding  trip  through  England  and  Scotland.  This  is  her 
journal,  and  she  hits  off  the  c'laracters  of  places  and  peoples 
with  shrewdness  and  humour,  her  point  of  view  being  a  perennial 
source  of  amusement. 

ivudder  Grange 

The  humorous  experiences  of  a  joung  married  pair,  who  begin 
housekeeping  in  a  derelict  barge,  and  retain  the  name  of  Rudder 
Grange  when  they  move  to  a  more  stable  dwelling.  These  two 
and  their  servant  Pomona,  and  the  lodger,  are  just  average 
characters  faithfully  drawn,  and  the  drollery  arises  fron  unstrained 
and  ordinary  situations. 

Rudder  Grangers  Abroad,  The

Autobiography  of  a  Boy 

"Being  passages  selected  by  his  friend." 
ironical  portrait  of  the  fin-de-siecle  young  man,  non-moral, 
devoted  to  the  gospel  of  self-indulgence.  In  the  various  episodes 
of  college  life  and  social  intercourse  he  writes  himself  down  a  fool.

Trials  of  the  Bantocks,  The 

A  satire  on  the  snobs  and  vulgarisms  of  moneyed  society- 
Bantock  is  a  banker,  who,  with  a  correct  and  highly  respectable 
family,  is  afflicted  by  parasitical  friends  and  other  social  plagues.

Man  of  the  Moors,  A 

A  tale  of  love  and  tragedy,  with  a  good  deal  of  sensation  and 
some  gruesome  scenes.  The  author  is  a  lover  of  the  lonely 
moorlands  of  YorUshire,  and  their  wild,  beautiful  and  solemn 
aspects  are  reflected  in  every  chapter.  The  Bronte  neigiibour- 
hood  of  40  years  ago  is  largel)-  dealt  with,  and  the  dialect  made 
use  of.

Tarkington,    Booth 

Gentleman  from  Indiana,  The 

A  local  book,  the  skies  and  landscapes  of  Indiana  being  described 
with  as  mucii  enjoyment  as  are  the  townsfolk  of  Platville.  The 
hero  wages  relentless  warfare  against  a  gang  of  scoundrels, 
survivals  of  the  age  of  lawlessness.  Sentimental  chapters 
alternate  with  humorous  scenes  of  life  in  the  county-town. 

Monsieur  Beaucaire 

A  little  drama  of  intrigue  laid  in  Bath  during  the  Beau  Nash 
regime  in  the  middle  of  the  18th  century.  Complications  arise 
from  a  French  nobleman's  masquerading  as  a  barber  and  falling 
in  love  with  an  aristocratic  Englishwoman.

Taunton,  W.  T.     Silent  Dominion 

A  girl,  five  years  after  marriage,  meets  two  men,  an  Hnglisliman

eerie  qualities  of  his  race  strongly  developed.

Thackeray,  Miss 
Bluebeard's  Keys 

A  series  of  long  stories  or  novelettes  ;  they  are  essentially  stories 
of  character,  and  are  new  illustrations  of  old  fables  and  fairy 
tales.  The  title-story,  e.g.,  adopts  a  well-known  motive.  An 
English  famil}'  in  Rome  and  an  Italian  marquis  are  the  dramatis 
personcE.  He  loves  the  jounger  daughter  passionately,  but  there 
is  a  secret  crime  in  his  past  over  which  he  broods  with  penitence 
and  remorse,  and  the  girl  accidentally  discovers  the  secret,  with 
momentous  consequences. 

Miss  Angel 

A  novel  of  manners,  portraying  18th  century  society  ;  Angelica 
Kauffman  is  the  leading  character  and  Sir  Joshua  Reynolds  a 
minor  character.  It  shows  the  same  delicate  and  thoughtful 
treatment  of  motive  and  conduct. 

Mrs.  Dymond 

A  gentle,  sweet-natured  woman,  whose  life  has  little  of  the 
eventful,  but  appeals  for  sympathj-  by  its  quiet  goodness  and 
unselfishness  ;  the  scene  is  France  during  the  adverse  months  of 
the  Franco-German  war,  which  is  set  before  us  as  the  women 
and  children  beheld  it. 


f^ook'^lovers    t.ibrary. 


433 


Thackeray,  Miss — cont'nmed. 
Old  Kensington 

Revelation  of  character  forms  its  gist,  plot  and  incideni  arc 
subordinate  ;  full  of  musings  on  life,  arising  out  of  the  action  like 
the  thoughts  of  an  observer  of  actual  events.  Robert  Henley  is 
a  very  complete  and  scathing  study  of  the  genus  prig  ;  the  heroine 
a  gentle,  poetical  nature,  whose  growth  is  traced  from  youth  up. 
The  whole  boolv  is  a  good  example  of  the  deep  human  feeling  that 
pervades  this  author's  work. 

Village  on  the  Cliff,  The 

Expresses  feelingly  the  sadness  of  those  sensitive  natures  con- 
demned bj-  fate  to  a  cheerless  and  purposeless  existence.  The 
story  of  a  poor  little  governess,  who  loves  a  man  that  cares  not 
for  her,  and  marries  from  mistaken  motives  one  who  is  not  her 
true  mate.  Her  girlish  hopes  and  fears,  her  awakening  to  tlic 
consciousness  of  her  error,  and  her  womanly  conquest  of  her 
passion,  are  related  with  delicate  sympathy.

Thompson,  Maurice 

Alice  of  Old  Vincennes 

Vincennes  is  an  old  town  of  French  Indiana,  and  into  this  story 
of  events  centreing  there  during  the  War  of  Independence  the 
author  has  worked  much  local  and  family  history  and  tradition. 
He  does  not  disguise  his  anti-English  bias.  French,  Engish, 
Colonists  and  Indians  appear  in  these  pages.  The  heroine  is  a 
high-spirited  woman,  who  does  great  deeds  in  defence  of  her 
country's  flag.      Full  of  exciting  incidents. 

Sweetheart  Manette 

A  love  romance,  centreing  in  a  charming  girl  who  has  at  her  feet 
a  miscellaneous  group  of  admirers — a  Boston  millionaire,  a 
novelist,  a  speculator  from  Colorado,  and  a  Southerner  of  old 
family.  The  surroundings  of  the  old  Creole  town  on  the  Gulf 
Coast  are  pleasantly  sketched.

Trollope,  Adolphus,     Diamond  Cut  Diamond 

Trollope,  Anthony 
Barchester  Towers 

Tlie  chief  incidents  are  connected  with  tlie  appointment  of  a  new 
bishop,  the  troubles  and  disappointments  that  ensue,  and  tlie 
intrigues  and  jealousies  of  tlie  clergy.  The  characters  comprise 
the  henpecked  bishop  and  his  Amazonian  lady,  Mrs.  Proudie  ; 
Archdeacon  Grantley,  son  of  the  late  bisliop,  who  had  hoped  to 
succeed;  Mr.  Harding;  the  eccentric  Stanhope  family  ;  and  the 
precentor,  canons  and  other  clergy  of  the  cathedral,  with  tlieir 
wives. 

Can  You  Forgive  Her  ? 

A  study  of  the  half-realised  motives  and  minor  causes  that 
determine  conduct.  Plantagenet  Palliser,  who  figures  repeatedly 
in  the  Phineas  novels,  is  a  character  here  ;  a  strong,  haughty 
and  frigid  English  gentleman,  a  politician  devoted  to  his  coimtry's 
service,  and  a  man  of  indestructible  principle,  yet  entirely  lacking 
in  personal  charm — a  very  representative  national  type. 

Clave  rings.  The 

Harry  Clavering  has  to  choose  between  the  girl  to  whom  he  is 
engaged  and  his  old  love,  who  had  jilted  him,  but  now  turns  to 
liim  again,  rich  and  a  widow.  These  are  the  three  principal 
figures  ;  while  in  the  minor  characters,  a  county  family  and  their 
friends,  tlie  parson  and  his  family,  &c.,  is  exhibited  the  typical 
life  of  the  landed  gentry — their  dinners,  hunting,  flirtations  and 
match-mal<ing,  their  egotistic  social  intercourse,  family  squabbles 
and  thoroughly  matter-of-fact  and  unintellectual  existence. 

Doctor  Thorne 

Main  thread  of  plot  concerned  witii  the  changes  of  fortune  in  the 
career  of  .Mary  Thorne,  whose  troubles  commence  with  her  birth. 
A  host  of  characters:  Beatrice  Grcsham  and  Mary,  two  attractive 
girls  ;  divers  pairs  of  lovers,  actual  or  potential,  whose  affairs 
occasion  humorous  situations  ;  genial  Dr.  Thorne,  a  country 
practitioner  of  strong  personal  character  ;  the  humorous  figures 
of  the  aristocratic  De  Courcys,  &c.  The  lesson  seems  to  be 
intended  that  people  should  marrj-  for  love  and  not  for  money. 


442  yd^^     ^ook=lovers    Clbrantf. 


Trollope,  Anthony — co)itiiiuc(L 
Eustace  Diamonds,  The 

Lady  Eustace  is  called  by  the  author,  "  an  opulent  and  aristocratic 
BecUy  Sharp."  Her  unscrupulous  lying  darkens  the  mystery  of 
the  diamonds,  and  brings  about  manj-  unexpected  and  amusing 
turns  in  the  story.  Among  tiie  principal  characters  are  several 
of  a  more  agreeable  type,  and  in  tlie  background  are  people 
made  familiar  in   '•  Phineas  Finn,"   &c. 

Eye  for  an  Eye,  An 

Framley  Parsonage 

.Anothei'  section  of  Barsetshire  society.  The  vicar  of  Framlej-, 
a  weak  but  honest  young  man,  is  led  astray  and  into  debt  by  a 
spendthi-ift  M.P.,  and  finds  himself  in  a  false  position.  The 
other  branch  of  the  plot  deals  with  Lucy  Hobarts,  his  sister's 
chequered  love  affair  and  marriage  with  young  Lord  Lufton.  As 
usual,  a  great  crowd  of  characters  are  engaged  in  the  social 
functions,  the  intrigues  and  the  match-making  business,  the 
general  effect  of  which  is  comic,  though  graver  intei-est  is  never 
far  off,  and  there  are  situations  of  deepest  pathos. 

He  Knew  He  Was  Right 

The  gradual  estrangement  of  husband  and  wife,  beginning  with 
an  insignificant  difference,  and  ending  in  strife  and  agony  ;  a 
traged)'  composed  of  the  homeliest  materials.  The  husband  is 
a  portentious  image  of  obstinate  and  stupid  suspicion,  and 
proneness  to  tai<e  offence.  This  main  story  is  complicated  by 
the  affairs  of  tiic  usual  large  number  of  minor  characters. 

Last  Chronicle  of  Barset,  The 

The  dominant  situation  is  one  of  intense  anguish.  A  poor  country' 
clergyman,  proud,  learned,  sternly  conscientious,  is  accused  of  a 
felony,  and  the  pressure  of  family  want  makes  his  guilt  seem 
only  too  probable.  His  own  agony,  his  wife's  dread,  and  the 
distress  of  his  daughter,  affianced  to  the  son  of  a  neighboLiring 
landowner,  are  the  elements  of  a  profound  tragedy.  The 
ecclesiastical  society  of  "  Tlie  Warden  "  and  its  successors, 
Mr.  Harding,  Mrs.  Proudic  and  the  rest,  make  their  last 
appearance   here. 

Mr   Scarborough's  Family 

Orley  Farm 

A  lengthy  chronicle  of  family  life  (two  country  families  supply 
most  of  the  chief  personages),  the  events  of  which  revolve 
principally  round  one  figure.  Lady  Mason,  a  mixed  character  of 
guilt  and  innocence,  weakness  and  strength,  who  forges  a  codicil 
in  favour  of  her  son,  and  for  twenty  years  preserves  the  secret 
intact.  A  chivalrous  old  baronet,  his  high-minded  daughter-in-law, 
and  a  dry  old  lawyer,  are  all  under  tlic  spell  of  Lad}'  Mason's 
personality,  and  the  drama  of  guilt  and  shame  has  a  pathetic 
bearing  on  many  lives.  The  legal  case  is  complex  and  difficult, 
and  the  proceedings  subser\e  the  author's  purpose  of  exposing 
the  immorality  of  wrongful  advocacy.  A  pair  of  bagmen  and 
other  minor  characters  relieve  the  graver  matters  with  chapters 
of  natural  comedy. 

Phineas  Finn 
Phineas  Redux

443 


TroUope,  Kn\\vony— continued. 

Small  House  at  Allington,  The 

Country  life,  with  its  quiet,  its  pleasures  and  troubles,  monotony 
and  dulness,  is  the  principal  subject-matter  ;  and  there  arc 
digressions  into  boarding-house  life  in  London,  and  into  high 
society.     Contains  the  usual  crowd,  including  many  old  friends. 

Three  Clerks,  The 

A  triple  love  story.  Three  men  in  the  civil  service  meet  and 
fall  in  love  with  three  girls,  whose  differences  of  character  are 
strongly  marked  out.  The  plot  ends  happih',  while  to  the 
romantic  interest  is  to  be  added  that  of  delicately  ironical 
portraiture,  two  at  least  of  the  clerks  being  sketched  from 
well-known   people. 

Vicar  of  Bullhampton,  'i'he 

A  group  of  tlToroughl)-  Englisii  characters,  and  tlieir  several 
histories,  connected  by  natiu-al  points  of  contact :  a  genial  and 
manly  country  vicar,  who  champions  the  cause  of  a  fallen  girl 
and  of  a  country  fellow  wrongly  suspected  of  murder,  and  suffers 
obloquy:  an  unfortunate  squire  in  love  with  the  heroine, 
who  loves  somebody  else  ;  a  crusty  old  farmer ;  and  so  on. 
Episodes  of  homcl)-  life,  with  its  every-day  interests,  humours 
and  sorrows,  form  a  complexity  like  the  complexity  of  actual  life. 

Warden,  The 

"  The  Warden,"  Mr.  Hai'ding,  is  a  gentle  and  innocent  old  man, 
living  a  calm  and  contented  life,  who  is  suddenly  assailed  by  the 
newspapers  fc^r  receiving  the  profits  of  a  rich  sinecure,  and,  iialf 
in  fear  of  the  odium  thus  created,  half  from  conscientious 
scruples,  resigns  his  income  and  accepts  penury.  The  cathedral 
city,  with  the  ecclesiastical  dignitaries,  Mr.  Harding's  friends, 
was  probably  suggested  by  Salisbury. 

\\  ay  We  Live  Now,  The 

Many  phases  of  English  life,  high  society,  country  life,  the  genteel 
and  the  humble,  journalists,  commercial  men  and  the  wor-Id  in 
general,  delineated  with  a  special  eye  for  weak  and  flagitious 
motive.  Largely  an  exposure  of  the  mari-iage  mari<et  and  the 
brutal  grossness  and  indelicacy  of  the  haggling  that  goes  on 
between  such  people  as  the  ruined  family  of  patricians  and  the 
rascally  millionaire  who  is  about  to  subscribe  to  them  his 
daughter  and  his  thousands.  Even  the  honest  young  man  of  the 
novel  is  a  far  from  admirable  person.

Twain,  Mark  (Samuel  L.  Clemens) 
Adventures  of  Huckleberry  Finn 

.\n  epic  of  boyhood  ;  the  adventures  of  a  boy  comrade  of  Tom 
Sawyer  in  a  voyage  down  the  great  Mississippi  on  a  raft.  Huck 
stands  out  among  the  author's  boy  characters  ;  he  is  the  central 
figure  of  these  episodes,  which  bring  out  liis  shrewdness,  his 
humour,  and  his  struggling  conscience.  Faithful  in  the  rendering 
of  Southern  dialects,  and  a  good  example  of  the  writer's  best 
work. 


X;M^     t^oo/i= lovers    I^iSrani/.  445 


Twain,  Mark   (Samuel  L.  Clzmzns)— continued. 

Adventures  of  Tom  Sawyer,  The 

The  basis  of  tliis  story  of  boy  life  is  furnished  by  reminiscences 
of  the  author's  own  bojhood  in  Hannibal,  Missouri,  and  a  very 
full  picture  of  life  in  the  Soutli  \\'est  is  painted:  the  little  town, 
the  rustic  notables,  the  humours  of  social  distinctions  restinf;  on 
slavery,  Tom's  relatives  and  their  family  life.  The  tale  is  full  of 
incident  and  fun  ;  it  enters  with  delight  into  the  rascalities  of  the 
boys,  and  in  Tom  and  his  comrade.  Huck  Finn,  offers  two 
humorous  types  of  the  genus. 

American  Claimant,  The 

Double- Barrelled  Detective  Story,  A 

Innocents  Abroad,  The 

An  account  of  travels  in  the  Mediterranean  and  the  adjacent 
countries.  The  party  of  Innocents  adopt  the  role  of  impervious 
Philistines  with  no  reverence  for  the  works  of  art  and  antiquity, 
the  historical  and  sacred  memorials  about  which  the  sentimental 
tourist,  guide-book  in  hand,  raves. 

Life  on  the  Mississippi 

Man  that  Corrupted  Hadleyburg 

Reminiscences,  records  of  striking  experiences,  and  fictitious 
episodes  of  a  humorous  kind.  Title  story  tells  how  a  man  en- 
trapped the  stainless  citizens  of  an  American  town  into  a  snare 
for  their  vanity  and  covetousness,  and  exploded  their  reputation 
for  incorruptible  probity.  "  The  Esquimaux  Maiden's  Romance  " 
is  a  farce  related  with  mock  gravity;  and  "  My  First  Lie  "  is  a 
humorous  parable. 

£1,000,000  Bank  Note,  The 

More  Tramps  Abroad 

Personal  Recollections  of  Joan  of  Arc 

Prince  and  the  Pauper,  The 

A  fantasia;  how  Prince  Edward  (VI.),  in  Henry  VIII. 's  reign, 
changed  positions  with  a  beggar,  and  what  came  of  it.  Realistic 
in  its  treatment  of  mediaeval  life,  showing  up  its  selfishness, 
cruelty  and  barbarism,  as  an  antidote  to  the  iiigh  romantic  style. 

Roughing  It 

Stolen  White  Elephant,  The 

New  and  old  stories  ;  among  the  new  "  The  Invalid's  Story  "  is 
a  very  characteristic  chapter.  Tiie  titular  piece  is  a  huge 
burlesque. 

Tom  Sawyer  Abroad 

Tom  Sawyer,  Detective 

Tramp  Abroad,  A 

In  this  and  its  sequel,  "  More  Tramps  Abroad,"  are  to  be  found 
two  facetious  narratives  of  the  author's  travels  over  the  globe, 
not  precisely  fiction,  yet  characterised  by  the  same  exaggeration 
and  humorous  interpretation  of  the  character  of  people  and  places 
as  the  foregoing  works  ;  humorous  impressionism  they  migiit  be 
called;  they  are  full  of  broad  jokes,  hilarious  incidents  and  mock- 
serious  criticism,  stamped  by  the  same  Philistinism  that 
characterised  the  Innocents,  and  by  a  serious  insight  that  lookr 
below  the  surface.

Twain,  Mark  (Samuel  L.  Clemens) — continued. 
Yankee  at  the  Court  of  King  Arthur,  A 

A  burlesque  of  the  historical  romance.  A  Yankee  of  the  most 
modern  type  is  plumped  down  in  the  middle  of  King  Arthur's 
England,  and  a  series  of  farcical  incidents  ensue.  The  serious 
purpose,  which  is  not  obtruded,  is  to  strip  off  the  glamour  and 
tinsel  of  chivalry,  and  show  the  evils  and  miseries  that  actually 
e.visted.

Daughter  of  the  Fields,  A

Vachell,  Horace  A. 

Drama  in  Sunshine,  A 

A  romance  of  the  land  boom  in  California.  A  strenuous  Yankee 
lawyer  successfuU)-  booms  an  estate,  and  is  quicUly  on  his  way 
to  fortune;  but  he  has  married  a  half-Spanish  girl  who  loves  him 
ardentl}-,  and  the  conflict  arises  between  his  ambition  and  her 
jealousy.  N'iolent  scenes  of  murder  and  vengeance  result  from 
the  inflamed  passions  of  the  dispossessed  Irish  squatters;  indeed, 
the  whole  story  is  essentially  a  tragedy-,  though  an  unexpected 
stroke  in  the  last  sentence  averts  a  sad  ending. 

John  Charity 

Romance  of  adventure  in  Alta  California  in  the  thirties;  the  hero 
tights  under  Alvarado  for  independence  against  Mexico,  and  meets 
with  accidents  in  the  field,  and  with  love  complications  among 
the  pretty  women. 

Pinch  of  Prosperity,  The 

In  this  book  prosperity  and  poverty  are  presented  side  by  side,  as 
they  may  be  found  in  the  book  of  life,  and  a  study  of  many  lives, 
curiously  interwoven,  has  been  attempted. 

Procession  of  Life,  The 
Romance  of  Judge  Ketchum

Ben  Hur 

A  long  and  gorgeous!}-  coloured  romance  of  Oriental  life  in  the 
first  century,  abounding  in  florid  scenes  of  pageantry.  The  plot 
is  intricate. 

Fair  God,  The 

An  archaeological  reconstruction  of  Mexican  life  at  the  time  of 
the  conquest ;    exceedingly  elaborate,  brilliant  in  effect. 

Prince  of  India,  The 

The  hero  takes  the  character  of  the  Wandering  Jew.  Gives  a 
florid  picture  of  the  Byzantine  Empire  in  the  15th  centurj'.

Ward,  E.

Pair  of  Originals,  A 

Ward,  Mrs.  Humphry 
Eleanor 

A  human  and  emotional  story  ;  though  the  poHtical  and  social 
life  of  modern  Italy  is  studied  exhaustively,  the  main  affair  is 
the  drama  of  passion,  two  women  of  fine  character  loving  the 
hero,  a  literary  man  of  high  intellectual  gifts.  It  is  a  pro- 
foundly affecting  story,  culminating  in  a  scene  of  tragic 
renunciation.  The  minor  personages  comprise  many  repre- 
sentatives of  the  ideas,  forces,  and  tendencies  that  are  working 
for  change  in  Italy,  and  of  the  antagonism  between  the  Papacy 
and  the  Italy  of  Garibaldi  and  Cavour,  &c.  Rich  in  descriptive 
passages  of  the  country,  of  Rome,  of  Catholic  ceremonial,   &c. 

Helbeck  of  Bannisdale 

The  tragic  loves  of  a  devout  Catholic  lawman  and  an  agnostic  girl. 
Like  the  foregoing,  a  tale  of  character  and  of  passion,  complicated 
by  problems  of  thought.  Both  sides  of  the  controversy  are  treated 
with  sympathy'  ;  the  Catholic,  Helbeck,  is  a  man  of  the  noblest 
nature,  and  Catholic  dogmas,  rites  and  social  agencies  are 
carefully  described. 

History  of  David  Grieve,  The 

Earnest  and  weighty  with  thought,  it  is  a  study  of  religious  and 
sociological  problems  rather  than  a  true  novel.  David's  different 
connections  with  two  women  illustrate  the  pros  and  cons  of 
lawful  marriage  and  free  union  ;  his  mental  history — revivalism, 
secularism,  socialism  and  communism — opens  up  the  broadest 
questions  of  the  relation  between  man  and  man,  and  between 
men  and  God  ;  he  ends  as  an  ardent  Theist  or  Unitarian.  The 
wild  surroundings  of  a  moorland  farm  in  northern  Derbyshire, 
and  the  savage  recesses  of  Kinderscout  are  described  with 
enthusiasm. 

Lady  Rose's  Daughter 

.\  study  in  character,  being  the  story  of  a  girl's  strong  temptation, 
also  of  her  transformation  and  development  both  in  disposition 
and  fortune. 


458  73^^     tJjook=lovers    dSnary. 


"Ward,  Mrs.  Humphry — continued. 

Marcella 

At  once  an  inductive  study  of  modern  political  and  social  ideas  in 
their  action  on  character,  and  a  novel  of  passion.  Marcella  is  a 
woman  of  unusual  intellect  and  culture,  who  desires,  above  all 
things,  to  be  of  use,  and  at  first  belieses  the  regeneration  of  the 
world  is  to  be  effected  by  means  of  socialism  and  the  sweeping 
away  of  plutocratic  institutions.  In  this  first  act  of  her  history 
she  comes  under  the  potent  influence  of  a  political  charlatan 
advocating  socialism,  and  of  a  solid  young  nobleman  of  liberal 
principles.  Ultimately  she  learns  by  experience  that  doctrines 
are  of  less  importance  than  character  and  personal  action,  and 
doubts  whether  the  social  order  can  be  overset  for  the  good  of 
humanity. 

Miss  Bretherton 

Robert  Elsmere 

The  struggle  between  faith  and  agnosticism  set  forth  in  the  life 
and  love  of  a  clergyman  who  leaves  the  church.  Elsmere's 
home,  education  and  O.xford  career,  where  he  is  influenced  b\- 
various  philosophical  tutors  (Mr.  Grey  is  meant  for  the  late 
Professor  F.  H.  Green),  are  described.  On  bec(jming  rector  of 
a  Lake  District  parish  his  mental  struggle  becomes  acute,  and 
tragedy  is  added  by  growing  estrangement  from  his  evangelical 
wife.  Psychological  analysis  is  very  minute  ;  the  criticism  of 
Christianity  is  inspired  by  older  German  theorists.  Preaches 
a  Christianity  purified  of  supernatural  accretions. 

Sir  George  Tressady 

Continues  the  development  of  Marcella's  noble  character,  and 
deals  with  a  young  politician,  Tressady,  in  the  same  minute 
and  exhaustive  way.  Here,  as  in  "  Marcella,"  the  stud}'  of 
political  movements  and  of  social  conditions  divides  attention 
about  equally  with  the  personal  drama. 

Story  of  Bessie  Costrell,  The 

A  cottage  tragedy  in  five  brief  acts.  A  flighty  country  woman  is 
tempted  to  borrow  from  the  hoard  of  a  thrifty  old  labourer,  and 
borrows  beyond  possibility  of  repayment.  In  the  crucial  scene 
she  is  confronted  by  her  Puritan  husband,  the  labourer  who  had 
trusted  her  and  now  sees  himself  left  to  chance  mercies,  and  the 
neighbours  who  make  themselves  her  judges,  and  she  commits 
suicide. 

Ward,  Mrs.  Wilfrid 
Light  Behind,  The 

A  societ}-  novel,  tinctured  with  love,  politics  and  religion.     The 
heroine,  having  married  a  brute,  is  tempted,  but  conquers,  and 
her    life    being    withered    away   by   a    foul    slander,    passes    into 
"The   Light   Behind,"  which  she  had  so  long  sought. 
One  Poor  Scruple 
Ward  of  the  Golden  Gate  ...  ...  Bret  H arte 

Ward  of  the  King         ...  ...  Katharine  S.  Macquoid 

Warden,  Florence  (Mrs.  James) 

Bohemian   Girls,  The  Fight  to  a  Finish,  A

Farm  in  the  Hills,  The  Girls  Will  be  Girls

Heart  of  a  Girl.  The

pressure  and  temptation.

Outsider's  Year,  An 

The  story  of  a  poor  relation,  who,  after  inheriting  a  fortune,  is

Perfect  Fool,  A  Strictly  Incog:

Warden,  Gertrude 

Beyond  the  Law 

A  sensational  story  of  modern  London. 

Haunted  House  at  Kew,  The 

Nobody's  Widow 

A  girl  of  independent  spirit  goes  to  South  Africa  in  search  of  her 
lover,  whom  she  finds  in  great  danger.  To  induce  his  companions 
to  rescue  him  she  declares  herself  his  wife  ;  he  is  found 
apparently  dead,  and  she  returns  to  England  as  his  widow.  He 
goes  back  eventually,  however,  and  marries  her  who  is  "  Nobody's 
Widow." 

Scoundrel  or  Saint? 

Sentimental  Se.\,  The 

Set  to  Partners 

Stolen  Pearl,  The 

The  story  of  the  machinations  of  a  clever  villain,  a  Russian 
doctor,  who  subjects  his  patients  to  his  influence  by  destroying 
their  will  power  in  a  peculiar  way.  His  diabolical  cunning  proves 
insufficient  to  avert  discovery  and  punishment,  or  the  happiness 
of  the  lovers  whom  he  hated.

Wells,  H.  G. 

First  Men  in  the  Moon,  The 

Making  one  bold  postulate,  the  discovery  of  a  substance  that 
resists  gravity,  the  author  depicts,  with  daring  imagination,  j-ct 
plausible  regard  to  scientific  analogy,  the  moon  and  its  plants 
and  inhabitants. 

Invisible  Man,  The 

Island  of  Dr.  Moreau,  The 

Love  and  Mr.  Lewisham 

Like  "  The  Wheels  of  Chance,"  a  study  of  the  life  of  very 
ordinary  and  commonplace  people.  Mr.  Lewisham  is  a  school- 
master and  student  who  spoils  his  plans  for  advancement  by 
falling  in  love,  and  ruins  his  literary  and  socialist  ambitions 
by  getting  married. 

Plattner  Story,  The 

Sea  Lady,  The 

Stolen  Bacillus,  The 

Tales  of  Space  and  Time 

Miscellaneous  studies  in  astronomy  and  imaginative  chronology. 
Two  stories  of  the  far  future  and  the  far  past  :  "  A  Story  of  the 
Stone  Age;"  "A  Story  of  the  Days  to  Come."  "  Tlie  Star" 
describes  a  cataclysm  caused  by  a  comet  on  the  earth,  and  the 
feelings  of  terrified  humanity.  Like  the  author's  other  fantasies, 
contains  a  good  deal  of  serious  speculation  on  the  future  of 
mankind,  and  criticism  of  society. 
Time  Machine,  The 

A  scientific  fantasy.  The  machine  transports  the  owner  at  will 
into  the  past  or  into  the  future. 

War  of  the  Worlds,  The 

The  inhabitants  of  Mars,  a  terribly  bestial,  yet  highly  organised 
race,  invade  England,  and,  by  their  command  of  superior  weapons, 
subdue  and  prey  on  the  people.

Wells,  H.  (j,—contimied. 
Wheels  of  Chance,  The 

Serio-comic  narrative  of  a  poor  ovcr-worited  draper's  adventures 
on  a  cycling  holiday  in  Surrey  and  Sussex.  He  plays  knight- 
errant  to  a  distressed  damsel,  his  latent  goodness  and  heroism 
are  brought  out,  and  he  goes  home  with  new  ideas  of  what  is 
good  and  worthy  in  life. 

When  the  Sleeper  W^akes 

London,    a.d.    2100.     A   picture   of   topsy-turvy    institutions   and 
humanity    metamorphosed,     socially    and     morally,     and     more 
especially  of  the  mechanical  wonders  of  the  future.      Extremely 
pessimistic  view  of  mankind  in  the  coming  centuries. 
Wonderful  Visit,  The 

The  doings  of  an  angel  who  visits  the  earth,  and  is  shot  by  a 
sporting  vicar.

Daisy  Melbourne  House 

Daisy  in  the  Field  Queechy 

Flag  of  Truce  Wide,  Wide  World,  The 

Glen  Luna  Family 

Weyman,  Stanley  J» 
Castle  Inn,  The 

A  tale  of  society  and  manners  at  Marlborough  in  the  18th 
century,  with  a  description  of  an  abduction,  a  rescue,  and  other 
episodes  of  travelling  during  the  period. 

Count  Hannibal,  The 

Ingenious  and  exciting  as  a  story,  not  of  much  account  as  a 
drawing  of  character.  The  massacre  of  St.  Bartholomew,  1572, 
is  the  central  historical  episode,  and  the  manners  and  atmosphere 
of  contemporary  France  are  vividly  reproduced.  Count  Hannibal 
is  another  of  the  somewhat  unprepossessing  heroes,  whose 
bravery  and  magnanimity  are  supposed  to  atone  for  his  violence 
and  passion  in  the  estimation  of  his  lady-love — and  of  the  reader. 

From  the  Memoirs  of  a  Minister  of  France 

Gentleman  of  France,  A 

The  hero  is  a  Huguenot  Breton,  one  of  Conde's  veterans,  whose 
povert}-  and  shabby  appearance  are  flouted  by  the  courtiers  ;  but 
in  an  important  and  delicate  commission  entrusted  to  him  he 
acquits  himself  so  valiantly  that  he  wins  the  love  of  his  beautiful 
charge,  a  noble  kinswoman  of  Turenne.  Court  life  and  warlike 
adventure  are  the  staple  of  the  book,  which  is  a  very  excellent 
one  of  its  kind. 

House  of  the  Wolf,  The 

A  swashbuckler  romance,  dealing  with  the  massacre  of  St. 
Bartholomew.  A  grim  and  pitiless  giant  woos  the  beloved  of  a 
Huguenot  and  seeks  his  life. 

Man  in  Black,  The 

A  brief  and  clever  story  of  adventure  and  intrigue  in  the  time  of 
Louis  XIII. 

My  Lady  Rotha 

Opens  in  Thuringia  during  the  Thirty  Years  War,  the  thread  of 
plot  being  the  love  story  of  the  Countess  Rotha,  which,  however, 
is  of  secondary  interest  to  the  descriptions  of  strife  and 
adventure  that  portray  the  hideous  devastation  and  demorali- 
sation of  Germany  overrun  by  mercenaries  and  thieves. 

New  Rector,  The 

Red  Cockade,  The 

Adventures  during  the  French  Revolution,  related  by  an 
aristocrat  in  sympathy  with  the  people.  Scene :  Cahors  and 
Nimes. 

Shrewsbury 

Deals  with  the  inner  history  of  Sir  John  Fenwick's  Jacobite  plot, 
and  with  other  incidents  of  the  English  Revolution  and  the 
subsequent  years  (1688-1713).  Many  of  the  characters  are 
cowards,  bullies  and  scoundrels,  but  the  famous  Duke  of 
Shrewsbury  is  an  attractive  figure. 


cSook'lovers    dbrany. 


465 


Weyman,  Stanley  J. — continued. 

Sophia 

A  romance  of  intrigue  and  social  comedy,  opening  in  1742  and 
picturing  the  manners  and  customs  of  the  18th  century.  The 
wayward  hoyden  Sophia  is  nearly  ruined  at  the  hands  of  an 
Irish  adventurer,  and  saved  by  the  hero,  another  of  the  author's 
middle-aged  gentlemen  who,  after  many  vicissitudes,  win  the 
heroine's  love  by  sheer  force  of  integrity  and  courage. 

Story  of  Francis  Cludde,  The 

A  romance  in  Dumas'  style  of  the  times  of  Bloody  Mary 
(1555-1558).  A  sturdy  young  Protestant,  slow-tongued,  but  quick 
to  act,  tired  of  persecution,  goes  forth  to  win  fame  by  a  career  of 
adventure. 

Under  the  Red  Robe 

An  adventurer  of  bad  character  is  sent  on  a  treacherous  mission 
by  Richelieu,  but  falls  in  love  with  the  sister  of  the  man  he  has 
to  betray.  He  redeems  his  character  by  liberating  the  prisoner, 
and  giving  himself  up  to  the  Cardinal.  B(iarn  supplies  the 
locality  and  the  scenery.

Whaleman's  Wife,  A  ...  ...  ...     Frank  T.  Bullen 

Wharton,  Edith 

Gift  from  the  Grave,  A 

A  novelette  dealing  with  an  episode  in  a  man's  moral  life.     For  a 
large     sum     he     sells    the    love-letters    written    to    him    by    a 
distinguished  lady,  and  after  he  has  married  the  woman  he  loves, 
comes  to  realise  the  meanness  of  his  act.

Grip  of  the  Bookmaker,  The 

The  chief  figure  is  a  retired  booUniiiUer  with  a  shady  past,  engaged

An  English  lord,  disgusted  bj-  the  vices  and  sophistications  of 
modern  society — and  he  beholds  it  in  gilded  London  and  Paris — 
retires  from  the  world.  Cast  away  on  a  Pacific  isle,  he  discovers 
a  community  of  English  people  living  in  peace  and  happy 
innocence,  and  intercourse  with  them  renews  his  faith  in 
humanity. 
Number  5  John  Street 

After  the  peer's  demise  or  disappearance,  his  friend,  a  rich  old 
baronet,  continues  his  researches  into  social  conditions  by  living 
the  life  of  the  poor  in  a  London  slum.  His  journal  presents  a  series 
of  vividly  contrasted  pictures,  the  luxury  of  the  rich  and  the 
misery  of  the  poor,  the  former  the  alleged  cause  of  the  latter,  for 
tlie  two  stories  together  are  the  expression  of  the  author's  plea 
for  a  radical!)'  new  system  of  distribution. 
WhiteJacket,orTheWorldinaMan-of-\Var  H.  Melville

Wiggin,  Kate  D.  (Mrs.  G.  C.  Riggs) 

Cathedral  Courtship,  A 

Marm  Lisa 

Penelope's  English  Experiences 

The  experiences  of  three  American  ladies  on  a  visit  to  England. 
Scenes  in  London  and  the  village  of  Belvern,  containing  fanciful 
sketches  of  a  West  End  ball,  portraits  of  domestic  originals,  &c., 
characterised  by  humorous  trifling  and  droll  exaggeration  of 
English  traits. 

Penelope's  Experiences  in  Scotland 

The  three  go  to  Scotland,  and  meet  with  still  funnier  experiences 
in  Edinburgh  and  farther  North.  Places,  people  and  society  are 
depicted  in  a  droll  and  buoyant  style  ;  old  world  manners  and 
characters  of  an  original  kind  arc  sketched  with  fanciful  humour. 

Penelope's  Irish  Experiences 

The  trio  of  fair  Americans  visit  Ireland,  and  criticise  the  country 
and  people  in  the  genial,  light-hearted  style  already  familiar. 
Comic  incidents,  good  stories,  legends  and  racy  sayings  make  up 
the  greater  part  of  the  volume. 

Timothy's  Quest 

A  pretty  and  touching  little  story  of  two  children's  venture  into 
the  world  ;  the  boy  a  figure  of  precocious  manliness  ;  all  the 
characters  drawn  with  kindliness  and  humour.  A  storj-  for 
the  young  that  is  interesting  to  older  people  ;  the  authoress 
asserts  the  rights  of  children  at  every  opportunity.

Wilkins,  Mary  E. 

Heart's  Highway,  The 

A  liistorical  romance,  dealing  with  Virginia  under  Charles  II.  and 
the  tobacco  riots  after  Nathaniel  Bacon's  rebellion  (1682). 

Humble  Romance,  A 

Twenty-eight  stories  and  sUetches  of  a  liomely,  penurious  life  in  a 
Massachusetts  village,  characterised  by  terse  realism.  The 
stories  are  all  pathetic  studies  of  unhappiness  in  various  phases, 
and  this  monotony  answers  to  a  certain  monotony  in  the 
characters,  who  are  nearly  all  abnormal. 

Jamesons,  The 

Jane  Field 

The  spiritual  tragedj-  of  an  intrinsically  noble  woman,  a  rigid 
Puritan,  who  is  sorely  tempted  by  maternal  love  and  by  the 
unlawful  impulse  to  rii^lit  mundane  injustice,  and  sits  stubbornly 
and  perscveringly.  Tlie  spectacle  of  her  severe  integrity'  brought 
by  her  own  act  to  a  position  of  falseliood  and  shame  is  lit  up  by  a 
sort  of  tragic  humour  ;   here  begins  her  penitence  and  expiation. 

Jerome 

A  poor  young  man  makes  a  kind  of  wager  that,  if  he  becomes  rich 
he  will  gi\e  up  all  his  wealtli  to  the  poor  ;  botli  of  which  he  does. 
Tlie  book  is  a  study  of  lowly,  straitened  life,  and  a  sentimental 
indictment  of  tlie  self-indulgence  of  the  rich.  Jerome  is  a  good 
specimen  of  the  wrong-headed,  stiff-necked  New  Englander, 
sacrificing  everything  to  his  wilful  pride.  Among  the  minor 
characters  are  some  sketches  of  the  genial  old-fashioned  gentry, 
very  unlike  the  author's  average  New  Englander. 

Long  Arm,  The 

Love  of  Parson  Lord,  The 

Old  Parson  Lord  has  dedicated  his  only  daughter  Love  to 
missionary  work,  but  his  affection  makes  him  acquiesce  in  her 
clTildish  frivolities,  and  even  allows  her  to  marry,  though  without 
his  open  consent.  The  "Tree  of  Knowledge"  tells  how  a  would-be 
burglar  was  converted  on  the  spot  by  a  pretty,  innocent  maiden. 
"One  Good  Time  "  is  the  characteristic  stor\- of  a  penurious  country- 
woman who  spends  £300  in  a  week  of  enjoyment,  then  settles 
down  resignedly  to  sober  married  life.  There  is  also  a  story  of 
revolutionarj-  times,  and  a  iiumorous  sketch  of  three  old  sisters 
and  an  old  beau. 

Madelon 

A  tragedj-  in  the  same  rural  setting,  the  unusual  motive  being 
romantic  love.  Here  again  is  a  series  of  characters  all  more  or 
less  under  the  dominion  of  a  fixed  idea. 

New  England  Nun,  A 

Twenty-four  compressed  stories  and  genre  pictures,  humorous  or 
pathetic,  of  the  same  rather  sombre  people. 


CV^     t/^ooA'=/<)i<ers    L.i6/'cirtj.  471 


Wilkins,  Mary  E,—coiiti)ii(ccL 

Pembroke 

Tlic  writer's  most  complete  presentation  of  the  life  of  a  New 
En<»land  village,  and  a  summary  of  lier  observations  of  these 
people  of  perverted  wills — on  the  whole  an  unlovely  picture, 
though  relieved  by  descriptions  of  beaiitifid  landscapes,  orchards 
and  old-world  homesteads.  Quarrels  persisted  in  to  the  bitter 
end,  life  feuds  between  neighbours  and  kindred,  stubborn  and  sellish 
pride  blighting  the  love  of  youth  and  maid,  and  entailing  tragic 
consequences,  such  are  the  prevailing  motives,  and  most  of  the 
characters  are  abnormal  developments  of  this  one  atti'ibute  of  the 
Puritan  nature.  There  are  beside  the  pathos  many  passages  of 
humour  and  of  keen  enjoyment  of  life. 

Portion  of  Labour,  The 

A  study  of  the  problem  of  capital  and  labour  in  New  England  ; 
with  delineations  of  character  in  a  large  group  of  people,  descen- 
dants chiefly  of  the  old  colonial  stock,  and  similar  in  their  salient 
qualities  to  the  types  with  which  the  authoress  has  familiarised  us. 

Silence 

The  title  story  deals  with  the  horrors  and  heroism  of  the  old  wars 
with  the  Indians  ;  Silence  is  a  village  girl  who  becomes  crazed 
with  sorrow  for  her  lover  carried  away  by  the  red  men.  "The 
Little  Maid  at  the  Door"  is  a  tale  of  Puritan  superstitions  and 
the  suffering  they  entailed  on  the  innocent  people.  "  Evelina's 
Garden  "  is  a  fanciful  story  in  Hawthorne's  manner,  more  than 
half  apologue,  glorifying  pure  affection  as  the  most  precious 
thing  in  human  life. 

Six  Trees 

Si.\  short  stories  of  New  England  life,  based  respectively  upon 
"  si.K  trees,"  and  showing  the  inter-acting  influence  of  trees  and 
people  upon  each  other. 

Under  Studies 

Hardly  a  characteristic  book.  Two  series  of  fanciful  stories;  one 
about  people  and  their  pets,  interpreting  traits  of  human  nature 
by  their  affinities  with  certain  animals  ;  the  other  scries,  more 
poetical,  about  certain  flowers  that  are  shown  to  he  symbolical 
types  of  human  character. 

Wind  in  the  Rose  Bush,  The 

Six  stories  of  the  supernatural.  The  -'wind"  moved  the  rose- 
bush when  all  around  was  still. 

Young  Lucretia  and  other  Stories

Adventures  of  Princess  Sylvia

Wood,  Mrs.  Henry—continued. 
East  Lynne 

The  main  situation  is  one  of  harrowing  pathos,  a  divorced  wife 
re-entering  her  husband's  house  disguised  as  a  governess,  nursing 
her  own  child  and  dying  there,  tardily  forgiven.  This  is  the 
climax  of  the  plot,  the  basis  of  which  is  a  murder,  with  the 
ultimate  clearing  up  of  the  mystery  and  the  full  proceedings  of 
trial,  cross  examination,  etc.

Mrs.  Halliburton's  Troubles 

More  domestic  history  ;  a  mother's  quiet  endurance  of  adversity, 
a  little  girl's  death,  a  family  of  virtuous  children  and  a  naughty 
boy  as  foil,  with  the  tiny  events  of  average  life — episodes  invented 
for  the  purpose  of  moralising,  the  good  young  men  {e.g.)  are 
rewarded  with  signal  success  in  their  various  callings,  despite 
initial  poverty  ;   the  wicked  fall  into  disgrace  and  want.

Shadow  of  Ashlydat,  The 

Typical  of  a  numerous  class  of  Mrs.  Wood's  novels,  whose 
interes-t  lies  in  the  working  out  of  a  plot  containing  romantic  and 
supernatural  elements  and  a  good  deal  of  family  history. 

St.  Martin's  Eve 

A  lady  who  inherits  insanity  marries  a  man  with  hereditary 
tendency  to  wasting  disease.  A  day  of  ill-omen  is  among  the 
sensational  effects.  The  lady  in  one  scene  leaves  her  step-son  to 
be  burned  to  death.     A  mixture  of  sensation  and  domesticity.

"Woodroffet  Daniel 
Tangled  Trinities 

A  Kentish  vicar  and  his  daughter,  born  in  the  West  Indies,  inherit 
a  tinge  of  black  in  their  blood.  The  author  illustrates  the  effects 
of  these  inherited  traits  of  barbarism  on  themselves  and  on  their 
English  neighbours.  The  girl's  character  is  analysed,  and  her 
story  is  pathetic,  sombre  in  its  ending.  Many  of  the  scenes  of 
modern  life  are  extremely  outspoken  in  their  realism,  and 
occasionally  morbid  and  cynical. 

Woods,  Margaret  L. 

Esther  Vanhomrigh 

The  love  story  of  Swift  and  "  Vanessa  "  told  anew  ;  an  imagi- 
native stud)-  of  18th  century  life,  introducing,  beside  Swift,  his 
friends  Addison  and  Steele,  Pope  and  Bolingbroke. 

Sons  of  the  Sword 

Adventures  of  an  Irish  girl  at  Madrid  and  elsewhere  in  Spain 
during  the  Peninsular  War.  She  comes  into  contact  witli 
Napoleon,  who  is  ably  portrayed  ;  and  Sir  John  Moore  is  also 
introduced. 

Vagabonds,  The 

A  pathetic  story  of  humble  life,  a  good-natured,  middle-aged 
clown,  his  pretty  young  wife  and  her  lover  being  the  three 
personages  of  the  tragedy.  Describes,  with  friendly  realism,  the 
daily  life  of  a  "  giant  circus  "  on  tour,  life  in  the  ring,  and  on  the 
road. 

Village  Tragedy,  A 

Shows  the  struggle  for  e.Kistence  in  its  most  elemental  phase  :  a 
simple  story,  and  a  realistic  picture  of  country  life  ;  but  almost 
intolerably  painful  ;  a  pair  of  ignorant  but  blameless  young 
people  being  done  to  death  through  the  cruelty  of  circumstances 
and  heartlessness  of  himianity.

Yonge,  Charlotte  M. — continued. 
Dove  in  the  Eagle's  Nest,  The 

The  heroine,  a  maiden  of  Ulm,  is  carried  off  by  a  robber  and 
baron  to  his  Suabian  hold,  and,  as  nurse  to  his  sick  daughter, 
brings  an  influence  for  oeace  and  goodness  into  the  house.  The 
heir  marries  her,  but  is  killed,  and  her  life-work  consists  in 
rearing  her  twin  sons  to  a  life  of  piety  and  noble  deeds.  Time  : 
middle  of  the  15th  century.

Modern  Broods 

Interesting  as  the  views  and  criticisms  of  a  mid-century  novelist 
on  the  young  person  of  to-day.  Crowded  with  characters, 
including  several  familiar  types  of  girlhood,  very  similar  to  those 
that  peopled  her  earlier  novels.  A  maiden  aunt  in  charge  of 
foiu"  girls  is  a  prominent  figui-e,  troubled  with  the  anxieties  and 
perplexities  of  their  religious  and  social  interests,  and  later,  of 
their  love  affairs.

**Zack'*  (Miss  Gwendoline  Keats) 

Life  is  Life 

"Life  is  Life'  is  a  longish  short-storj-  of  Australian  life,  rising  by  a 
climax  of  calamitj'  to  tragic  heights  of  pathos  ;  it  is  inspired  by 
profound  faith  in  the  efficac\-  of  love. 

On  Trial 

A  country  girl  commits  a  felony  for  the  salic  of  a  weak  lover,  who 
makes  her  life  a  wreck,  and  he  is  too  cowardh-  to  stand  by  her, 
whilst  miserably  conscious  of  her  degradation. 

Roman  Road,  The 

Three  short  stories.  The  "Roman  Road,"  straight  and 
unswerving,  has  an  ethical  significance,  being  in  opposition  to 
the  conduct  of  the  owner  of  the  estate  through  whicli  it  passes, 
since,  imtil  his  mother's  death,  he  keeps  his  lialf-brotlier  from  his 
birthright. 

Tales  of  Dunstable  Weir 

White  Cottage,  The 

The  man  who  engages  the  readers  sympathies,  though  by  no 
means  a  hero,  is  robbed  of  the  woman  he  loves  by  a  village 
libertine,  whom  he  subsequcntlj-  convicts  of  bigamy. 

Zambra  the  Detective...  ...  ...  Headon  Hill 

Zangwill,  Israel 

Celibate's  Club,  The 

Children  of  the  Ghetto 

The  life  of  the  Jews  of  London,  poor  and  rich,  depicted  by  one 
who  knows  and  loves  them,  a  Jew  of  the  Jews.  It  is  a  succession 
of  scenes,  connected  by  a  tliread  of  narrative;  copious  in  detail 
and  in  traits  of  character  ;  ideas  and  ways  of  thinking  as  well  as 
ceremonial  and  external  features  enter  tiiis  presentment  of 
modern  Jewry. 

Dreamers  of  the  Ghetto 

Conversatives  with,  and  imaginative  memoirs  of  historic  re- 
presentations of  tlie  Jewish  race  ;  particularly  of  such  as  rebelled 
against  the  narrow  limits  of  orthodox  Judaism.  Uriel  Acosta, 
Spinoza,  Heine,  and  Ferdinand  Lassalle,  are  among  these  figures 
resuscitated  by  an  extension  of  Landor's  method  in  the 
"  Imaginary  Conversations,""  but  witli  a  warmer  infusion  of 
feeling. 

Grey  Wig,  The 

King  of  Schnorrers,  The 

Mantle  of  Elijah,  The 

A  stud}'  of  the  politics  of  Palmerston"s  period,  with  an  anticipa- 
tion of  the  Transvaal  question  in  the  Novabarba  difficulty,  which 
affords  opportunity  for  caustic  satire  on  jingoism  and  the 
claptrap  offered  as  apology  fur  wai-. 

Master,  The 

A  circumstantial  biography  of  a  Nova  Scotian  lad,  by  nature  an 
artist,  who  works  his  way  through  almost  insuperable  difficulties 
to  success,  but  finds  the  hardest  struggle  is  to  subdue  himself. 
Deals  philosophical!}-  with  the  deeper  problems  of  life  ;  presents 
the  ideal  character  of  the  artist,  repelling  the  temptations  of  the 
v/orld,  and  devoting  bod}'  and  soul  to  art  until  he  attains  a  vision 
of  the  truth  and  beaut\-  that  are  in  tiie  world  around  him.

Zola,  Emile 

Abbe  Mouret's  Transgressions 

He  is   a    priest  of  a   squalid    village  in    Provence,  the   degenerate 
inhabitants    of    which    furnish    subjects   for    an    ugly    picture    of 
country   life.       The  central  episode  of  the  boolv  might  be  called  a 
modern  essay  in  mytiiolog)-  ;  in  it  the  author  embodies  ideas  from 
his  early  poem  of  "  Genesis." 
Conquest  of  Plassans,  The 
Dead  Woman's  Wish,  A 
Doctor  Pascal 

Doctor  Pascal,  the  learneii  student  of  heredity,  draws  up  the 
genealogical  tree,  and  traces  the  development  of  eacii  branch, 
seeing  herein  an  image  of  societ}-  and  an  explanation  of  its 
mysterious  laws.  No  doubt  Pascal  is  a  study  of  the  famous 
savant  Claude  Bernard.  The  romantic  inteix'st  of  the  story 
centres  in  his  passion  for  his  niece  Clothilde,  who  is  treated  as 
inoffensivelj'  as  a  thoroughly  sensuous  art  permits  ;  the  end  is 
deeplj-  tragic. 
Downfall,  The 

Here  Zola  applies  liis  nalLu-aiislic  methods  to  a  representation  of 
the  great  calastropiic  of  KS7()-71.  The  dis.istrous  campaign  that 
ended  at  Sedan  is  described  from  the  point  of  \  iew  of  his  two 
heroes,  private  soldiers,  who  are  in  the  thicU  of  the  iiglit,  and  not 
only  are  the  aw  ful  realities  of  modern  warfare  brougiit  before  tiie 
eye,  but  the  intolerable  fatigue  of  the  marching,  tiic  agonies  oi 
the  hospitals,  and  the  miseries  endured  bj-  prisoners-of-war,  arc 
vividly  depicted. 


486  Jd^^     ^ook=lovers    Cihnany. 


Zola,  Emile — continued. 

Dram  Shop,  The  (L'Assommoir) 

The  book  that  made  Zola  famous.  The  original  is  tremendously 
outspoken,  and  shocked  the  public  with  its  terrible  revelations  of 
the  social  depravity  due  to  drink.  The  grossness  of  the  argot 
and  of  the  incidents  is,  of  course,  much  modified  here,  as  in  all 
these  translations. 

Dream,  The  Fruitful ness 

Fat  and  the  Thin,  The  Germinal 

Fortune  of  the  Rougons,  The 

His  Excellency 

The  victorious  career  of  Eugene  Rougon,  the  great  man  of  the 
family,  who  became  prime  minister  under  the  Empire  as  the  result 
of  his  masterly  advocacy  of  the  regime  of  force,  and  later,  of  the 
liberal  movement  inaugurated  by  Napoleon  III.  ;  he  is  obviously 
a  study  of  Eugene  Rouher.  This  powerful  and  complex  character 
is  likewise  a  product  of  hereditary  tendencies,  and  these  explain 
his  nature  and  his  life. 

His  Masterpiece 

Honour  of  the  Army,  &c..  The 

Joy  of  Life,  The 

Lourdes 

Love  Episode,  A  (Une  Page  d'Amour) 

A  pathetic  story  of  Parisian  life,  though  essentially  naturalistic 
in  its  treatment  of  the  growth  of  love.  A  widow,  living  with  her 
consumptive  daughter,  in  whom  the  constitutional  weakness  of 
the  family  reappears,  enters  into  a  passionate  liaison  with  a 
doctor,  and  the  poor  little  girl  manages  to  kill  herself  with 
jealousy.  The  drab  realism  of  such  scenes  as  the  child's  funeral 
intensifies  the  pathos,  while,  seen  through  a  window  from  hour 
to  hour,  the  vast  ocean  of  Paris,  moody  and  changeful,  throws  a 
poetic  light  over  the  story. 

Money  Rome 

Monomaniac,  The  Therese  Raquin 

Paris 

Truth 

This  is  M.  Zola's  last  novel.  Its  main  incidents  are  based  on, 
though  not  identical  with,  the  Dreyfus  case.  It  is  the  story  of  a 
horrible  crime,  which,  being  falsely  imputed  to  a  Jewish  school- 
master, rouses  a  violent  Anti-Semite  outbreak.  The  story  deals 
with  the  French  religious,  political  and  educational  atmosphere, 
and  exposes  the  incivism  of  the  Romish  Church.
